2.blurb: Auburn's basketball program dates to 1906, and the 'Tigers' nickname has been associated with Auburn athletics since the university's earliest days — inspired by a line from Oliver Goldsmith's poem 'The Deserted Village' referencing a 'bold peasantry.' While historically a football school, Auburn's basketball program has experienced a dramatic rise under Bruce Pearl, who brought energy, sellout crowds, and a Final Four appearance in 2019. Charles Barkley, one of the NBA's most iconic personalities, starred at Auburn from 1981-1984 before being drafted fifth overall.
2.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' nickname has been used since the 1890s, inspired partly by a line from Oliver Goldsmith's poem 'The Deserted Village'; the live eagle mascot War Eagle is a separate tradition from the Tigers name
2.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2019 Final Four under Bruce Pearl, the program's first Final Four appearance in over 30 years, energizing the fan base
2.funFact: Charles Barkley was listed as 6-foot-6 at Auburn but later admitted he was closer to 6-foot-4 — he went on to become one of basketball's greatest and most entertaining personalities

5.blurb: UAB's basketball program was founded in 1978, and the Birmingham-based school quickly became one of the most successful programs in Conference USA. Gene Bartow, who had previously coached UCLA, built UAB into a consistent NCAA Tournament team in the 1980s and 1990s.
5.mascotOrigin: The Blazers name was chosen in a student contest in 1978, referencing Birmingham's history as an iron and steel city — the city's blast furnaces "blazed" through the night.
5.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 1 seed Iowa State in the 2015 NCAA Tournament as a 14-seed, capped by a Robert Brown three-pointer, just weeks after the university had threatened to shut down the program.
5.funFact: Gene Bartow left UCLA — the most storied program in college basketball — to build UAB from scratch, and he turned the Blazers into a perennial NCAA Tournament team.

6.blurb: South Alabama's basketball program began in 1968, and the Mobile-based school has been a Sun Belt Conference staple. The Jaguars enjoyed their best era in the 1970s and 1980s under coach Cliff Ellis, making multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
6.mascotOrigin: The Jaguars name was selected by the student body in 1965, chosen for the jaguar's combination of speed, power, and agility.
6.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1980 NCAA Tournament Sweet Sixteen, the deepest tournament run in program history, under coach Cliff Ellis.
6.funFact: South Alabama's Mitchell Center is one of the largest arenas in the Sun Belt Conference, and the school has produced several NBA players including Terry Catledge.

8.blurb: Arkansas fielded its first basketball team in 1924, a relatively late start, and the 'Razorbacks' name was born in 1909 when coach Hugo Bezdek said his team played 'like a wild band of razorback hogs.' The program reached its peak under Nolan Richardson, whose '40 Minutes of Hell' pressure defense delivered Arkansas its only NCAA Championship in 1994, defeating Duke 76-72. Richardson is the only coach in history to win a Division I title, an NIT title, and a junior college championship. The famous 'Hog Call' — Woo Pig Sooie — is the only cheer of its kind in college sports.
8.mascotOrigin: Coach Hugo Bezdek said his team played 'like a wild band of razorback hogs' after a 16-0 win over LSU in 1909; students voted to change the mascot from Cardinals to Razorbacks in 1910
8.iconicMoment: Winning the 1994 NCAA Championship under Nolan Richardson's '40 Minutes of Hell' — Scotty Thurman's key three-pointer sealed the 76-72 win over Duke
8.funFact: Nolan Richardson is the only coach in basketball history to win the NCAA Championship, the NIT Championship, and a Junior College National Championship

9.blurb: Arizona State's basketball program dates to 1909, and the Sun Devils have been a Pac-10/Pac-12/Big 12 member for decades. The program reached its peak under Ned Wulk in the 1960s-80s, making multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. ASU has produced NBA talents like James Harden, Byron Scott, and Fat Lever.
9.mascotOrigin: The Sun Devils name was adopted in 1946, replacing 'Bulldogs,' inspired by the intense desert heat of Tempe, Arizona — students felt 'Sun Devils' better captured the fiery environment.
9.iconicMoment: James Harden's dominant 2008-09 season, averaging 20.1 points per game and leading the Sun Devils to the NCAA Tournament as a sophomore before going third overall in the NBA Draft.
9.funFact: Desert Financial Arena (formerly Wells Fargo Arena) in Tempe is nicknamed 'The Madhouse on McAllister' by fans, and the student section is called the 'Curtain of Distraction,' featuring bizarre antics to distract opposing free throw shooters.

12.blurb: Arizona's basketball program dates to the early 1900s, and the 'Wildcats' name was born in 1914 after a columnist wrote that the team 'showed the fight of wildcats.' The program became a national powerhouse under Lute Olson, who arrived in 1983 and built a dynasty in the desert. Arizona won its lone national championship in 1997, upsetting three No. 1 seeds en route to the title. McKale Center's court is named 'Lute and Bobbi Olson Court' in honor of the beloved coach and his late wife.
12.mascotOrigin: A 1914 Los Angeles Times columnist wrote Arizona 'showed the fight of wildcats' after a tough football game against Occidental College
12.iconicMoment: Winning the 1997 NCAA Championship, upsetting three No. 1 seeds including Kansas, North Carolina, and Kentucky
12.funFact: Arizona's original live mascot was a bobcat named Rufus Arizona, purchased for $9.41 in 1915, who sadly died a year later when he fell from a tree

13.blurb: Cal Poly's basketball program began in 1938 at the polytechnic university in San Luis Obispo, California. The Mustangs compete in the Big West Conference and have had occasional conference success. The program is known for its academic rigor and beautiful Central Coast setting.
13.mascotOrigin: The Mustangs nickname reflects Cal Poly's agricultural roots and the wild mustang horses associated with California's Central Coast ranching heritage.
13.iconicMoment: Winning the 2014 Big West Conference Tournament to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament appearance at the Division I level.
13.funFact: Cal Poly's 'Learn by Doing' motto extends to athletics — the university is one of the most academically rigorous Division I schools on the West Coast, with an engineering program that rivals many major research universities.

16.blurb: Sacramento State's basketball program dates to 1953, and the Hornets compete in the Big Sky Conference. The program has been a mid-major competitor in Northern California, occasionally challenging for conference titles.
16.mascotOrigin: The Hornets nickname dates to the school's founding era, chosen to represent the aggressive and industrious nature of the hornet.
16.iconicMoment: Winning the 2022 Big Sky Conference Tournament title to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament bid at the Division I level.
16.funFact: Sacramento is known as the "City of Trees," and Sacramento State's campus is one of the most tree-covered in the CSU system, giving the Nest (their arena) a unique woodland setting.

21.blurb: San Diego State's basketball program dates to 1921, and the Aztecs have become one of the premier mid-major programs in the country. Under Steve Fisher and then Brian Dutcher, SDSU became a perennial NCAA Tournament team known for suffocating defense.
21.mascotOrigin: The Aztecs name was adopted in 1925, chosen to honor the ancient Aztec civilization and reflect the region's connection to Mesoamerican heritage.
21.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2023 NCAA Championship game as a 5-seed, the program's first-ever title game appearance, behind Lamont Butler's buzzer-beating semifinal winner against FAU.
21.funFact: Viejas Arena, the Aztecs' home court, is known as one of the toughest places to play in college basketball — SDSU posted a remarkable home winning percentage under Steve Fisher and Brian Dutcher.

23.blurb: San Jose State's basketball program dates to 1912 in the heart of Silicon Valley. The Spartans compete in the Mountain West Conference and historically have been more renowned for their football (with Bill Walsh connections) than basketball, though the program has had competitive stretches.
23.mascotOrigin: The Spartans name was adopted in 1924 after students voted to replace the previous "Normalites" nickname, selecting Spartans for the warriors' courage and toughness.
23.iconicMoment: Winning the 1996 Big West Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, one of the program's rare March Madness appearances.
23.funFact: San Jose State is located in downtown San Jose, making it one of the few Division I basketball programs situated in the middle of a major city's urban core — right in the heart of Silicon Valley.

24.blurb: Stanford's men's basketball program dates to 1914, and the Cardinal have combined academic prestige with athletic success on the hardwood. The program enjoyed its best modern era under Mike Montgomery in the 1990s and 2000s, reaching the Final Four in 1998.
24.mascotOrigin: Stanford's "Cardinal" refers to the color cardinal red, not the bird — the singular form is intentional, adopted officially in 1981 after years of mascot debate.
24.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1998 Final Four behind stars Arthur Lee and Mark Madsen, Stanford's first Final Four appearance since 1942.
24.funFact: Stanford's unofficial mascot is the Stanford Tree, a member of the band who designs a new tree costume each year — it has been called the most bizarre mascot in college sports.

25.blurb: Cal's basketball program dates to 1907 at the flagship University of California in Berkeley. The Golden Bears won the NCAA Championship in 1959 under coach Pete Newell and have competed in the Pac-10/Pac-12/ACC. The program produced NBA stars like Jason Kidd, Kevin Johnson, and Shareef Abdur-Rahim.
25.mascotOrigin: The Golden Bears nickname was adopted in 1895, inspired by the California grizzly bear featured on the state flag, which was once native to the region before going extinct in the 1920s.
25.iconicMoment: Winning the 1959 NCAA Championship under Pete Newell, defeating West Virginia 71-70 in a game considered one of the finest displays of defensive basketball.
25.funFact: Haas Pavilion sits at the base of the Berkeley Hills with a view of the San Francisco Bay, and the program's 1959 title team was the last Pac-8/10/12 school to win a championship until UCLA in 1964.

26.blurb: UCLA basketball began in 1919, and the school was originally known as the 'Cubs' before adopting 'Bruins' in 1926. John Wooden, the 'Wizard of Westwood,' built the greatest dynasty in college sports history, winning ten NCAA championships in twelve years including seven consecutive titles from 1967 to 1973. Before Wooden arrived in 1948, UCLA had never won a conference title and once lost 39 straight games to rival USC. The program produced legends like Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and Bill Walton and holds the record of 88 consecutive victories.
26.mascotOrigin: Originally called the 'Cubs' as the younger sibling of UC Berkeley, UCLA adopted 'Bruins' in 1926 when Berkeley gifted them the name
26.iconicMoment: The John Wooden dynasty — winning ten NCAA Championships in twelve years (1964-1975), including an unprecedented seven straight titles
26.funFact: UCLA's first live mascot was a stray dog named Rags found on campus by a gardener, and when Wooden arrived, he found lopsided basketballs and a gym smaller than his hometown high school's

27.blurb: UC Riverside's basketball program began in 1954 and moved to Division I in 2001. The Highlanders compete in the Big West Conference and have steadily built competitiveness since their D-I transition.
27.mascotOrigin: The Highlanders name reflects the campus's location at the base of the Box Springs Mountains in the Inland Empire, referencing the Scottish highland warriors.
27.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first-ever Division I postseason berth in the 2011 CollegeInsider.com Tournament.
27.funFact: UC Riverside's mascot Scotty the Bear is a unique combination — a Scottish highland bear wearing a kilt, blending the Highlander identity with a bear, the symbol of California.

28.blurb: UC San Diego's basketball program began in 1964, and the Tritons made the historic transition from Division II to Division I in 2020. UCSD was one of the most successful D-II programs in the nation before joining the Big West Conference.
28.mascotOrigin: The Tritons name was chosen in reference to Triton, the Greek god of the sea, fitting for a campus perched on the cliffs above the Pacific Ocean in La Jolla.
28.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to Division I in 2020, joining the Big West Conference and opening a new chapter for the oceanside university.
28.funFact: UC San Diego is consistently ranked among the top public universities in the nation and is one of the newest Division I basketball programs, having spent decades as a D-II powerhouse.

30.blurb: USC's basketball program dates to 1907, and while the Trojans are better known for football, they have a rich basketball history. USC has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and produced NBA stars like DeMar DeRozan, O.J. Mayo, and Nick Young.
30.mascotOrigin: The Trojans name was adopted in 1912, chosen by LA Times reporter Owen Bird who compared USC athletes to the warriors of ancient Troy for their fighting spirit against larger opponents.
30.iconicMoment: Reaching the Elite Eight in 2021 behind Evan Mobley, who dominated as a freshman before being drafted third overall in the NBA Draft.
30.funFact: USC's Galen Center sits in the heart of downtown Los Angeles, making it one of the most urban-set arenas in college basketball — and its proximity to Hollywood has brought countless celebrity fans courtside.

36.blurb: Colorado State's basketball program dates to 1897 in Fort Collins and the Rams compete in the Mountain West Conference. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and was especially competitive in the 2010s-2020s. CSU has produced several NBA players.
36.mascotOrigin: The Rams nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, inspired by the bighorn sheep native to the Rocky Mountains surrounding Fort Collins.
36.iconicMoment: The 2013 NCAA Tournament run under coach Larry Eustachy, where the Rams earned a tournament bid after an impressive Mountain West season.
36.funFact: Colorado State's Moby Arena, opened in 1966, was nicknamed 'Moby' both for its whale-like shape and as a pun on the arena's original name — its loud atmosphere has made it one of the toughest road games in the Mountain West.

38.blurb: Colorado's basketball program dates to 1901 in Boulder and the Buffaloes now compete in the Big 12 (formerly Pac-12). The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and had its greatest success under coach Tad Boyle in the 2010s-2020s. CU has produced NBA players like Chauncey Billups and Cliff Meely.
38.mascotOrigin: The Buffaloes nickname was adopted in 1934 after a campus newspaper contest, referencing the American bison that once roamed Colorado's plains in massive herds.
38.iconicMoment: Chauncey Billups' stellar 1996-97 season, averaging 17.1 points as a sophomore before becoming the No. 3 overall NBA pick and eventual NBA Finals MVP.
38.funFact: Colorado's live mascot, Ralphie the Buffalo, is one of the most famous in college sports — a 1,200-pound bison that charges across the football field, though she hasn't visited the basketball court.

41.blurb: Connecticut played its first organized basketball game on February 8, 1901, a 17-12 win against Willimantic High School. The 'Huskies' name was adopted in 1934 via student poll, possibly a play on the Yukon/UConn homophone. UConn has won six NCAA championships — more than any school since the tournament expanded to 64 teams — and is the only university whose men's and women's basketball teams have both won national titles in the same year. Under Dan Hurley, UConn won back-to-back championships in 2023 and 2024, going undefeated in tournament play by double digits both years.
41.mascotOrigin: Adopted in 1934 via student poll after the school's name changed from Connecticut Agricultural College; the Yukon/UConn homophone may have influenced the choice
41.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 2023 and 2024 under Dan Hurley, with all 12 tournament wins coming by double digits
41.funFact: UConn is 6-0 in NCAA Championship games — a perfect record in title contests — and is the only school where both men's and women's basketball teams won national titles in the same year

43.blurb: Yale's basketball program is one of the oldest in the country, dating to 1896. The Bulldogs compete in the Ivy League and were actually an early powerhouse — Yale won the Helms Athletic Foundation national championship in 1909.
43.mascotOrigin: Yale's Bulldog mascot, "Handsome Dan," was introduced in 1889 and is considered the first live college mascot in America. The current Handsome Dan continues a lineage of English bulldogs.
43.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 5 seed Baylor 79-75 in the 2016 NCAA Tournament as a 12-seed, the program's first NCAA Tournament win since 1962.
43.funFact: Yale's Handsome Dan, first introduced in 1889, is widely recognized as the first live animal mascot in American college athletics.

44.blurb: American University's basketball program began in 1925 in Washington, D.C. The Eagles compete in the Patriot League and have won multiple conference championships. The program has enjoyed NCAA Tournament appearances in recent decades.
44.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was adopted as a patriotic symbol befitting a university named 'American' and located in the nation's capital.
44.iconicMoment: Winning the 2014 Patriot League Tournament to earn the program's second NCAA Tournament appearance, led by a dominant regular season.
44.funFact: American University's Bender Arena is located just miles from the White House, making it one of the closest Division I basketball venues to the seat of American government.

45.blurb: George Washington's basketball program dates to 1906 in Washington, D.C. The Revolutionaries (formerly Colonials) compete in the Atlantic 10 and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has been a competitive force in D.C.-area basketball.
45.mascotOrigin: The Revolutionaries nickname was adopted in 2023, replacing 'Colonials,' to better reflect George Washington's role in the American Revolution and align with the university's namesake.
45.iconicMoment: The 1993 Sweet Sixteen run under coach Mike Jarvis, the deepest NCAA Tournament run in program history.
45.funFact: GW's Charles E. Smith Center is located in Foggy Bottom, just blocks from the White House, the State Department, and the Kennedy Center — making it the most politically connected basketball venue in the country.

46.blurb: Georgetown played its first basketball game on February 9, 1907, defeating Virginia 22-11. The 'Hoya' name derives from the college cheer 'Hoya Saxa,' a mix of Greek and Latin roughly meaning 'what rocks' — a nod to the school's stone walls. John Thompson made history in 1984 as the first African-American coach to win an NCAA Championship, led by the dominant Patrick Ewing. Georgetown's basketball alumni include four Naismith Hall of Famers: Ewing, Alonzo Mourning, Dikembe Mutombo, and Allen Iverson.
46.mascotOrigin: From the cheer 'Hoya Saxa' — Greek 'hoia' (what) and Latin 'saxa' (rocks), meaning 'what rocks,' referencing the school's stone walls; the bulldog mascot is named Jack
46.iconicMoment: John Thompson becoming the first African-American coach to win the NCAA Championship in 1984, led by Patrick Ewing's dominant performance
46.funFact: Georgetown's bulldog mascot was supposed to be named 'Hoya,' but the dog only responded to 'Jack' — so every Georgetown bulldog mascot since has been named Jack

47.blurb: Howard University, a historically Black university founded in 1867 in Washington, D.C., has fielded basketball teams since the 1920s. The 'Bison' mascot reflects strength and resilience, fitting for a school named after Civil War general Oliver O. Howard. Howard competes in the MEAC and earned a spot in the 2026 NCAA Tournament, representing HBCU basketball on the national stage. The university has produced more Black PhD recipients than any institution in America, extending its legacy of excellence beyond athletics.
47.mascotOrigin: The 'Bison' mascot was chosen to represent strength, resilience, and the American spirit, fitting for a school named after Civil War general Oliver O. Howard
47.iconicMoment: Earning a spot in the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 16-seed, representing HBCU basketball on the national stage
47.funFact: Howard University has produced more Black PhD recipients than any university in America, and its athletic programs have been a cornerstone of HBCU sports tradition

48.blurb: Delaware's basketball program started in 1908 and the Blue Hens compete in the CAA. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and has been a competitive force in mid-major basketball. Delaware has produced several professional players over the decades.
48.mascotOrigin: The Blue Hens nickname dates to the Revolutionary War, when a Delaware regiment brought Blue Hen chickens to cockfights between battles — the fighting blue hen became the state bird in 1939.
48.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2014 NCAA Tournament and giving Michigan State a competitive game in the second round, the program's most prominent national appearance.
48.funFact: Delaware's mascot, YoUDee, has won the UCA National Mascot Championship multiple times, making it one of the most decorated mascots in all of college sports.

50.blurb: Florida A&M's basketball program began in 1924 at the HBCU in Tallahassee, Florida. The Rattlers compete in the SWAC and have a proud athletic tradition. The program has won multiple conference championships and represented the MEAC/SWAC in the NCAA Tournament.
50.mascotOrigin: The Rattlers nickname references the eastern diamondback rattlesnake native to Florida, chosen to represent a dangerous and fearless competitor.
50.iconicMoment: Winning the MEAC Tournament championship to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, giving the Rattlers their biggest national basketball stage.
50.funFact: FAMU's Marching 100 band is considered one of the greatest college marching bands in history and often provides an electric atmosphere at basketball games as well.

52.blurb: Florida State's basketball program began in 1947 in Tallahassee and the Seminoles compete in the ACC. The program reached its peak under Leonard Hamilton, who built FSU into a consistent NCAA Tournament team and reached the Elite Eight multiple times. The Seminoles made the 1972 national championship game under Hugh Durham.
52.mascotOrigin: The Seminoles nickname honors the Seminole Tribe of Florida, with whom the university maintains a formal, ongoing relationship — the tribe has endorsed the use of its name and imagery.
52.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1972 NCAA Championship game behind Ron King, the program's closest brush with a national title.
52.funFact: Florida State's tradition of Chief Osceola riding an Appaloosa horse named Renegade and planting a flaming spear at midfield is one of the most iconic traditions in college sports, and the Seminole Tribe of Florida has formally approved it.

55.blurb: Jacksonville State University in Alabama has competed in several conferences and currently plays in Conference USA. The Gamecocks have built a solid mid-major program with multiple conference championships and NCAA Tournament appearances from the Ohio Valley Conference.
55.mascotOrigin: The Gamecocks nickname was adopted in the 1940s, inspired by the fighting spirit of the gamecock, a symbol of toughness and tenacity.
55.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first NCAA Tournament victory in 2023, upsetting a heavily favored opponent and putting Jacksonville State on the national stage.
55.funFact: Jacksonville State moved from the OVC to the ASUN and then to Conference USA in consecutive years (2021-2023), one of the most rapid conference-hopping stretches in college sports.

56.blurb: Stetson's basketball program dates to 1930, and the DeLand, Florida school competes in the ASUN Conference. The Hatters have been a consistent presence in the conference, with occasional bursts of strong play.
56.mascotOrigin: The Hatters name comes from the university's namesake, John B. Stetson, the famous hat manufacturer whose family helped fund the school.
56.iconicMoment: Winning the 2024 ASUN Conference Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament bid since 1970, ending a 54-year drought.
56.funFact: Stetson is named after John B. Stetson, creator of the iconic Stetson cowboy hat, making "Hatters" one of the most literally accurate nicknames in college sports.

57.blurb: Florida's basketball program started in 1915 but spent decades in football's shadow before Billy Donovan transformed it into a national power after arriving in 1996. The 'Gators' nickname was chosen in 1911 as an homage to the alligators native to the Everglades region. Under Donovan, Florida became the first team to repeat as national champions with the same starting lineup, winning back-to-back titles in 2006 and 2007. The Gators added a third title in 2025, and remain the only school to have won both football and basketball national championships in the same year (2007).
57.mascotOrigin: Chosen in 1911 as a tribute to the alligators native to Florida's Everglades region
57.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 2006 and 2007 with the same starting five — the first team to accomplish this since Duke in 1991-92
57.funFact: Florida is the only school to win both football and basketball national championships in the same calendar year, accomplishing the feat in 2007

58.blurb: USF's basketball program launched in 1959, just three years after the university opened in 1956. The 'Bulls' name was chosen to represent strength and the cattle ranching heritage of Central Florida. The program's history includes stretches of strong play in the American Athletic Conference and Conference USA, with several postseason appearances. Under current coach Amir Abdur-Rahim, USF earned its first NCAA Tournament at-large bid in decades for the 2024 tournament, and was selected as an 11-seed in the 2026 tournament, marking a new era for Bulls basketball.
58.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulls' nickname was adopted to represent strength and Florida's cattle ranching heritage; the mascot Rocky the Bull has been a fixture since the school's early years
58.iconicMoment: Earning a 2024 NCAA Tournament bid under Amir Abdur-Rahim, the program's first at-large selection in the modern era
58.funFact: USF is one of the youngest major basketball programs in the country, having been founded just three years after the university itself opened in 1956

59.blurb: Georgia Tech's basketball program dates to 1906 in Atlanta and the Yellow Jackets compete in the ACC. The program reached the 2004 national championship game under coach Paul Hewitt and has produced NBA stars like Chris Bosh, Stephon Marbury, and Kenny Anderson. Tech has been a competitive ACC program.
59.mascotOrigin: The Yellow Jackets nickname was adopted around 1905 when fans noticed supporters wearing yellow jackets to games — the wasp imagery was later formally adopted.
59.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2004 NCAA Championship game behind Jarrett Jack, Will Bynum, and Luke Schenscher, the program's closest brush with a national title.
59.funFact: Georgia Tech's mascot Buzz is a yellow jacket (wasp), and the 1990 team featuring Kenny Anderson and Dennis Scott, known as 'Lethal Weapon 3,' is considered one of the most entertaining college basketball teams ever.

61.blurb: Georgia's basketball program dates to the early 1900s, and the 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in 1920 due to the school's historical ties to Yale, whose mascot is also a Bulldog — Georgia's first president was a Yale graduate. The live English Bulldog mascot Uga has been provided by the Seiler family since 1956, when a law student brought his wedding-gift puppy to a football game. Anthony Edwards became the first Georgia basketball player selected first overall in the NBA Draft in 2020. Georgia is the only major college that buries its mascots inside the stadium.
61.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in 1920, partly due to ties to Yale (whose first president was a Yale grad); the live Uga mascot has been owned by the Seiler family since 1956
61.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1983 Final Four, the deepest NCAA Tournament run in program history
61.funFact: Georgia is the only major college that buries its mascots inside the stadium — every Uga has a bronze epitaph at Sanford Stadium, and flowers are placed on their graves before each home game

62.blurb: Hawai'i's basketball program began in 1920 and the Rainbow Warriors compete in the Big West Conference. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and reached the Sweet Sixteen. The team's remote location makes it one of the most unique road trips in college basketball.
62.mascotOrigin: The Rainbow Warriors nickname comes from the rainbows frequently visible over Manoa Valley where the campus is located, combined with warrior imagery from Hawaiian culture.
62.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2016 NCAA Tournament as a Big West champion and earning a competitive first-round game, one of the program's most exciting March moments.
62.funFact: Hawai'i's geographic isolation means the team travels more miles than almost any other D-I program — road trips to the mainland routinely exceed 5,000 miles round trip, and opponents face severe jet lag visiting Honolulu.

66.blurb: Iowa State's basketball program dates to 1907, and the Cyclones play in one of the most feared home courts in America — Hilton Coliseum. The school uses a cardinal named Cy as its mascot since you cannot dress up as a cyclone, though the athletic nickname references the powerful storms of the Great Plains. The term 'Hilton Magic' was coined in 1989 after Iowa State stunned third-ranked Missouri, and the atmosphere has been legendary ever since. Johnny Orr transformed the program in the 1980s, establishing the tradition of passionate basketball that continues under current coaches.
66.mascotOrigin: The 'Cyclones' name references the powerful storms of the Great Plains; the mascot Cy is a cardinal since the school colors (cardinal and gold) were adopted in 1899
66.iconicMoment: The birth of 'Hilton Magic' — coined by a Des Moines Register writer after Iowa State's upset of No. 3 Missouri on February 14, 1989
66.funFact: Hilton Coliseum was specifically built to hold in sound with solid concrete walls and steel doors, and opposing players have said the floor literally shakes from the crowd noise

68.blurb: Boise State's basketball program began in 1933 and the Broncos now compete in the Mountain West Conference. While better known for football, Boise State has built a competitive basketball program with several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has produced NBA players including Chandler Hutchison.
68.mascotOrigin: The Broncos nickname was adopted in 1932, reflecting Idaho's cowboy culture and the wild horses (broncos) that roamed the region's open ranges.
68.iconicMoment: Earning a 2015 NCAA Tournament bid after winning the Mountain West Conference Tournament, one of the program's most exciting March runs.
68.funFact: While Boise State's football team is famous for the blue turf, the basketball team plays in ExtraMile Arena, which features a court with orange-and-blue accents matching the school's bold branding.

70.blurb: Idaho's basketball program dates to 1904 in Moscow, Idaho. The Vandals compete in the Big Sky Conference and were previously in the WAC and Big West. The program has made occasional NCAA Tournament appearances and produced NBA player Dan O'Sullivan.
70.mascotOrigin: The Vandals nickname was adopted in the 1920s, inspired by the ancient Germanic tribe known for their fierce, fearless warrior culture.
70.iconicMoment: The 1982 NCAA Tournament appearance under coach Don Monson, when the Vandals won the Big Sky and competed in March Madness.
70.funFact: Moscow, Idaho sits on the Palouse — rolling wheat-covered hills — and is just eight miles from Washington State, creating one of the smallest and most remote college towns with a Division I basketball program.

71.blurb: Bradley's basketball program began in 1902 in Peoria, Illinois, and the Braves compete in the Missouri Valley Conference. The program enjoyed its golden era in the 1950s, reaching back-to-back NCAA Championship games in 1950 and 1954. Bradley has remained a competitive MVC program throughout its history.
71.mascotOrigin: The Braves nickname was adopted in honor of Lydia Moss Bradley, the university's founder, with the warrior imagery representing her courageous spirit in establishing the school.
71.iconicMoment: Reaching back-to-back NCAA Championship games in 1950 and 1954, with the 1950 team losing a heartbreaker to CCNY in the final.
71.funFact: Bradley's Carver Arena in downtown Peoria seats over 11,000, making it one of the largest home venues in the Missouri Valley Conference relative to the school's enrollment.

77.blurb: Northwestern University in Evanston, Illinois, competes in the Big Ten and was historically one of the most struggling programs in a power conference. That changed dramatically in 2017 when Chris Collins led the Wildcats to their first-ever NCAA Tournament appearance, ending the longest drought in Division I history.
77.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats nickname was bestowed by a Chicago sportswriter in 1924 who wrote that the players "fought like wildcats" during a tough game against Chicago.
77.iconicMoment: The 2017 NCAA Tournament — Northwestern's first-ever appearance — including a first-round victory over Vanderbilt, ending the longest NCAA Tournament drought in Division I history.
77.funFact: Northwestern went 78 years without an NCAA Tournament appearance (1939-2017), the longest drought in Division I history, making their 2017 breakthrough one of college basketball's most celebrated moments.

79.blurb: Southern Illinois' basketball program dates to 1913, and the Salukis have been one of the Missouri Valley Conference's strongest programs. The Salukis reached national prominence in the 2000s under coach Chris Lowery and Bruce Weber, making deep NCAA Tournament runs.
79.mascotOrigin: The Salukis name was adopted in 1951, replacing "Maroons." The Saluki is an ancient Egyptian hunting dog breed, chosen because southern Illinois is known as "Little Egypt."
79.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2007 Sweet Sixteen as an 11-seed, upsetting Virginia Tech and Holy Cross before falling to Kansas, the deepest tournament run in program history at that time.
79.funFact: The region of southern Illinois is nicknamed "Little Egypt" — a reference dating to the 1800s — which inspired the choice of the Saluki, an Egyptian dog breed, as the school's mascot.

82.blurb: UIC's basketball program was established in 1946, and the Chicago-based school competes in the Missouri Valley Conference. The Flames have been a competitive mid-major in the Chicago area, with several conference titles and NCAA Tournament appearances.
82.mascotOrigin: The Flames name was adopted in 1982, referencing the Great Chicago Fire of 1871, symbolizing the city's resilience and rebirth from the ashes.
82.iconicMoment: Winning the 1998 Horizon League Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, where the Flames nearly upset Arkansas as a 14-seed.
82.funFact: UIC's campus is in the heart of Chicago's Near West Side, making it one of the most urban Division I basketball programs in the country — students can take the L train to games.

84.blurb: Indiana's basketball program has competed since 1901 and holds a special place in the sport's history with five NCAA championships. The 1976 team, coached by Bob Knight, went 32-0 and remains the last team to finish a season undefeated and win the national title. Knight's fiery coaching style produced three championship teams and 661 wins at Indiana. The Hoosiers are known for their distinctive candy-striped warm-up pants and are one of the few programs that do not put players' names on the back of their jerseys.
84.mascotOrigin: The term 'Hoosier' has been associated with Indiana residents since the 1840s, though its exact origin remains debated among historians
84.iconicMoment: The 1976 undefeated season (32-0) under Bob Knight — the last team in men's Division I basketball to go wire-to-wire without a loss and win the national title
84.funFact: Indiana's distinctive candy-striped warm-up pants have been a tradition for decades, and the team is one of the few that refuses to put players' names on game jerseys

85.blurb: IU Indianapolis (formerly IUPUI) joined Division I in 1998 and competes in the Horizon League. The program rebranded from IUPUI Jaguars to IU Indianapolis Jaguars in 2024 as part of a university identity consolidation. The Jaguars have been building toward consistent competitiveness in their conference.
85.mascotOrigin: The Jaguar mascot was chosen in 1979 to represent speed, agility, and power, giving the urban commuter campus a distinctive athletic identity.
85.iconicMoment: George Hill starring for IUPUI before transferring to Indiana, going on to become an NBA first-round pick and eventual NBA champion with the Cleveland Cavaliers.
85.funFact: IUPUI was one of the longest acronyms in Division I athletics, with fans and broadcasters often struggling to pronounce the five-letter abbreviation.

87.blurb: Notre Dame's basketball program dates to 1897 and the 'Fighting Irish' nickname — whose exact origin is debated — became the school's official moniker in the 1920s. While Notre Dame is best known for football, the basketball program has a proud history including an upset of UCLA that ended the Bruins' record 88-game winning streak in 1974. Under Mike Brey, Notre Dame became a consistent ACC contender with multiple Sweet Sixteen appearances. The program plays at the Purcell Pavilion at the Edmund P. Joyce Center.
87.mascotOrigin: The exact origin of 'Fighting Irish' is debated — theories include references to the Irish Brigade in the Civil War and the school's Irish Catholic heritage
87.iconicMoment: Ending UCLA's NCAA-record 88-game winning streak with a 71-70 victory on January 19, 1974, one of college basketball's most historic upsets
87.funFact: Digger Phelps' Notre Dame team broke UCLA's legendary 88-game winning streak in 1974, and fans famously stormed the court and tore down the backboard

88.blurb: Southern Indiana's basketball program began in 1968, and the Evansville-based school made the historic move from Division II to Division I in 2022. The Screaming Eagles were a D-II powerhouse, winning national championships in 1995 and 2022.
88.mascotOrigin: The Screaming Eagles nickname was chosen by students, inspired by the bald eagle and the 101st Airborne Division (the "Screaming Eagles") based at nearby Fort Campbell.
88.iconicMoment: Winning the 2022 Division II National Championship in the program's final D-II season before transitioning to Division I — a storybook ending.
88.funFact: Southern Indiana won D-II national titles in 1995 and 2022, bookending their Division II era perfectly before moving up to D-I competition in the Ohio Valley Conference.

91.blurb: Bellarmine's basketball program dates to 1918 at the Catholic university in Louisville, Kentucky. The Knights were a Division II powerhouse, winning the 2011 national championship, before transitioning to Division I in 2020. They joined the ASUN Conference and quickly became competitive at the D-I level.
91.mascotOrigin: The Knights nickname honors the medieval tradition of chivalry and reflects the university's Catholic heritage, named after Saint Robert Bellarmine.
91.iconicMoment: Winning the 2011 NCAA Division II national championship, capping a dominant era that established Bellarmine as one of the best small-school programs in the country.
91.funFact: During their D-I transition period, Bellarmine was ineligible for the NCAA Tournament but still managed to defeat multiple tournament-caliber opponents.

93.blurb: Murray State University in western Kentucky competes in the Missouri Valley Conference and has a remarkable history of mid-major basketball success. The Racers have produced NBA stars Ja Morant, Joe Fulks, and Cameron Payne, and have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances.
93.mascotOrigin: The Racers nickname honors the thoroughbred horse racing heritage of Kentucky, with the university located near the western Kentucky horse country.
93.iconicMoment: Ja Morant's explosive 2019 NCAA Tournament performance against Marquette, scoring 17 points with 16 assists and 11 rebounds in a triple-double that launched him to the #2 overall pick.
93.funFact: Joe Fulks, who played at Murray State in the 1940s, is credited as the inventor of the modern jump shot and was the first scoring champion in NBA/BAA history.

94.blurb: Northern Kentucky University in Highland Heights competes in the Horizon League and transitioned to Division I in 2012. The Norse have quickly become competitive, winning Horizon League championships and making NCAA Tournament appearances.
94.mascotOrigin: The Norse mascot references the Viking warriors of Scandinavian legend, chosen for their strength, exploration, and fighting spirit.
94.iconicMoment: The 2017 NCAA Tournament appearance, the program's first after transitioning to Division I, marking a rapid ascent for the Greater Cincinnati-area school.
94.funFact: NKU's BB&T Arena is located just minutes from downtown Cincinnati, giving the Norse access to one of the best basketball recruiting hotbeds in the country.

96.blurb: Kentucky basketball was founded in 1903 and has grown into the sport's most passionate fan base, known as 'Big Blue Nation.' The 'Wildcats' nickname stuck after a 1909 football upset of Illinois. Under Adolph Rupp, the 'Baron of the Bluegrass,' Kentucky won four NCAA championships and 27 SEC titles. The program has won eight national championships overall, second only to UCLA. Rupp Arena, which opened in 1976 as the largest basketball-specific arena in the country, has a .892 all-time home winning percentage.
96.mascotOrigin: The 'Wildcats' nickname became associated with Kentucky after a 6-2 football victory over Illinois on October 9, 1909
96.iconicMoment: The 1996 NCAA Championship team, known as 'The Untouchables,' which brought Kentucky back to glory after years of NCAA sanctions
96.funFact: Kentucky has led the nation in home attendance since 1976 and won the national attendance title a record 25 times; Memorial Coliseum sold out all 345 home games during its 26-year tenure

97.blurb: Louisville's basketball program was founded in 1911 and became a national force under Denny Crum, who coached for 30 seasons and earned the nickname 'Cool Hand Luke' for his sideline composure. The Cardinals won NCAA titles in 1980, led by Darrell Griffith's 'Doctors of Dunk,' and in 1986, when freshman Pervis Ellison — dubbed 'Never Nervous' — led them past Duke. Louisville is the only school in the nation to have claimed championships in three major postseason tournaments: the 1948 NAIB, the 1956 NIT, and the NCAA Tournament.
97.mascotOrigin: The Cardinal Bird has been Louisville's mascot, representing the state bird of Kentucky
97.iconicMoment: The 1986 NCAA Championship, where freshman 'Never Nervous' Pervis Ellison led Louisville to the title over Duke in Denny Crum's second championship
97.funFact: Louisville is the only school to have won the NAIB tournament (1948), the NIT (1956), and the NCAA Championship (1980, 1986) — claiming a triple crown of postseason basketball

98.blurb: Western Kentucky's basketball program dates to 1914 and is one of the winningest in college basketball history. Coach E.A. Diddle led the program for 42 years, and the Hilltoppers have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances across multiple conferences.
98.mascotOrigin: The Hilltoppers name comes from the university's location on a hill overlooking Bowling Green, Kentucky — the campus sits atop "The Hill" and has been called that since the school's founding.
98.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1971 Final Four under coach Jim Richards and again earning a No. 1 seed equivalent in the NIT era, cementing WKU as one of the most historically successful mid-major programs.
98.funFact: Western Kentucky's legendary coach E.A. Diddle won 759 games in 42 seasons and was famous for waving a red towel during games — a tradition WKU fans continue to this day.

99.blurb: LSU has a strong basketball tradition in the SEC, producing legendary players like Shaquille O'Neal, Pete Maravich, and Bob Pettit. Pete "Pistol Pete" Maravich holds the NCAA all-time scoring record with 3,667 career points, a mark achieved without a three-point line or a shot clock. The Tigers have reached the Final Four multiple times and compete in the Pete Maravich Assembly Center.
99.mascotOrigin: The Tigers name dates to 1896, inspired by a Confederate regiment from New Orleans known as the "Louisiana Tigers" during the Civil War.
99.iconicMoment: Pete Maravich's final home game in 1970, where he scored 66 points against Ole Miss and set the all-time NCAA scoring record of 3,667 career points.
99.funFact: Maravich averaged 44.2 points per game for his career at LSU — all without a three-point line — a record that will almost certainly never be broken.

103.blurb: Boston College's basketball program dates to 1904 at the Jesuit university in Chestnut Hill, Massachusetts. The Eagles compete in the ACC and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances, including Elite Eight runs. BC has produced NBA players like Jared Dudley and Reggie Jackson.
103.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was adopted in 1920 after the school's student newspaper, The Heights, ran a contest; the eagle was chosen as a symbol of American strength and the heights of achievement.
103.iconicMoment: Troy Bell leading the Eagles to the Sweet Sixteen in 2001, scoring 29 points against USC in one of the program's most memorable NCAA Tournament performances.
103.funFact: Boston College's Conte Forum is located on a hilltop in Chestnut Hill with a view of the Boston skyline, and the arena doubles as the home for BC's hockey team, another perennial power.

104.blurb: Boston University's basketball program dates to 1902 and the Terriers compete in the Patriot League. BU has won multiple conference championships and made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has produced NBA players including Carlos Clark.
104.mascotOrigin: The Terriers nickname was adopted in 1922, inspired by the Boston Terrier dog breed, which originated in Boston in the late 19th century and is the official dog of Massachusetts.
104.iconicMoment: Upsetting a heavily favored team in the 2011 Patriot League Tournament to earn an NCAA bid, continuing the program's tradition of competitive March basketball.
104.funFact: BU's Case Gym was one of the oldest basketball venues in New England before the Terriers moved to Agganis Arena in 2005, a modern 7,200-seat facility named after legendary BU athlete Harry Agganis.

107.blurb: Holy Cross' basketball program dates to 1900 at the Jesuit college in Worcester, Massachusetts. The Crusaders won the 1947 NCAA Championship with Bob Cousy on the roster and were a powerhouse in the 1940s-50s. The program competes in the Patriot League.
107.mascotOrigin: The Crusaders nickname references the medieval Christian crusades, fitting for a Jesuit college — though the school has had ongoing discussions about the name in modern times.
107.iconicMoment: Winning the 1947 NCAA Championship, led by George Kaftan and featuring freshman Bob Cousy, establishing Holy Cross as one of the original basketball powers.
107.funFact: Bob Cousy, who revolutionized basketball as a Boston Celtic with his flashy passing and became known as 'The Houdini of the Hardwood,' played at Holy Cross and helped win the 1947 title as a freshman.

108.blurb: Harvard's basketball program dates to 1900 at the Ivy League university in Cambridge, Massachusetts. The Crimson have won multiple Ivy League titles and made several NCAA Tournament appearances in the 2010s under coach Tommy Amaker. As an Ivy League school, Harvard does not offer athletic scholarships.
108.mascotOrigin: The Crimson nickname simply refers to Harvard's official color, crimson, which was adopted in 1858 and has been the school's athletic identity ever since.
108.iconicMoment: Defeating third-seeded New Mexico 68-62 in the 2013 NCAA Tournament first round, the program's first NCAA Tournament win and a statement for Ivy League basketball.
108.funFact: Harvard's Lavietes Pavilion is the oldest basketball-only arena in the world, originally opened in 1926 as the Indoor Athletic Building, and has hosted Ivy League games for nearly a century.

111.blurb: Northeastern University in Boston competes in the Coastal Athletic Association and has won multiple conference championships in basketball. The Huskies have made several NCAA Tournament appearances and represent Boston in the mid-major basketball landscape.
111.mascotOrigin: The Huskies mascot was adopted in 1927, chosen for the breed's resilience and toughness in cold climates, fitting for a university in New England.
111.iconicMoment: Reggie Lewis's dominant career at Northeastern in the 1980s, where he became the school's all-time leading scorer before being drafted by the Boston Celtics.
111.funFact: Reggie Lewis starred at Northeastern before becoming a Celtics captain, making him one of the rare players to star in both college and professional basketball in the same city.

113.blurb: UMass basketball reached its zenith under John Calipari in the 1990s, reaching the 1996 Final Four with Marcus Camby. The Minutemen compete in the Atlantic 10 Conference and have a passionate fan base at the Mullins Center in Amherst. The program has also produced NBA player Julius Erving, who played at UMass before becoming "Dr. J."
113.mascotOrigin: The Minutemen name honors the colonial Minutemen of the American Revolution, reflecting Massachusetts' central role in the founding of the United States.
113.iconicMoment: The 1996 Final Four run under John Calipari, led by Marcus Camby, which put UMass basketball on the national map — though the appearance was later vacated by the NCAA.
113.funFact: Julius "Dr. J" Erving played at UMass from 1969-71, averaging over 20 points and 20 rebounds per game before becoming one of the most influential players in basketball history.

116.blurb: Mount St. Mary's University in Emmitsburg, Maryland, competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference and has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. The Mountaineers are one of the smaller Division I programs but have consistently competed for conference titles.
116.mascotOrigin: The Mountaineers name reflects the university's location in the Catoctin Mountains of western Maryland, near the Appalachian Trail.
116.iconicMoment: The 2008 NCAA Tournament Play-In Game victory over Coppin State, followed by a competitive first-round matchup with North Carolina, putting the Mount on the national stage.
116.funFact: Mount St. Mary's is the second-oldest Catholic university in the United States, founded in 1808, giving it a longer history than most of its athletic peers.

119.blurb: Towson's basketball program dates to 1913, and the Baltimore-area school competes in the Colonial Athletic Association. The Tigers have steadily improved in recent decades, becoming a competitive force in the CAA.
119.mascotOrigin: The Tigers nickname has been associated with Towson since the early 1900s, chosen as a traditional symbol of fierceness and athletic prowess.
119.iconicMoment: Winning the 2023 CAA Tournament to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament bid, ending decades of near-misses.
119.funFact: Towson is the second-largest university in the University System of Maryland and is located just north of Baltimore, giving it access to one of the most passionate basketball markets on the East Coast.

120.blurb: Maryland's basketball program dates to 1904 and the 'Terrapins' nickname refers to the diamondback terrapin turtle native to the Chesapeake Bay region. The Terps won their only NCAA Championship in 2002 under Gary Williams, defeating Indiana behind tournament MOP Juan Dixon. Maryland was a founding member of the ACC before moving to the Big Ten in 2014. Legendary coach Lefty Driesell proclaimed he would make Maryland the 'UCLA of the East' and transformed the program into a national contender in the 1970s.
120.mascotOrigin: Named for the diamondback terrapin, a small turtle native to Maryland's Chesapeake Bay region and the official state reptile
120.iconicMoment: Winning the 2002 NCAA Championship under Gary Williams, with Juan Dixon leading the Terps past Indiana for the school's only national title
120.funFact: Lefty Driesell famously declared he would make Maryland the 'UCLA of the East' when he was hired in 1969, and while he didn't win a title, he built the program into a perennial national power

127.blurb: Michigan State's basketball program dates to 1899, and the 'Spartans' name was chosen in honor of the ancient Greek warriors of Sparta renowned for their bravery. The program reached iconic status in 1979 when Earvin 'Magic' Johnson led the Spartans to the NCAA Championship in the most-watched college basketball game ever, defeating Larry Bird's Indiana State Sycamores. Under Tom Izzo, who arrived in 1995, Michigan State has reached ten Final Fours and won a second national title in 2000. ESPN named MSU the best basketball team of the 2000s decade.
127.mascotOrigin: Named after the ancient Greek warriors of Sparta, chosen for the bravery and discipline associated with the Spartan military tradition
127.iconicMoment: Magic Johnson's 24-point performance in the 1979 NCAA Championship game against Larry Bird's Indiana State — the most-watched college basketball game in history
127.funFact: Tom Izzo has led Michigan State to ten Final Fours, tied for sixth most all-time, and ESPN named the Spartans the best team of the 2000s decade

130.blurb: Michigan's basketball program began in 1909 and won its sole NCAA Championship in 1989 when interim coach Steve Fisher guided the team to an overtime title win on Rumeal Robinson's clutch free throws. The program is best known for the 1991 'Fab Five' — Chris Webber, Jalen Rose, Juwan Howard, Ray Jackson, and Jimmy King — the first team to start five freshmen in an NCAA Championship game. Their baggy shorts and black socks revolutionized basketball fashion and culture. The Fab Five reached back-to-back title games in 1992 and 1993, though those appearances were later vacated.
130.mascotOrigin: The Wolverines name was adopted in the 1860s, tied to Michigan's historical association with the wolverine animal dating to territorial disputes with Ohio
130.iconicMoment: The Fab Five starting five freshmen in the 1992 NCAA Championship game, forever changing college basketball culture
130.funFact: The Fab Five's ESPN Films documentary became the highest-rated documentary in ESPN history, and their merchandise sales dwarfed even the 1989 championship team's

135.blurb: Minnesota has a long basketball history in the Big Ten, including two NCAA Tournament title claims in 1919 and 1937 (pre-tournament era). The Golden Gophers reached the Final Four in 1997 under Clem Haskins, though that appearance was later vacated due to academic fraud. The program plays in Williams Arena, one of the most historic venues in college basketball.
135.mascotOrigin: The "Golden Gophers" nickname dates to the 1880s, derived from Minnesota's state nickname as the "Gopher State," a reference to the striped gophers found throughout the region.
135.iconicMoment: The 1997 Final Four run under Clem Haskins, which energized the program and filled Williams Arena to capacity, even though the achievement was later vacated.
135.funFact: Williams Arena, known as "The Barn," opened in 1928 and features a raised floor that sits above the arena's concourse level, creating one of the most unique playing environments in college basketball.

139.blurb: Saint Louis University's basketball program dates to around 1900, making it one of the oldest in the Midwest. The 'Billikens' name — one of college sports' most unusual nicknames — was adopted in 1911 after a law student sculpted a figurine of coach John Bender that resembled a popular 'Billiken' good-luck charm of the era. Saint Louis won the NIT Championship in 1948, then college basketball's most prestigious tournament, under coach Eddie Hickey. As a founding member of the Missouri Valley Conference, SLU has a proud tradition in the Atlantic 10.
139.mascotOrigin: A law student's figurine of coach John Bender resembled a popular 'Billiken' good-luck charm from the era, and the name was adopted in 1911
139.iconicMoment: Winning the 1948 NIT Championship under coach Eddie Hickey, at a time when the NIT was considered college basketball's premier postseason tournament
139.funFact: The Billiken is actually a patented charm doll created by a Kansas City art teacher in 1908 — making Saint Louis one of the few schools whose mascot originated as a mass-produced good-luck figurine

140.blurb: The University of Missouri-Kansas City competes in the Summit League and rebranded from the Kangaroos to the Roos for a more modern identity. The program has been building competitiveness in recent years and plays in the Kansas City metro area, one of the largest media markets in the Summit League.
140.mascotOrigin: The Kangaroos (now Roos) nickname was adopted in 1936 because UMKC's original campus was located on a hilly terrain where students had to "hop" between buildings.
140.iconicMoment: The program's transition to Division I in 1987, marking UMKC's commitment to competing at the highest level of college basketball.
140.funFact: UMKC shares Kansas City with multiple professional sports teams, making it one of the few Division I programs competing directly with pro franchises for local fan attention.

142.blurb: Missouri's basketball program was founded in 1907, and the 'Tigers' nickname dates to the Civil War when a local militia called 'The Missouri Tigers' defended Columbia from guerrilla raiders — who never attacked, as the Tigers' fierce reputation preceded them. Norm Stewart, a Missouri native who played both basketball and baseball for the school, coached the Tigers for 32 seasons and won more games (634) than were won in the 60 years of the program before him. Stewart also founded the Coaches vs. Cancer initiative, which has raised over $150 million.
142.mascotOrigin: Named after a Civil War-era militia that defended Columbia, Missouri from guerrilla raiders; mascot Truman is named after President Harry Truman, the only Missouri-born president
142.iconicMoment: The 1994 team going a perfect 14-0 in Big Eight conference play under Norm Stewart, who was named AP Coach of the Year
142.funFact: Norm Stewart refused to let his team stop for gas, food, or lodging in the state of Kansas during road trips, not wanting any Missouri tax dollars going to the rival Jayhawks' state

145.blurb: Ole Miss competes in the SEC and has had periods of basketball success despite being traditionally overshadowed by football. The Rebels have produced NBA players and made NCAA Tournament appearances, with the program experiencing a resurgence in recent years.
145.mascotOrigin: The "Rebels" nickname was adopted in 1936 through a student contest. The university retired the Colonel Reb mascot in 2003, eventually adopting the Landshark as an unofficial mascot.
145.iconicMoment: The 2001 SEC Tournament Championship under coach Rod Barnes, when Ole Miss upset several higher-ranked SEC opponents to earn an NCAA Tournament bid.
145.funFact: The Pavilion at Ole Miss, which opened in 2016, replaced the beloved Tad Smith Coliseum and gave the Rebels one of the premier basketball facilities in the SEC.

147.blurb: Montana State University in Bozeman competes in the Big Sky Conference and maintains an intense in-state rivalry with Montana. The Bobcats have won multiple Big Sky championships and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
147.mascotOrigin: The Bobcats mascot was adopted to represent the elusive and agile wild bobcats found throughout the mountains and forests of Montana.
147.iconicMoment: The 2022 NCAA Tournament appearance, the program's first since 1996, when Montana State won the Big Sky Tournament and returned to the national stage.
147.funFact: Montana State's Brick Breeden Fieldhouse is one of the most unique arenas in college basketball, with a distinctive barrel-roof design dating to its 1957 construction.

149.blurb: The University of Montana in Missoula competes in the Big Sky Conference and has one of the most passionate fan bases in mid-major basketball. The Grizzlies have won numerous Big Sky titles and made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
149.mascotOrigin: The Grizzlies mascot represents the grizzly bears native to Montana's wilderness, embodying the rugged and powerful spirit of the state.
149.iconicMoment: The 2006 NCAA Tournament appearance and competitive first-round game, a highlight for the Grizzly faithful who packed Dahlberg Arena all season.
149.funFact: Dahlberg Arena in Missoula is one of the loudest mid-major venues in the country, and Montana fans regularly sell out the arena for Big Sky Conference games.

150.blurb: Duke's basketball program began in 1906 when athletic director Wilbur Wade Card introduced the sport to Trinity College, which later became Duke University. The 'Blue Devils' nickname was adopted in 1924, inspired by France's Chasseurs Alpins — elite Alpine soldiers known as 'les Diables Bleus' for their distinctive blue uniforms. Under Mike Krzyzewski, Duke won five national championships and became synonymous with college basketball excellence, producing six first-overall NBA draft picks. Cameron Indoor Stadium, home to the legendary 'Cameron Crazies,' has been one of the toughest environments in college sports since opening in 1940.
150.mascotOrigin: Inspired by France's Chasseurs Alpins, elite Alpine soldiers known as 'les Diables Bleus' during World War I, chosen by student newspaper editors in 1923
150.iconicMoment: Christian Laettner's turnaround buzzer-beater to defeat Kentucky 104-103 in overtime in the 1992 East Regional Final
150.funFact: Duke holds the best NCAA tournament winning percentage (.755) in history and has had six players selected first overall in the NBA Draft, the most of any school

151.blurb: East Carolina's basketball program dates to 1909 in Greenville, North Carolina. The Pirates compete in the AAC and have made multiple NCAA and NIT appearances. The program has been competitive in its various conference affiliations throughout its history.
151.mascotOrigin: The Pirates nickname was adopted in 1934, inspired by the Blackbeard and other pirates who once sailed the waters off North Carolina's Outer Banks coastline.
151.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1993 NCAA Tournament and earning a first-round upset, one of the program's most exciting March Madness moments.
151.funFact: ECU's Williams Arena at Minges Coliseum is located in Greenville, which is just 90 miles from the coast where Blackbeard the pirate met his end in 1718.

152.blurb: NC State began basketball in 1911 and adopted the 'Wolfpack' nickname in 1921. The program has won two NCAA Championships, but none is more famous than 1983 under Jim Valvano. As a No. 6 seed, the 'Cardiac Pack' pulled off improbable last-second wins throughout the tournament, culminating in Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk to stun Houston's 'Phi Slama Jama' 54-52 for the title. Valvano's famous image, running across the court looking for someone to hug, remains one of basketball's most iconic scenes.
152.mascotOrigin: The 'Wolfpack' nickname was adopted in 1921; NC State was one of the first universities to federally trademark its mascot and logos in 1983
152.iconicMoment: Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk off Dereck Whittenburg's airball to beat Houston's Phi Slama Jama 54-52 in the 1983 NCAA Championship
152.funFact: Jim Valvano's famous ESPY speech included the line 'Don't give up, don't ever give up' — the Jimmy V Foundation he inspired has raised over $150 million for cancer research

153.blurb: North Carolina's basketball program began in 1911, and the 'Tar Heels' nickname traces to Civil War-era soldiers who proudly adopted the term rooted in the state's tar and pitch industry. Under Dean Smith, who coached for 36 years with a 96.6% graduation rate, UNC won two national championships and became a model of excellence. The program has won six NCAA titles overall, producing icons like Michael Jordan, whose game-winning shot in the 1982 championship game launched his legendary career. The Dean Smith Center, known as the 'Dean Dome,' stands as a monument to his legacy.
153.mascotOrigin: Traces to North Carolina's history as a leading tar and pitch producer; Civil War soldiers turned the once-derogatory 'tar heel' term into a badge of state pride
153.iconicMoment: Michael Jordan's game-winning jumper as a freshman to beat Georgetown 63-62 in the 1982 NCAA Championship game
153.funFact: When Dean Smith passed away in 2015, his will included a $200 check to every former player with a note reading 'Enjoy a dinner out compliments of Coach Dean Smith'

154.blurb: Wake Forest's basketball program dates to 1906, and the Demon Deacons have been an ACC member since the conference's founding in 1953. The program produced Tim Duncan, one of the greatest players in NBA history, and won the ACC Tournament three times.
154.mascotOrigin: Wake Forest was a Baptist school, and students were known as "Deacons." After a particularly feisty 1923 football game, a newspaper writer called them "Demon Deacons," and the name stuck.
154.iconicMoment: Tim Duncan's four years at Wake Forest (1993-97), culminating in National Player of the Year honors and the No. 1 overall pick in the 1997 NBA Draft.
154.funFact: Tim Duncan stayed all four years at Wake Forest despite being projected as a top pick after his sophomore season — he earned his degree and is still celebrated as the program's greatest player.

155.blurb: The University of North Dakota competes in the Summit League and transitioned to Division I in 2012. The Fighting Hawks have been building their basketball program at the Division I level after decades of success in Division II.
155.mascotOrigin: The Fighting Hawks name was adopted in 2015 after the university retired the controversial "Fighting Sioux" nickname. Students voted to embrace the hawk, a bird of prey common in North Dakota.
155.iconicMoment: The transition to full Division I status and eligibility for the NCAA Tournament, marking a new era for the Grand Forks program.
155.funFact: The debate over retiring the "Fighting Sioux" nickname lasted over a decade and involved the NCAA, the North Dakota legislature, and local tribal nations before "Fighting Hawks" was adopted in 2015.

156.blurb: Creighton, a Jesuit university in Omaha founded in 1878, began basketball in 1907 and adopted the 'Bluejays' name in 1923 via a public contest coordinated by the Omaha Bee newspaper. Doug McDermott, nicknamed 'Dougie McBuckets,' left Creighton as the fifth-leading scorer in NCAA history with 3,150 points — all while playing for his father, coach Greg McDermott. He even relinquished his scholarship as a senior to make room for a teammate, becoming perhaps the greatest walk-on in Big East history. Baseball Hall of Famer Bob Gibson also played basketball at Creighton.
156.mascotOrigin: The 'Bluejays' name was selected from 200 entries in a 1923 public contest because the bird's color matched the school's blue and white colors
156.iconicMoment: Doug McDermott's 2014 Senior Night, scoring a career-high 45 points against Providence and surpassing 3,000 career points
156.funFact: Baseball Hall of Famer Bob Gibson played basketball at Creighton before his legendary pitching career, finishing as the school's third-leading scorer with 1,272 points in 1957

158.blurb: Nebraska's basketball program was founded in 1897, making it one of the oldest in the country, though football has historically dominated the school's identity. Before 1900, teams went by names like 'Bugeaters' and 'Rattlesnake Boys' until Lincoln sportswriter Charles Sherman borrowed the 'Cornhuskers' name from Iowa, whose fans preferred 'Hawkeyes.' The nickname became so popular it eventually became the official name of the entire state. Under Fred Hoiberg and then Matt Abdelmassih, Nebraska has worked to elevate its basketball profile, earning a No. 4 seed in the 2026 NCAA Tournament.
158.mascotOrigin: Sportswriter Charles Sherman coined 'Cornhuskers' around 1900, borrowing it from Iowa (whose fans preferred 'Hawkeyes'); the name stuck and became the state's official nickname too
158.iconicMoment: Danny Nee's 1990s era brought four consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances and made the Devaney Center a feared venue
158.funFact: Nebraska's inflatable mascot Lil' Red, introduced in 1993, was the first inflatable mascot in either college or professional sports history, standing 8 feet 4 inches tall

159.blurb: Dartmouth's basketball program dates to 1901 at the Ivy League school in Hanover, New Hampshire. The Big Green were a national power in the 1940s, reaching the 1944 NCAA Championship game. As an Ivy League school, Dartmouth does not offer athletic scholarships.
159.mascotOrigin: The 'Big Green' nickname refers to Dartmouth's school color, which dates to the 1860s. Dartmouth has no official mascot, though an unofficial 'Keggy the Keg' mascot has appeared at games.
159.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1944 NCAA Championship game, where Dartmouth lost to Utah in what remains the program's finest hour.
159.funFact: The unofficial student-created mascot 'Keggy the Keg' — a person dressed as a beer keg — has appeared at Dartmouth basketball games since 2003, despite having no official university endorsement.

160.blurb: The University of New Hampshire competes in the America East Conference and has a basketball tradition spanning over a century. The Wildcats have been competitive in their conference and occasionally contend for NCAA Tournament bids.
160.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats mascot was adopted to represent the fierce and independent spirit of the wild cats found in New Hampshire's forests, matching the state's "Live Free or Die" motto.
160.iconicMoment: The 2006 America East Tournament Championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, the program's first Big Dance bid in over a decade.
160.funFact: UNH's Lundholm Gymnasium was one of the most intimate and oldest venues in Division I basketball before the school moved to the larger Whittemore Center Arena.

161.blurb: Fairleigh Dickinson's basketball program began in 1957 at the university with campuses in New Jersey. The Knights compete in the NEC and have won multiple conference championships. FDU made NCAA Tournament history in 2023 with one of the most stunning upsets ever.
161.mascotOrigin: The Knights nickname represents medieval warriors of chivalry and honor, fitting for a university that emphasizes integrity and excellence in education.
161.iconicMoment: Becoming just the second 16-seed to beat a 1-seed in NCAA Tournament history, defeating Purdue 63-58 in the 2023 first round — one of the greatest upsets in sports history.
161.funFact: FDU's 2023 upset of top-seeded Purdue made them only the second 16-seed ever to beat a 1-seed, joining UMBC's 2018 upset of Virginia in the most exclusive club in March Madness history.

163.blurb: Princeton competes in the Ivy League and is one of the most storied programs in college basketball history, having won the very first NCAA Tournament game ever played in 1939. The Tigers are known for their methodical Princeton offense, which has been adopted by coaches at all levels of the sport.
163.mascotOrigin: The Tigers mascot was adopted in the 1880s, with Princeton's orange-and-black colors naturally lending themselves to the tiger, which became the university's official symbol.
163.iconicMoment: The 1996 NCAA Tournament upset of defending champion UCLA, 43-41, using the Princeton offense to perfection and pulling off one of the greatest upsets in tournament history.
163.funFact: The "Princeton offense" is a motion-based system featuring backdoor cuts and ball movement that has been widely adopted across basketball, making it one of the few offensive systems named after a college team.

164.blurb: Rutgers' basketball program dates to 1907, and the New Jersey school made a remarkable run to the 1976 Final Four as an undefeated team before losing to Michigan. The Scarlet Knights joined the Big Ten in 2014 and have worked to rebuild their program.
164.mascotOrigin: The Scarlet Knight was adopted as the official mascot in 1955 after a student contest, replacing "Chanticleers" — the scarlet color had been associated with Rutgers since 1869.
164.iconicMoment: The 1976 undefeated regular season and Final Four run under coach Tom Young, led by Phil Sellers and Mike Dabney, remains the program's crowning achievement.
164.funFact: Rutgers hosted the first-ever college football game in 1869, and while basketball has lived in football's shadow, the 1976 Final Four run proved the Scarlet Knights could compete with the nation's best.

166.blurb: New Mexico State in Las Cruces competes in Conference USA and has been one of the most consistent mid-major programs in the country. The Aggies have made over 20 NCAA Tournament appearances and have dominated their conference for decades.
166.mascotOrigin: The Aggies nickname reflects the university's roots as an agricultural college, with the mascot "Pistol Pete" paying tribute to the ranching culture of southern New Mexico.
166.iconicMoment: The 1970 Final Four appearance, when the Aggies reached the national semifinal and established New Mexico State as a legitimate basketball power.
166.funFact: New Mexico State has one of the longest active NCAA Tournament appearance streaks among mid-major programs, regularly qualifying for the Big Dance from whichever conference they play in.

167.blurb: The University of New Mexico competes in the Mountain West Conference and plays in "The Pit," one of the most legendary home-court advantages in college basketball. The Lobos have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances and have a passionate fan base in Albuquerque.
167.mascotOrigin: The Lobos (Spanish for "wolves") mascot was adopted in 1920, reflecting the Mexican gray wolves native to the American Southwest and New Mexico's bilingual heritage.
167.iconicMoment: The 2013-14 season, when the Lobos went undefeated in Mountain West play and earned a high seed in the NCAA Tournament, showcasing the power of The Pit's home-court advantage.
167.funFact: The Pit (Dreamstyle Arena) is built below ground level, creating a sunken arena effect that amplifies crowd noise and gives the Lobos one of the best home-court advantages in the country.

171.blurb: Columbia's basketball program dates to 1900 at the Ivy League university in Manhattan, New York City. The Lions have won multiple Ivy League titles and made several NCAA Tournament appearances, with their peak in the 1960s-70s. As an Ivy League school, Columbia does not offer athletic scholarships.
171.mascotOrigin: The Lions nickname has been associated with Columbia since the late 1800s, with the lion symbolizing strength and courage — a fitting emblem for the university named after Christopher Columbus.
171.iconicMoment: Jim McMillian leading the Lions to an undefeated Ivy League season in 1968 before his standout NBA career with the Lakers.
171.funFact: Columbia's Levien Gymnasium is located in Morningside Heights, Manhattan — perhaps the only Division I basketball venue where players can grab a slice of New York pizza within a block of the arena.

172.blurb: Cornell's basketball program dates to 1898 at the Ivy League university in Ithaca, New York. The Big Red have won multiple Ivy League titles and are best remembered for their magical 2010 NCAA Tournament run. As an Ivy League school, Cornell does not offer athletic scholarships.
172.mascotOrigin: The 'Big Red' nickname simply references the university's official color, carnelian red, and has been used since the early 1900s — Cornell has no official mascot.
172.iconicMoment: The 2010 NCAA Tournament run to the Sweet Sixteen, upsetting Temple and Wisconsin behind Ryan Wittman's sharpshooting, the best Ivy League tournament run in decades.
172.funFact: Cornell's Newman Arena is perched on a hill in Ithaca, New York, where the notoriously harsh winters have made visiting teams dread the trip — the joke is that 'Ithaca is Gorges' but the weather is not.

179.blurb: St. Bonaventure's basketball program dates to 1920, and the small Franciscan university in Olean, New York has punched well above its weight. The Bonnies reached the Final Four in 1970 behind Bob Lanier and have remained a passionate Atlantic 10 program.
179.mascotOrigin: The "Bonnies" is a shortened form of Bonaventure. The wolf mascot represents St. Francis of Assisi's legendary taming of the Wolf of Gubbio.
179.iconicMoment: The 1970 Final Four run led by Bob Lanier, who averaged 27.6 points per game that season before a knee injury in the East Regional final against Villanova ended his college career.
179.funFact: Bob Lanier's jersey was retired by St. Bonaventure, and his famous size-22 shoes became one of basketball's most iconic symbols — the Reilly Center court is named in his honor.

183.blurb: Syracuse's basketball program began around 1900 and ranks fifth all-time in total wins among NCAA programs. Jim Boeheim, a former walk-on who earned a scholarship, coached the Orange for 47 years from 1976 to 2023 — the longest tenure at one school in Division I history. His signature 2-3 zone defense led Syracuse to the 2003 national championship behind freshman Carmelo Anthony. The JMA Wireless Dome (formerly Carrier Dome) is the largest arena in college basketball with a capacity exceeding 35,000, and Syracuse has led the nation in attendance 25 times.
183.mascotOrigin: The teams were known as the 'Orangemen' for most of their history before shortening to 'Orange' in 2004, reflecting the school's primary color
183.iconicMoment: Carmelo Anthony leading Syracuse to the 2003 NCAA Championship as a freshman, scoring 20 points in the title game victory over Kansas
183.funFact: Jim Boeheim was a skinny walk-on in 1963 who shared a backcourt with Hall of Famer Dave Bing — he went on to spend his entire adult life at Syracuse as player, assistant, and head coach

189.blurb: Bowling Green's basketball program started in 1915 in northwest Ohio and the Falcons compete in the Mid-American Conference. The program enjoyed its greatest success in the 1940s-60s under Harold Anderson, reaching the NIT finals multiple times when the NIT was the premier postseason tournament. Nate Thurmond, one of the NBA's all-time greats, played at Bowling Green.
