189.mascotOrigin: The Falcons nickname was chosen in 1927 after an assistant basketball coach read an article about falconry and proposed the name, noting that falcons were powerful for their size.
189.iconicMoment: Nate Thurmond dominating college basketball in the early 1960s before becoming an NBA legend, putting Bowling Green on the national basketball map.
189.funFact: Bowling Green's Anderson Arena, opened in 1960, is named after legendary coach Harold Anderson and was once called 'The House That Roars' for its deafening crowd noise.

193.blurb: Miami University in Oxford, Ohio, is known as the "Cradle of Coaches" and competes in the Mid-American Conference. The RedHawks have a long basketball tradition and have produced coaches and players who made significant impacts on the sport at higher levels.
193.mascotOrigin: The RedHawks name was adopted in 1997, replacing "Redskins" after a student vote. The red-tailed hawk is native to the Oxford, Ohio area and represents speed and vision.
193.iconicMoment: Ron Harper's dominant career at Miami (OH) in the mid-1980s, averaging over 24 points per game as a senior before being drafted 8th overall by the Cleveland Cavaliers.
193.funFact: Miami University is called the "Cradle of Coaches" because legendary coaches like Weeb Ewbank, Woody Hayes, Bo Schembechler, and Ara Parseghian all coached or attended the school.

194.blurb: Ohio State's basketball program formed in 1898 and appeared in the very first NCAA Championship game in 1939, losing to Oregon. The 'Buckeye' name comes from the Ohio state tree; the mascot Brutus Buckeye — a student wearing a giant buckeye nut head — debuted in 1965. The Buckeyes won their lone national championship in 1960, led by future Hall of Famers Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek. A little-known fact: future Indiana coaching legend Bob Knight was a reserve on that championship team.
194.mascotOrigin: Named after the Ohio buckeye tree, the state tree of Ohio; 'Buckeye' has referred to Ohio residents since 1788 and was formally adopted as the school nickname in 1950
194.iconicMoment: The 1960 NCAA Championship, led by Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek, defeating California 75-55 for Ohio State's only basketball title
194.funFact: Future Indiana coaching legend Bob Knight was a reserve player on Ohio State's 1960 championship team, sitting behind Hall of Famers Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek

195.blurb: Ohio University in Athens competes in the Mid-American Conference and has a basketball tradition that includes a 1960 Final Four appearance. The Bobcats have produced NBA players and maintain one of the most passionate fan bases in the MAC.
195.mascotOrigin: The Bobcats mascot was adopted in 1925, named after the wild bobcats found in the hills of southeastern Ohio surrounding the Athens campus.
195.iconicMoment: The 1960 Final Four appearance, when Ohio reached the national semifinals before falling to California, the greatest achievement in program history.
195.funFact: The Convocation Center in Athens is known as "The Convo" and is considered one of the toughest road environments in the MAC due to its intimate seating and rowdy student section.

197.blurb: Oklahoma State has a proud basketball history, winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1945 and 1946 under coach Henry Iba. The Cowboys compete in the Big 12 and have produced legends including Bob Kurland, the first player to regularly dunk, and have reached the Final Four six times overall.
197.mascotOrigin: The Cowboys nickname reflects Oklahoma's ranching and frontier heritage, honoring the cowboy culture that defined the state's identity in the American West.
197.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1945 and 1946 under Henry Iba, featuring Bob Kurland, who is credited with pioneering the dunk and the goaltending rule.
197.funFact: Bob Kurland of Oklahoma State is credited as the first player to regularly dunk the basketball, and his dominance led directly to the NCAA adopting the goaltending rule.

198.blurb: Oral Roberts University in Tulsa, Oklahoma, competes in the Summit League and had one of the most improbable NCAA Tournament runs in history in 2021. The Golden Eagles have a passionate following and play an up-tempo style of basketball.
198.mascotOrigin: The Golden Eagles mascot represents soaring achievement and spiritual aspiration, aligned with the Christian mission of the university founded by evangelist Oral Roberts.
198.iconicMoment: The 2021 NCAA Tournament Cinderella run as a #15 seed, upsetting #2 Ohio State and #7 Florida to reach the Sweet Sixteen, led by Max Abmas and Kevin Obanor.
198.funFact: Oral Roberts' 2021 Sweet Sixteen run was only the second time a #15 seed had ever reached that stage, joining Florida Gulf Coast's 2013 run as the only such achievements in tournament history.

201.blurb: Oklahoma has a strong basketball tradition in the Big 12, reaching the Final Four multiple times including the 1988 National Championship Game under Billy Tubbs. The Sooners have produced NBA stars like Blake Griffin, Buddy Hield, and Trae Young, and the program's high-scoring style under Tubbs electrified the sport in the 1980s.
201.mascotOrigin: The "Sooners" name comes from settlers who entered Oklahoma Territory before the official 1889 land run, staking claims "sooner" than allowed — the name represents competitive initiative.
201.iconicMoment: The 1988 season under Billy Tubbs, when the Sooners averaged over 100 points per game and reached the National Championship Game before falling to Kansas.
201.funFact: Trae Young's 2017-18 season at Oklahoma was one of the most hyped in college basketball history — he led the nation in both scoring and assists, something no player had ever done before.

202.blurb: Tulsa's basketball program dates to 1935, and the Golden Hurricane have been a consistent mid-major contender. Under coaches like Nolan Richardson, Tubby Smith, and Bill Self, Tulsa made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and deep runs.
202.mascotOrigin: The "Golden Hurricane" name originated in 1922 when a sports commentator said the football team "rolled over opponents like a hurricane." The singular form is intentional.
202.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2000 Elite Eight as a 12-seed under coach Bill Self, upsetting UNLV and Miami before falling to North Carolina.
202.funFact: Tulsa served as a launching pad for legendary coaches — Nolan Richardson, Tubby Smith, and Bill Self all coached at Tulsa before leading programs to NCAA Championships elsewhere.

204.blurb: Oregon State competes in the Pac-12 (now independent/transitioning) and has a basketball history that includes a 2021 Elite Eight run and decades of Pac-10/12 competition. The Beavers won back-to-back national championships in 1981 and 1982 under Ralph Miller... actually, their greatest achievement was reaching the 2021 Elite Eight as a #12 seed.
204.mascotOrigin: The Beavers mascot was adopted in 1910, honoring the industrious beaver that is the Oregon state animal and a symbol of the Pacific Northwest's natural heritage.
204.iconicMoment: The 2021 NCAA Tournament run as a #12 seed, upsetting Tennessee, Oklahoma State, and Loyola Chicago to reach the Elite Eight in one of the tournament's best Cinderella stories.
204.funFact: Gary Payton starred at Oregon State in the late 1980s before being drafted second overall by the Seattle SuperSonics, becoming one of the greatest defensive guards in NBA history.

213.blurb: Penn State competes in the Big Ten and has historically struggled to match the basketball success of its football program. However, the Nittany Lions have made NCAA Tournament appearances and have been working to elevate the program in one of college basketball's toughest conferences.
213.mascotOrigin: The Nittany Lion is a now-extinct eastern mountain lion that once roamed Mount Nittany near the University Park campus, adopted as the mascot in 1907.
213.iconicMoment: The 2001 season under coach Jerry Dunn, when Penn State reached the NCAA Tournament Sweet Sixteen by upsetting North Carolina, the deepest tournament run in program history at that time.
213.funFact: The Bryce Jordan Center seats over 15,000 fans and is one of the largest arenas in the Big Ten, though Penn State has struggled historically to fill it for basketball games.

218.blurb: Temple's basketball program dates to 1894, making it one of the oldest in the country. The Philadelphia school was a perennial power under John Chaney, whose matchup zone defense and fiery personality made Temple one of the most feared programs from the 1980s through 2000s.
218.mascotOrigin: The Owls nickname was adopted because Temple was originally a night school — owls are nocturnal creatures, and the name reflected the students who attended classes after dark.
218.iconicMoment: John Chaney's tenure from 1982-2006, during which Temple made 17 NCAA Tournament appearances and earned five No. 1 seeds in the regular season AP poll.
218.funFact: Temple was founded as a night school for working-class students, which is why the owl — a creature of the night — became the mascot, and the Liacouras Center remains one of Philadelphia's premier basketball venues.

219.blurb: The University of Pennsylvania competes in the Ivy League and has one of the most successful basketball programs among Ivy schools. The Quakers have won more Ivy League titles than any other school and have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances, including a 1979 Final Four run.
219.mascotOrigin: The Quakers name honors William Penn and the Quaker heritage of Pennsylvania's founding, reflecting the religious and cultural roots of the Commonwealth.
219.iconicMoment: The 1979 Final Four appearance, when the Quakers reached the national semifinals and competed against eventual champion Michigan State and Magic Johnson.
219.funFact: Penn's Palestra is known as the "Cathedral of College Basketball" and has hosted more college basketball games than any other arena in the country, making it a sacred venue in the sport's history.

221.blurb: Pittsburgh competes in the ACC and has a strong basketball tradition, particularly under coaches Doc Carlson and Jamie Dixon. The Panthers won the 1928 and 1930 national championships (Helms Foundation) and have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances, including an Elite Eight run in 2009.
221.mascotOrigin: The Panthers mascot was adopted in 1909, chosen through a student contest for its ferocity and aggressiveness, and the panther has been Pitt's symbol for over a century.
221.iconicMoment: The 2009 season under Jamie Dixon, when #1 seed Pitt reached the Elite Eight before falling to Villanova, one of the program's strongest modern seasons.
221.funFact: Pitt's Petersen Events Center replaced the legendary Fitzgerald Fieldhouse and has become one of the better home-court advantages in the ACC since its 2002 opening.

222.blurb: Villanova's basketball program was established in 1920 at the private Catholic university outside Philadelphia. The Wildcats have won three national championships (1985, 2016, 2018) and are a cornerstone of Big East basketball. Their 1985 title as a No. 8 seed — shooting 78.6% from the field against Patrick Ewing's Georgetown in 'The Perfect Game' — remains the lowest seed ever to win the championship. Kris Jenkins' buzzer-beating three-pointer to beat North Carolina in the 2016 title game is considered one of the greatest shots in sports history.
222.mascotOrigin: The 'Wildcats' nickname has been used since the early days of the program, though the exact reason for its selection is lost to history
222.iconicMoment: Kris Jenkins' buzzer-beating three-pointer to give Villanova a 77-74 victory over North Carolina in the 2016 NCAA Championship game
222.funFact: In the 1985 championship game known as 'The Perfect Game,' Villanova shot 78.6% from the field — a championship game record that still stands — to upset heavily favored Georgetown

225.blurb: Brown's basketball program dates to 1900 at the Ivy League institution in Providence, Rhode Island. The Bears have competed in the Ivy League since its formation and have had periods of conference competitiveness. As an Ivy League school, Brown does not offer athletic scholarships.
225.mascotOrigin: The Bears nickname naturally derives from the university's name, Brown, which has been associated with the bear since the early 1900s.
225.iconicMoment: Winning the 1986 Ivy League title to earn the program's most recent NCAA Tournament appearance, a season that remains the high-water mark for Brown basketball.
225.funFact: Brown's Pizzitola Sports Center is one of the smallest Division I basketball venues in the country, seating around 2,800, creating an intimate atmosphere for Ivy League games.

227.blurb: Rhode Island's basketball program dates to 1908, and the Rams have been an Atlantic 10 Conference staple. The program enjoyed a golden era under coach Frank Keaney, who pioneered the fast-break style of basketball in the 1930s and 1940s.
227.mascotOrigin: The Rams nickname comes from Rhode Island's status as a land-grant university with agricultural roots — the ram representing strength and the state's farming heritage.
227.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1998 Elite Eight under coach Jim Harrick, where stars Lamar Odom and Cuttino Mobley led a thrilling tournament run.
227.funFact: Frank Keaney, Rhode Island's legendary coach, is credited as one of the pioneers of fast-break basketball — his up-tempo style in the 1930s-40s was revolutionary and influenced the modern game.

228.blurb: Clemson's basketball program was first sponsored in 1912 and won its first four games that season. The Tigers are a founding member of the ACC and have built a competitive program highlighted by multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. Clemson's iconic Tiger Paw logo, introduced in 1970, is one of the most recognizable symbols in college sports. The program reached the Elite Eight in both 1980 — when they upset No. 1 Duke in overtime — and again in 2024, marking the high points of Clemson basketball history.
228.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' name reflects the school's orange and purple colors; Clemson's iconic Tiger Paw logo was introduced on this date in 1970 and has become one of college sports' most recognizable symbols
228.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 1 ranked Duke 87-82 in overtime at Littlejohn Coliseum on January 9, 1980, before the largest crowd in arena history
228.funFact: Clemson's first indoor basketball game on campus in 1913 was played in the basement of the administration building, with players reportedly dribbling around the support pillars

231.blurb: Furman's basketball program dates to 1908 at the private university in Greenville, South Carolina. The Paladins compete in the Southern Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances.
231.mascotOrigin: The Paladins nickname refers to the twelve legendary knights of Charlemagne's court, chosen to represent honor, courage, and chivalric ideals.
231.iconicMoment: Upsetting fourth-seeded Virginia 68-67 in the first round of the 2023 NCAA Tournament on a buzzer-beating three-pointer, one of March Madness's most thrilling finishes.
231.funFact: Furman is one of the few schools with the Paladins mascot, and the university's beautiful lakeside campus in Greenville has been ranked among the most scenic in America.

232.blurb: The College of Charleston's basketball program dates to 1900, making it one of the oldest in the South. The Cougars compete in the CAA and gained national recognition under John Kresse, who won over 500 games from 1979 to 2002. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
232.mascotOrigin: The Cougars nickname was adopted to represent the fierce, agile predator, giving the historic liberal arts college a strong athletic identity.
232.iconicMoment: John Kresse reaching his 500th career victory, cementing his legacy as one of the most successful coaches in mid-major basketball history.
232.funFact: The College of Charleston, founded in 1770, is the oldest college in South Carolina and the 13th oldest in the nation — its TD Arena sits in the heart of historic downtown Charleston.

233.blurb: South Dakota's basketball program dates to 1902, and the Coyotes made the transition from Division II to Division I in 2008. Competing in the Summit League, USD has steadily built competitiveness in their D-I era.
233.mascotOrigin: The Coyotes nickname reflects the prairie wildlife native to South Dakota, where coyotes are among the most commonly seen wild animals.
233.iconicMoment: Winning the 2024 Summit League Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance since moving to Division I.
233.funFact: The University of South Dakota is the state's oldest public university, founded in 1862, and its Sanford Coyote Sports Center gives the basketball program one of the best facilities in the Summit League.

235.blurb: Memphis basketball began in 1920 and the program became a national powerhouse under several notable coaches. The 'Tigers' nickname was adopted in the 1910s. Larry Finch, a Memphis native and legend, played on the team that reached the 1973 NCAA Championship game and later coached the program. Under John Calipari, Memphis reached the 2008 title game before Mario Chalmers' three-pointer forced overtime in favor of Kansas. The program has been one of the most successful non-power-conference teams in college basketball history.
235.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, with the live mascot TOM (Tigers of Memphis) appearing at games
235.iconicMoment: The 2008 NCAA Championship game against Kansas, where Memphis led by nine points with two minutes left before Mario Chalmers' miraculous three-pointer forced overtime
235.funFact: Memphis has produced more NBA players than almost any non-power-conference school, including Penny Hardaway (who later returned as head coach), Derrick Rose, and Tyreke Evans

236.blurb: Chattanooga's basketball program dates to 1904 and the Mocs compete in the Southern Conference. The program has won multiple SoCon championships and made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances. The Mocs have been one of the most successful mid-major programs in the South.
236.mascotOrigin: The 'Mocs' nickname is short for Moccasins, later changed to represent a mockingbird — the name has evolved over the decades while the shortened 'Mocs' persisted.
236.iconicMoment: The 1997 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of third-seeded Georgia, a thrilling victory that put the Mocs on the national map.
236.funFact: Chattanooga's McKenzie Arena is known as the 'Roundhouse' for its distinctive circular design and has hosted NCAA Tournament games as a neutral site.

238.blurb: Vanderbilt claims one of basketball's earliest origins, defeating the Nashville YMCA 9-6 on February 7, 1893 — just two years after James Naismith invented the sport. The 'Commodores' nickname honors founder Cornelius Vanderbilt, a steamship entrepreneur whose rank of Commodore was the highest in the U.S. Navy. As the SEC's only private university, Vanderbilt has carved out a unique niche, with Memorial Gymnasium's distinctive end-of-the-floor bench setup creating one of basketball's most unusual home-court advantages. Perry Wallace broke the SEC's color barrier when he became the conference's first Black varsity basketball player in 1967.
238.mascotOrigin: Named for university founder Cornelius Vanderbilt, a steamship tycoon known as 'the Commodore' — the highest rank in the U.S. Navy at the time
238.iconicMoment: Perry Wallace breaking the SEC's color barrier as the conference's first African-American varsity basketball player from 1967-1970, facing hostility at opposing venues
238.funFact: Vanderbilt won the 1951 SEC Tournament in an upset over Kentucky, and legend has it that 'Kentucky' was already inscribed on the championship trophy before the Commodores' victory

239.blurb: Baylor's basketball program began in 1906 at the world's largest Baptist university, located in Waco, Texas. The 'Bears' mascot dates to 1914 when a live bear was brought to campus. Baylor's greatest moment came in 2021 when Scott Drew's Bears won the NCAA Championship, demolcing previously undefeated Gonzaga 86-70 in the title game. Drew had rebuilt the program from scratch after a tragedy-marred scandal in 2003, making the championship one of the most remarkable turnarounds in college sports history.
239.mascotOrigin: The first live bear mascot was brought to campus in 1914; the current live bears Joy and Lady are North American black bears that live on campus
239.iconicMoment: Winning the 2021 NCAA Championship by dominating undefeated Gonzaga 86-70, capping one of college basketball's greatest rebuilding stories under Scott Drew
239.funFact: When Scott Drew took over in 2003, the program was in disarray after a murder scandal; he rebuilt it from nearly nothing to a national championship in 18 years — one of the great turnaround stories in all of sports

242.blurb: Rice's basketball program dates to 1914, and the Houston-based private university competes in Conference USA. While Rice is better known for its world-class academics and engineering, the Owls have had competitive stretches in basketball.
242.mascotOrigin: The Owls nickname was chosen in 1912 because the owl is a traditional symbol of wisdom, fitting for a university known for academic excellence.
242.iconicMoment: Winning the 2003 Conference USA Tournament and reaching the NCAA Tournament under coach Willis Wilson, a rare March Madness appearance for the program.
242.funFact: Rice's student body is so small (under 4,000 undergraduates) that it is one of the smallest Division I basketball schools in the country, yet it competes in a Power-adjacent conference.

245.blurb: Texas A&M, the state's first public university (opened 1876), has fielded basketball teams since around 1912. The 'Aggies' nickname reflects the school's agricultural roots, formally adopted in the 1920s. Reveille, a dog the school's first live mascot since 1931, is the highest-ranking member of the Corps of Cadets and tradition holds that if she barks in class, the professor should dismiss the students. The basketball program experienced a surge under Billy Gillispie in 2007, spending most of the season in the top 10 and becoming the first Big 12 South team to win at Kansas.
245.mascotOrigin: The 'Aggies' name reflects the school's agricultural origins; mascot Reveille — a stray dog hit by cadets' car in 1931 — got her name when she barked at the morning bugle call
245.iconicMoment: The 2007 season under Billy Gillispie, when A&M spent most of the year in the top 10 and became the first Big 12 South team to win at Allen Fieldhouse
245.funFact: Reveille holds the rank of five-diamond general in the Corps of Cadets, outranking every student including the Corps commander, and tradition says if she falls asleep on a cadet's bed, the cadet must sleep elsewhere

248.blurb: Houston's basketball program began in 1946 and rose to national prominence through Elvin Hayes and the famous 1968 'Game of the Century' — a nationally televised upset of top-ranked UCLA before 50,000 fans in the Astrodome. The Cougars reached their apex as 'Phi Slama Jama' from 1982-1984, featuring Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler in an explosive, dunk-heavy attack. The nickname, coined by a Houston Post writer after a game with 29 dunks, captured an era. Houston won its first NCAA Championship in 2025, finally breaking through after heartbreaking losses in the 1980s.
248.mascotOrigin: Named by former head coach John Bender, who brought the 'Cougars' name from his previous job at Washington State because of his fondness for the animal
248.iconicMoment: The 'Phi Slama Jama' era (1982-84) featuring Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler, with three consecutive Final Four appearances and a style that changed basketball forever
248.funFact: The Cougar Paw hand sign originated from an insult: in 1953, live mascot Shasta lost a toe in a cage door accident, and Texas students mocked UH by folding their thumbs — Houston fans later embraced the gesture as their own

249.blurb: The University of North Texas in Denton competes in the American Athletic Conference and has a basketball tradition that includes a 1988 Sun Belt Conference title. The Mean Green have produced NBA players and continue to build their program under conference realignment.
249.mascotOrigin: The "Mean Green" nickname originated in the 1960s as a description of the football defense, and it stuck as the primary athletic identity, replacing the previous "Eagles" mascot.
249.iconicMoment: The 2021 NCAA Tournament appearance, when North Texas upset #13 seed Purdue in the first round, giving the Mean Green their first tournament victory in school history.
249.funFact: North Texas's Super Pit arena holds over 10,000 fans and is one of the largest on-campus basketball venues in the American Athletic Conference.

250.blurb: UT Arlington's basketball program dates to 1959, and the school is the largest university in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex by enrollment. The Mavericks compete in the Western Athletic Conference and have had occasional conference success.
250.mascotOrigin: The Mavericks name was adopted in 1971 when the school joined the Southland Conference, chosen to reflect the independent, untamed spirit of Texas ranching culture.
250.iconicMoment: Winning the 2008 Southland Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, the program's most recent March Madness appearance.
250.funFact: UT Arlington is one of the largest universities in Texas by enrollment and sits between Dallas and Fort Worth, giving it access to one of the largest metropolitan recruiting grounds in the state.

251.blurb: Texas basketball dates to 1906 at the state's flagship university, which opened in 1883. The live mascot Bevo, a Longhorn steer, has been a tradition since 1916 — the first Bevo was famously barbecued at a football banquet and reportedly the meat was tough. While Texas has historically been a football school, the basketball program produced Kevin Durant, who played just one season in 2007 before becoming one of the NBA's greatest scorers. The Longhorns have been regular NCAA Tournament participants and joined the SEC in 2024.
251.mascotOrigin: Named for the Texas Longhorn cattle; the first live mascot Bevo arrived in 1916 after an alumnus bought an orange-tinted steer with $1 donations from 124 alumni
251.iconicMoment: Kevin Durant's dominant 2007 freshman season, averaging 25.8 points per game and winning the Naismith Award before being selected second overall in the NBA Draft
251.funFact: The first Bevo was famously barbecued and served at a football banquet — according to The Alcalde, the meat was tough; Texas is currently on Bevo XV

252.blurb: BYU's basketball program was established in 1902, making it one of the oldest west of the Mississippi. The 'Cougars' name was adopted in 1924 when athletic director George Cobabe chose the animal for its strength and courage. BYU captured national attention in 2011 when Jimmer Fredette's sensational scoring sparked 'Jimmermania' — earning him the Naismith Award while leading the country in scoring. The program has won 27 conference championships and produced NBA talent like Danny Ainge. BYU joined the Big 12 Conference in 2023.
252.mascotOrigin: Athletic director George Cobabe chose 'Cougars' in 1924 for the animal's qualities of strength and courage
252.iconicMoment: Jimmer Fredette's 2011 'Jimmermania' season — scoring 52 points against New Mexico and winning the Naismith Award as national player of the year
252.funFact: Jimmer Fredette dropped 49 points at Arizona's McKale Center in 2009, still the most points ever scored by a visiting player there — the Arizona fans gave him a standing ovation

253.blurb: Southern Utah's basketball program dates to 1964, and the Cedar City school competes in the WAC. The Thunderbirds have been competitive in their conference and play in one of the most scenic settings in college basketball.
253.mascotOrigin: The Thunderbirds name draws from Native American mythology of the Southwest, where the thunderbird is a powerful supernatural creature associated with storms and strength.
253.iconicMoment: Winning the 2001 Mid-Continent Conference Tournament to earn the program's only NCAA Tournament appearance.
253.funFact: Southern Utah's campus in Cedar City sits at 5,800 feet elevation near Bryce Canyon and Zion National Park, giving it one of the most scenic settings of any Division I program.

254.blurb: Utah's basketball program dates to 1908, and the Utes won the NCAA Championship in 1944 under coach Vadal Peterson. The program reached the championship game again in 1998 under Rick Majerus, losing to Kentucky. Utah has been a consistent presence in the Big 12 (formerly Pac-12).
254.mascotOrigin: The Utes name honors the Ute people, the indigenous Native American tribe of the region. The university has a formal agreement with the Ute Indian Tribe to use the name.
254.iconicMoment: The 1998 NCAA Championship game appearance under Rick Majerus, where All-American Andre Miller and Keith Van Horn (earlier) made Utah a legitimate national contender.
254.funFact: Utah's 1944 NCAA Championship team was largely composed of players who had been rejected by the military for various medical reasons during World War II.

256.blurb: James Madison University in Harrisonburg, Virginia, competes in the Sun Belt Conference after spending decades in the Colonial Athletic Association. The Dukes have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for competitive basketball in the mid-major landscape.
256.mascotOrigin: The Dukes nickname honors James Madison, the fourth President of the United States and "Father of the Constitution," for whom the university is named.
256.iconicMoment: The Dukes' 2013 NCAA Tournament upset of LIU Brooklyn, followed by a competitive game against Indiana, showcasing the program's ability to compete on the big stage.
256.funFact: James Madison's Convocation Center is one of the louder mid-major arenas in the country, with students creating a hostile environment for visiting teams.

257.blurb: Richmond's basketball program dates to 1914, and the Spiders have been one of the Atlantic 10's most competitive programs. Richmond has a proud history of NCAA Tournament upsets and produced NBA player Kevin Anderson.
257.mascotOrigin: The Spiders nickname dates to 1894 when a newspaper reporter said the Richmond baseball team's thin, lanky players looked like spiders on the field.
257.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 2 seed Syracuse 73-69 in the 2011 NCAA Tournament as a 12-seed, part of a run to the Sweet Sixteen under coach Chris Mooney.
257.funFact: Richmond is one of the few schools to have upset a No. 1, No. 2, and No. 3 seed in the NCAA Tournament across its history, earning a reputation as a giant-killer.

258.blurb: Virginia's basketball program dates to 1905, and the 'Cavaliers' name references the Royalist supporters of King Charles I who are said to have fled to colonial Virginia. The program authored college basketball's greatest redemption story: one year after becoming the first No. 1 seed to lose to a No. 16 seed (falling to UMBC 74-54 in 2018), Virginia won the 2019 NCAA Championship in overtime against Texas Tech. In a remarkable twist, the coach who led UMBC to that historic upset, Ryan Odom, was later hired as Virginia's head coach in 2025.
258.mascotOrigin: Named for the Royalists of the English Civil War who remained loyal to King Charles I and were associated with colonial Virginia aristocracy
258.iconicMoment: Winning the 2019 NCAA Championship one year after the historic UMBC loss — NBC Sports called it 'the greatest redemption story in the history of sports'
258.funFact: The coach who led UMBC's historic 2018 upset of Virginia as a No. 16 seed, Ryan Odom, was hired as Virginia's head coach in 2025 after Tony Bennett's surprise retirement

259.blurb: Virginia Tech's basketball program dates to 1909, and the Hokies have been an ACC member since 2004. The program reached new heights under Buzz Williams and then Mike Young, becoming a consistent NCAA Tournament team.
259.mascotOrigin: The "Hokie" is a made-up word from an 1896 spirit yell written by O.M. Stull. The HokieBird mascot is a turkey-like creature representing the university's agricultural heritage.
259.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2019 Sweet Sixteen and the program's deep 2023 tournament run, establishing Virginia Tech as a legitimate ACC basketball contender.
259.funFact: Nobody knows exactly what a "Hokie" is — the word was invented for a school cheer in 1896 and has no actual meaning, making it one of the most unique mascot names in college sports.

261.blurb: Vermont's basketball program dates to 1903, and the Catamounts have dominated the America East Conference in recent years. Under coaches Tom Brennan and John Becker, Vermont has been a perennial conference champion and NCAA Tournament participant.
261.mascotOrigin: The Catamounts name refers to the eastern catamount (mountain lion), which once roamed the Green Mountains of Vermont and is the state's unofficial animal.
261.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 4 seed Syracuse 60-57 in the 2005 NCAA Tournament behind T.J. Sorrentine's clutch three-pointer, one of the most famous upsets in first-round history.
261.funFact: Vermont's T.J. Sorrentine hit one of the most iconic shots in NCAA Tournament history — a deep three-pointer with under a minute left to seal the 2005 upset of Syracuse.

263.blurb: Drake's basketball program began in 1906 in Des Moines, Iowa, and the Bulldogs compete in the Missouri Valley Conference. The program reached its peak in the late 1960s under coach Maury John, reaching the Final Four in 1969. Drake has remained a competitive MVC program.
263.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, representing the tenacious, never-give-up attitude the university wanted in its athletic teams.
263.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1969 Final Four behind the play of Willie Wise and Willie McCarter, the greatest season in program history.
263.funFact: Drake's Knapp Center hosted the first-ever NCAA Tournament basketball game played in Iowa, and Des Moines has since become a regular host city for early-round tournament games.

264.blurb: Washington's basketball program dates to 1896, making it one of the oldest on the West Coast. The Huskies won the NCAA Championship in 1953 and have been a consistent Pac-12 (now Big Ten) program, producing NBA talent like Brandon Roy, Markelle Fultz, and Isaiah Thomas.
264.mascotOrigin: The Huskies name was adopted in 1922 by student vote, replacing "Sun Dodgers." The Alaskan Malamute mascot Dubs has become a beloved campus figure.
264.iconicMoment: Winning the 1953 NCAA Championship under coach Tippy Dye, the program's only national title, defeating Kansas 69-68 in the final.
264.funFact: Washington's Brandon Roy was named the 2006 Pac-10 Player of the Year and went on to become NBA Rookie of the Year, before injuries tragically shortened his career.

265.blurb: Washington State's basketball program dates to 1902, and the Pullman-based school has been a competitive presence in the Pac-12 (now WCC). Coach Tony Bennett built the Cougars into a national contender in the mid-2000s before leaving for Virginia.
265.mascotOrigin: The Cougars name was adopted in 1919, chosen after the cougars (mountain lions) that roamed the Palouse region of eastern Washington.
265.iconicMoment: Tony Bennett's 2006-08 run, including a 26-8 season in 2007 and Sweet Sixteen appearance, the program's best stretch in modern history.
265.funFact: Tony Bennett developed his famous "pack line" defense at Washington State before perfecting it at Virginia, where he won a national championship — making WSU the birthplace of one of basketball's most influential modern defenses.

269.blurb: Marquette basketball dates to 1916 and the program won its lone NCAA Championship in 1977 — Al McGuire's final game as coach, having announced his mid-season retirement. Previously known as the 'Warriors,' Marquette changed to 'Golden Eagles' in 1994 out of respect for Native American communities. Dwyane Wade put Marquette back on the national stage in 2003, recording a triple-double in the Elite Eight upset of Kentucky to reach the Final Four. The program now plays at Fiserv Forum, sharing an arena with the NBA's Milwaukee Bucks.
269.mascotOrigin: Changed from 'Warriors' to 'Golden Eagles' in 1994 out of respect for Native American communities; the mascot is named 'Iggy' after St. Ignatius of Loyola
269.iconicMoment: Al McGuire winning the 1977 NCAA Championship in his final game as coach, having announced his retirement mid-season — he broke down in tears as time expired
269.funFact: Dwyane Wade was only recruited by Marquette, Illinois State, and DePaul due to low ACT scores, and he had to sit out his freshman year — he went on to become the highest-drafted player in Marquette history

270.blurb: The University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee competes in the Horizon League and had its greatest basketball success in the mid-2000s under coach Bruce Pearl. The Panthers have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and represent the largest city in Wisconsin in Division I basketball.
270.mascotOrigin: The Panthers mascot was adopted in 1964 to represent power and agility, giving the Milwaukee campus a strong and distinctive athletic identity.
270.iconicMoment: The 2005 Sweet Sixteen run under Bruce Pearl, when the Panthers upset Alabama and Boston College before falling to Illinois, the program's deepest NCAA Tournament run.
270.funFact: Bruce Pearl's tenure at Milwaukee launched him to stardom, leading to his hire at Tennessee and later Auburn, where he reached the Final Four in 2019.

275.blurb: Wisconsin's basketball program dates to the 1890s, making it one of the oldest in the Big Ten. The 'Badgers' nickname refers to 1820s lead miners who lived in hillside tunnels 'like badgers' during Wisconsin winters. The Badgers won their only NCAA Championship in 1941 in their first-ever tournament appearance. The program experienced a modern renaissance with 19 consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances from 1999-2017 and back-to-back Final Four runs in 2014 and 2015. Bucky Badger's full name is 'Buckingham U. Badger,' from the fight song urging the team to 'buck right through that line.'
275.mascotOrigin: Named for 1820s lead miners who lived in hillside tunnels 'like badgers' during harsh Wisconsin winters; Wisconsin was dubbed the 'Badger State' as a result
275.iconicMoment: Back-to-back Final Four appearances in 2014 and 2015, including a run to the 2015 National Championship game
275.funFact: Wisconsin tried to replace Bucky Badger with a cow named 'Henrietta Holstein' in the 1970s, but the university ultimately kept Bucky — whose full name is 'Buckingham U. Badger'

276.blurb: Marshall University in Huntington, West Virginia, competes in the Sun Belt Conference and has a passionate basketball fan base. The Thundering Herd won the 1947 NAIB Championship and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances, most notably under coach Billy Donovan's tenure in the 1990s.
276.mascotOrigin: The Thundering Herd nickname was inspired by a Zane Grey novel and represents the power and energy of a stampeding buffalo herd, adopted in the 1930s.
276.iconicMoment: Hal Greer's outstanding career at Marshall in the 1950s, which launched him to an NBA Hall of Fame career with the Philadelphia 76ers and Syracuse Nationals.
276.funFact: Hal Greer was the first Black athlete to play at Marshall and went on to become one of the greatest shooting guards in NBA history, with his number retired by the 76ers.

277.blurb: West Virginia's basketball program dates to 1903 and the 'Mountaineers' name reflects the rugged Appalachian terrain of the state. WVU's greatest basketball achievement was reaching the 2010 Final Four under Bob Huggins, a proud West Virginia native who returned home after coaching at Cincinnati. Jerry West, widely considered one of the greatest basketball players ever, played at WVU from 1957-1960 and led the Mountaineers to the 1959 NCAA Championship game. His silhouette became the inspiration for the NBA logo.
277.mascotOrigin: The 'Mountaineers' name reflects West Virginia's Appalachian mountain terrain and the rugged spirit of the state's residents
277.iconicMoment: Jerry West leading West Virginia to the 1959 NCAA Championship game, averaging 32.2 points per game during the tournament run
277.funFact: Jerry West's silhouette became the inspiration for the NBA logo, and he remains the only player to win the NBA Finals MVP from the losing team (1969 Lakers)

278.blurb: Fresno State's basketball program began in 1921 in Fresno, California. The Bulldogs compete in the Mountain West Conference and enjoyed their greatest success under coaches Boyd Grant and Jerry Tarkanian. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
278.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted in the 1920s, representing tenacity and determination — a common mascot choice that has served the California Central Valley school well.
278.iconicMoment: Jerry Tarkanian's 2000-01 team earning an NCAA Tournament bid, bringing the legendary coach's star power and national attention to Fresno.
278.funFact: Fresno State's Save Mart Center seats over 15,000, making it one of the largest on-campus arenas in the Mountain West, and the Bulldogs' mascot Victor E. Bulldog is one of the most recognizable in California college sports.

279.blurb: The University of the Pacific in Stockton, California, competes in the West Coast Conference and has a basketball tradition that includes producing NBA players and making NCAA Tournament appearances. The Tigers have been competitive in the WCC for decades.
279.mascotOrigin: The Tigers mascot was adopted in 1923, chosen for the animal's strength and ferocity, giving the Stockton-based university a powerful athletic identity.
279.iconicMoment: The 2004-05 season, when Pacific went 27-3 and earned a #6 seed in the NCAA Tournament, the program's highest seed and one of its best seasons in history.
279.funFact: Pacific's Alex G. Spanos Center is named after the late NFL owner who was a major benefactor of the university, connecting the school to professional sports history.

282.blurb: Indiana State basketball became nationally famous in 1979 when Larry Bird led the Sycamores to an undefeated regular season and an NCAA Championship Game appearance against Magic Johnson and Michigan State. The program competes in the Missouri Valley Conference and has produced one of the greatest players in basketball history.
282.mascotOrigin: The Sycamore tree is abundant in Indiana, and the nickname was adopted in 1920 to reflect the natural landscape of Terre Haute and the surrounding Wabash Valley.
282.iconicMoment: Larry Bird leading Indiana State to the 1979 NCAA Championship Game against Magic Johnson and Michigan State, a matchup that drew the highest TV ratings in NCAA Tournament history.
282.funFact: Larry Bird averaged 30.3 points per game in the 1978-79 season and won the Naismith Award, yet he was originally drafted by the Boston Celtics a year before he finished at Indiana State.

284.blurb: Stonehill's basketball program dates to 1948, and the Easton, Massachusetts school made the transition from Division II to Division I in 2022. The Skyhawks joined the Northeast Conference and are one of the newest D-I programs in the country.
284.mascotOrigin: The Skyhawks name was adopted to represent ambition and soaring achievement, fitting for a school on the rise to Division I athletics.
284.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to Division I in 2022, joining the Northeast Conference and beginning a new era for the small Catholic college.
284.funFact: Stonehill is one of the newest Division I programs in the country, having made the jump from D-II in 2022, and is located just 20 miles south of Boston.

288.blurb: Lipscomb University in Nashville, Tennessee, competes in the ASUN Conference and made a breakthrough NCAA Tournament appearance in 2018 under coach Casey Alexander. The Bisons have grown from an NAIA program to a competitive Division I mid-major.
288.mascotOrigin: The Bisons (deliberately spelled with an "s" rather than the grammatically standard plural) mascot represents power and resilience, chosen to give the Nashville-based school a distinctive identity.
288.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first NCAA Tournament bid in 2018, defeating Florida Gulf Coast in the ASUN Tournament Championship to punch their ticket to the Big Dance.
288.funFact: Lipscomb is affiliated with the Churches of Christ and is located in Nashville's Green Hills neighborhood, making it one of the few Division I programs situated in a major music industry hub.

290.blurb: Georgia Southern's basketball program began in 1926 in Statesboro, Georgia. The Eagles compete in the Sun Belt Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances throughout its history.
290.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was adopted to represent the powerful bald eagles native to Georgia's coastal plains, fitting for a university in southeastern Georgia.
290.iconicMoment: Winning the 1992 Trans America Athletic Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's signature postseason achievements.
290.funFact: Georgia Southern's Hanner Fieldhouse was built in 1969 and is known for its unique design — the intimate, loud arena has made it one of the toughest road trips in the Sun Belt.

292.blurb: UTRGV's basketball program traces its roots to 1968 through predecessor institutions UT Pan American and UT Brownsville. The Edinburg, Texas school competes in the WAC and serves one of the most diverse student populations in the country.
292.mascotOrigin: The Vaqueros name honors the Mexican cowboys (vaqueros) who worked ranches along the Rio Grande Valley, reflecting the region's deep ranching heritage.
292.iconicMoment: Winning the 2023 WAC Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance as UTRGV.
292.funFact: UTRGV is one of the youngest university consolidations in the country, formed in 2015 by merging UT Pan American and UT Brownsville, and serves the heavily Hispanic Rio Grande Valley region.

294.blurb: Jacksonville University burst onto the national scene in 1970 when the Dolphins reached the NCAA Championship Game behind 7-foot-2 center Artis Gilmore. The private university in Jacksonville, Florida, competes in the ASUN Conference and has never quite replicated that magical 1970 run.
294.mascotOrigin: The Dolphins mascot was adopted to reflect Jacksonville's coastal location along the St. Johns River and its proximity to the Atlantic Ocean.
294.iconicMoment: The 1970 Cinderella run to the NCAA Championship Game, where Artis Gilmore and the Dolphins stunned the basketball world before falling to UCLA in the final.
294.funFact: Artis Gilmore averaged 24.3 points and 22.7 rebounds per game during the 1969-70 season, one of the greatest individual seasons in college basketball history.

295.blurb: Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, competes in the Sun Belt Conference and has a basketball tradition that includes several NCAA Tournament appearances. The Monarchs have been competitive in multiple conferences and represent the Hampton Roads region in Division I basketball.
295.mascotOrigin: The Monarchs nickname references Virginia's historical title as the "Old Dominion," a name given by King Charles II of England, representing the state's colonial heritage.
295.iconicMoment: The 2011 Colonial Athletic Association Tournament championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, a highlight for the Norfolk-based program.
295.funFact: Old Dominion shares Norfolk with Norfolk State, creating a unique local rivalry between a Division I program and an HBCU in the same city.

299.blurb: Long Beach State has a proud basketball tradition in the Big West Conference, including a 1970 Final Four appearance and years of competitive teams. The program has produced numerous NBA players and was once coached by Jerry Tarkanian and Lute Olson early in their legendary careers.
299.mascotOrigin: The school dropped the "49ers" nickname in 2022 and rebranded to simply "The Beach," embracing its Southern California coastal identity and Long Beach's beach culture.
299.iconicMoment: The 1970 Final Four appearance under Jerry Tarkanian, establishing Long Beach State as a serious basketball program and launching Tark's legendary coaching career.
299.funFact: Both Jerry Tarkanian and Lute Olson — two of the greatest coaches in college basketball history — got their starts at Long Beach State before moving on to UNLV and Arizona, respectively.

300.blurb: UC Irvine's basketball program began in 1965, and the Anteaters have become one of the Big West Conference's top programs. Under Russell Turner, UCI became a regular conference contender and NCAA Tournament participant.
300.mascotOrigin: Students chose "Anteaters" in 1965 as a joke — they wanted a mascot that was unusual and non-threatening, inspired by the comic strip B.C. The name stuck and became a beloved identity.
300.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 4 seed Kansas State 70-64 in the 2019 NCAA Tournament as a 13-seed, the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament victory.
300.funFact: UC Irvine's Anteater mascot was originally chosen as a joke by the student body in 1965, but it became so popular that it's now one of the most recognizable — and oddest — mascots in college sports.

301.blurb: San Diego's basketball program dates to 1948, and the private Catholic university competes in the West Coast Conference. The Toreros have occasionally challenged Gonzaga and other WCC powers for conference supremacy.
301.mascotOrigin: The Toreros name means "bullfighters" in Spanish, reflecting San Diego's deep connection to Spanish and Mexican culture and the city's proximity to the Mexican border.
301.iconicMoment: Earning an at-large NCAA Tournament bid in 2008 and reaching the first round, one of the few times a WCC team other than Gonzaga has earned an at-large bid.
301.funFact: San Diego's Jenny Craig Pavilion sits on a mesa overlooking Mission Bay and the Pacific Ocean, giving it one of the most beautiful arena settings in all of college basketball.

302.blurb: UC Davis' basketball program dates to 1912, and the Aggies made the jump from Division II to Division I in 2003. Competing in the Big West Conference, UC Davis has steadily grown into a competitive D-I program.
302.mascotOrigin: The Aggies name reflects UC Davis' origins as the agricultural branch of UC Berkeley — the school was founded as the University Farm in 1905.
302.iconicMoment: Winning the 2017 Big West Tournament to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament bid at the Division I level.
302.funFact: UC Davis is one of the top veterinary schools in the world, and its Aggies mascot, a mustang named Gunrock, reflects the university's deep agricultural and equestrian heritage.

304.blurb: Idaho State's basketball program began in 1923 in Pocatello, Idaho. The Bengals compete in the Big Sky Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program reached the NCAA Tournament several times in the 1950s-80s.
304.mascotOrigin: The Bengals nickname was adopted to represent the fierce Bengal tiger, giving the small-town Idaho university an exotic and powerful athletic identity.
304.iconicMoment: Making the 1977 NCAA Tournament and competing against UCLA in the first round, a marquee matchup for the small Big Sky program.
304.funFact: Idaho State's Reed Gym is one of the smallest Division I basketball venues in the country, and Pocatello's remote location in southeastern Idaho makes it one of the most isolated D-I campuses in America.

305.blurb: DePaul's basketball program was established in 1923 at the Chicago Catholic university founded in 1898 by the Vincentian Fathers. The 'Blue Demons' name combines the school's blue colors with a spirited moniker. Under legendary coach Ray Meyer, who coached for 42 seasons, DePaul became a national power, reaching the Final Four in 1943 and 1979. Meyer's son Joey continued the coaching dynasty. DePaul produced NBA great George Mikan, widely considered the first dominant big man in basketball history, and Mark Aguirre.
305.mascotOrigin: The 'Blue Demons' name combines the school's signature blue color with an energetic and fierce spirit; the mascot is named DIBS (DePaul Intercollegiate Blue Spirit)
305.iconicMoment: George Mikan's dominant career in the 1940s, revolutionizing the center position and leading DePaul to the 1945 NIT Championship
305.funFact: George Mikan was so dominant at DePaul that the NCAA introduced the goaltending rule largely because of him — he went on to become the NBA's first superstar with the Minneapolis Lakers

309.blurb: The University of Louisiana at Lafayette competes in the Sun Belt Conference and has built a solid basketball tradition in Cajun Country. The Ragin' Cajuns have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for their passionate fan base in Lafayette.
309.mascotOrigin: The Ragin' Cajuns name celebrates the Cajun heritage of southern Louisiana, with "Ragin'" added to convey the team's fiery competitive spirit.
309.iconicMoment: The 2005 Sun Belt Conference Tournament title and subsequent NCAA Tournament appearance, a high point for the program in the mid-2000s.
309.funFact: The Cajundome, which opened in 1984, is one of the largest arenas in the Sun Belt Conference and also hosts concerts, making it a cultural hub for Lafayette.

311.blurb: The University of Maine competes in America East Conference and has produced competitive basketball teams despite being one of the most geographically isolated Division I programs. The Black Bears have won multiple conference titles and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
311.mascotOrigin: The Black Bear mascot reflects Maine's wildlife and rugged wilderness identity — the black bear is abundant in the forests of northern New England.
311.iconicMoment: The 2003-04 season, when Maine won the America East Tournament and earned an NCAA Tournament berth, representing the conference on the national stage.
311.funFact: Maine's campus in Orono is one of the northernmost Division I basketball programs in the country, creating challenging travel logistics for opposing teams.

314.blurb: Iona College in New Rochelle, New York, has built a reputation as a mid-major powerhouse in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference. The program saw sustained success under Tim Cluess and later Rick Pitino, who coached at Iona from 2020-2023 and led the Gaels to two NCAA Tournament appearances.
314.mascotOrigin: The Gaels name refers to the Gaelic heritage of the Irish Christian Brothers who founded Iona College in 1940, honoring their Celtic roots.
314.iconicMoment: Rick Pitino coaching Iona to a 2023 NCAA Tournament win over UConn in the first round, proving the Hall of Famer could still compete at the highest level.
314.funFact: Iona has won more MAAC Tournament championships than any other school, making the Gaels the conference's most dominant postseason program.

315.blurb: Niagara University near Niagara Falls, New York, competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference and produced one of the greatest scorers in college basketball history. The Purple Eagles have a basketball tradition anchored by Calvin Murphy's legendary career in the late 1960s.
315.mascotOrigin: The Purple Eagles mascot combines the school's purple primary color with the bald eagle, a symbol of American strength, creating a distinctive and unique athletic identity.
315.iconicMoment: Calvin Murphy's incredible 1967-70 career, during which the 5-foot-9 guard averaged 33.1 points per game and became one of the most electrifying scorers in college basketball history.
315.funFact: Calvin Murphy scored 2,548 career points at Niagara despite standing just 5-foot-9, proving that size doesn't determine greatness — he went on to a Hall of Fame NBA career with the Houston Rockets.

322.blurb: Lafayette College in Easton, Pennsylvania, competes in the Patriot League and has a basketball tradition stretching back over a century. The Leopards have made occasional NCAA Tournament appearances and maintain a fierce rivalry with nearby Lehigh.
322.mascotOrigin: The Leopards nickname was adopted in the 1920s, chosen for the animal's quickness and ferocity, replacing the informal "Maroon" designation.
322.iconicMoment: The 1999 NCAA Tournament appearance, when Lafayette earned a Patriot League Tournament title and faced Ohio State in the first round of the Big Dance.
322.funFact: The Lafayette-Lehigh rivalry is one of the most-played rivalries in all of college sports, with the two schools separated by just 17 miles in eastern Pennsylvania.

324.blurb: Coastal Carolina's basketball program began in 1963 in Conway, South Carolina. The Chanticleers compete in the Sun Belt Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program moved to Division I in 1986 and has made several NCAA Tournament appearances.
324.mascotOrigin: The Chanticleers nickname comes from the proud rooster character Chauntecleer in Geoffrey Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, making it one of the most literary mascots in college sports.
324.iconicMoment: Winning the 1991 Big South Conference Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance, a milestone for the young D-I program.
324.funFact: Coastal Carolina is the only school in Division I with the Chanticleer mascot, and the campus in Conway is located just minutes from the Myrtle Beach boardwalk.

325.blurb: Cleveland State's basketball program began in 1929 and the Vikings compete in the Horizon League. The program is best known for one of the greatest Cinderella runs in NCAA Tournament history in 1986 under Kevin Mackey. The Vikings have won multiple Horizon League titles.
325.mascotOrigin: The Vikings nickname was adopted in 1929, reflecting Cleveland's Scandinavian immigrant heritage and the warrior spirit of Norse explorers.
325.iconicMoment: The 1986 NCAA Tournament Cinderella run as a No. 14 seed, defeating Indiana and St. Joseph's before falling to Navy in the Sweet Sixteen.
325.funFact: Cleveland State's 1986 run remains one of the greatest underdog stories in March Madness history — coach Kevin Mackey's team was so unknown that CBS announcers initially mispronounced players' names.

326.blurb: Texas State's basketball program dates to 1920, and the San Marcos school competes in the Sun Belt Conference. The Bobcats have been a steady Sun Belt competitor and play in one of the fastest-growing regions in the country.
326.mascotOrigin: The Bobcats name was adopted in 1926, chosen after the bobcats native to the Texas Hill Country surrounding the San Marcos campus.
326.iconicMoment: Winning the 2024 Sun Belt Conference Tournament title to earn an NCAA Tournament berth under coach Terrence Johnson.
326.funFact: Texas State's campus in San Marcos sits between Austin and San Antonio along the I-35 corridor, one of the fastest-growing regions in the United States, giving it a huge and expanding recruiting base.

328.blurb: Utah State's basketball program dates to 1902 at the land-grant university in Logan, Utah. Like many agricultural schools, the 'Aggies' name reflects the university's farming roots. The Dee Glen Smith Spectrum, known simply as 'The Spectrum,' is considered one of the most hostile environments in college basketball thanks to the famed student section 'The HURD.' Utah State has been a mid-major powerhouse, particularly under Stew Morrill, who won 402 games and led the Aggies to multiple NCAA Tournament appearances during his tenure from 1998-2015.
328.mascotOrigin: The 'Aggies' name reflects Utah State's founding as an agricultural college, common among land-grant universities
328.iconicMoment: The 2011 NCAA Tournament upset of fifth-seeded Kansas State, showcasing the Aggies' ability to compete with power-conference programs
328.funFact: The Spectrum's student section, known as 'The HURD' (an acronym for 'Hostile, Utah-bred, Rowdy, and Dedicated'), is regularly cited as one of the most intense student sections in the country

331.blurb: Eastern Washington's basketball program dates to 1901 in Cheney, Washington. The Eagles compete in the Big Sky Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program is known for its distinctive red basketball court.
331.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was adopted in the 1920s, representing the bald eagles common in the Pacific Northwest's forests and waterways.
331.iconicMoment: Upsetting Georgetown in the 2015 NCAA Tournament first round, behind Tyler Harvey's sharpshooting, giving the program its first tournament win.
331.funFact: Eastern Washington's Reese Court features a bright red basketball court — one of the most distinctive and polarizing playing surfaces in all of college basketball.

333.blurb: Alabama's basketball program was founded in 1913 but spent most of its history in the shadow of the Crimson Tide's legendary football program. The 'Crimson Tide' nickname was coined by a sportswriter after a 1907 football game played in muddy conditions. Under Nate Oats, Alabama has emerged as a national basketball power with a fast-paced, three-point-heavy style, reaching their first Final Four in 2024. The undefeated 1929-30 team (20-0) was retroactively recognized as national champions.
333.mascotOrigin: Sports editor Hugh Roberts coined 'Crimson Tide' after watching Alabama play in the mud in 1907; the elephant mascot Big Al emerged from a 1930 sportswriter's description of players as 'Red Elephants'
333.iconicMoment: Reaching the program's first-ever Final Four in 2024, defeating No. 1 North Carolina and Clemson en route
333.funFact: Big Al the elephant mascot can make appearances at weddings for a $400 fee, with a maximum appearance time of one hour

338.blurb: Kennesaw State is a relatively new Division I program, having transitioned from Division II in 2005. Located in the Atlanta suburb of Kennesaw, Georgia, the Owls compete in Conference USA and are still building their basketball tradition at the highest level.
338.mascotOrigin: The Owls mascot was chosen to represent wisdom and knowledge, aligning with the university's academic mission when it was founded as a junior college in 1963.
338.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first NCAA Tournament bid in 2023 by winning the ASUN Conference Tournament, marking a milestone for the young Division I program.
338.funFact: Kennesaw State is one of the youngest Division I basketball programs in the country, having only been competing at the top level since 2005.

339.blurb: Evansville's basketball program began in 1919 in Evansville, Indiana. The Purple Aces compete in the Missouri Valley Conference and won five Division II national championships in the 1950s-60s under coach Arad McCutchan. The program moved to Division I in 1977 but suffered a devastating plane crash in 1977 that killed the entire team.
339.mascotOrigin: The Purple Aces nickname was coined in the 1920s by a sportswriter who said the team 'had aces up their sleeves,' combined with the school's purple color.
339.iconicMoment: Defeating No. 1-ranked Kentucky 67-64 at Rupp Arena in November 2019, one of the biggest upsets in college basketball history.
339.funFact: On December 13, 1977, a plane carrying the entire Evansville basketball team crashed shortly after takeoff, killing all 29 passengers — the tragedy remains one of the darkest moments in college sports history.

344.blurb: Mississippi State competes in the SEC and has built a reputation for tough defensive basketball, particularly under coach Rick Ray and later Ben Howland. The Bulldogs have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and reached the Final Four in 1996 under Richard Williams.
344.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs mascot was adopted in 1905 when a student brought a bulldog to campus. The famous cowbell tradition, while more associated with football, extends to basketball fan culture as well.
344.iconicMoment: The 1996 Final Four appearance under coach Richard Williams, the program's deepest NCAA Tournament run, featuring Erick Dampier and Dontae' Jones.
344.funFact: Mississippi State's Humphrey Coliseum is known as "The Hump" and was once one of the toughest road venues in the SEC due to its intimate seating and loud fans.

349.blurb: Army's basketball program dates to 1903 at the United States Military Academy in West Point, New York. The Black Knights compete in the Patriot League and have a proud basketball tradition, especially in the mid-20th century. Bob Knight played at Army before launching his legendary coaching career at Indiana.
349.mascotOrigin: The Black Knights nickname references the black, gold, and gray uniforms worn by Army cadets, evoking the image of medieval armored knights riding into battle.
349.iconicMoment: The 1968-69 season when Army's Mike Krzyzewski played point guard, before he returned to West Point as head coach in 1975 and launched his Hall of Fame career.
349.funFact: Every cadet at West Point is required to play a sport, giving Army one of the most athletically engaged student bodies in Division I athletics.

350.blurb: UNC Wilmington's basketball program dates to 1948, and the coastal Carolina school competes in the Colonial Athletic Association. The Seahawks have been a regular CAA contender and occasional NCAA Tournament participant.
350.mascotOrigin: The Seahawks name reflects the school's coastal location in Wilmington, North Carolina, where osprey and other sea hawks are commonly seen along the Cape Fear coast.
350.iconicMoment: Winning the 2003 CAA Tournament and earning an NCAA Tournament berth, highlighted by Brett Blizzard's dominant play.
350.funFact: UNC Wilmington's campus is just miles from Wrightsville Beach, making it one of the closest Division I basketball programs to the Atlantic Ocean.

356.blurb: Illinois has been playing basketball since 1906 and ranks among the top 15 programs all-time in wins. The 'Fighting Illini' nickname first appeared in a 1911 newspaper article and references the Illiniwek confederation of Native American tribes. The 2005 team went 37-2, tying the record for most wins in a season, highlighted by a legendary 15-point comeback against Arizona in the Elite Eight. The 1989 'Flyin' Illini' under Lou Henson scored 100-plus points eight times, capturing the imagination of college basketball fans nationwide.
356.mascotOrigin: The 'Illini' name references the Illiniwek confederation; 'Fighting Illini' first appeared in a 1911 newspaper describing a basketball game against Purdue
356.iconicMoment: The 2005 Elite Eight comeback from 15 points down against Arizona with four minutes left, led by Luther Head and Deron Williams, to reach the Final Four
356.funFact: Despite being ranked No. 1 and undefeated, the 1943 'Whiz Kids' team opted not to play in the NCAA Tournament when three of its five starters were called to duty in World War II

357.blurb: Texas A&M-Corpus Christi's basketball program dates to 1973, and the coastal Texas school competes in the Southland Conference. The Islanders play on Ward Island, giving them one of the most unique campus settings in college basketball.
357.mascotOrigin: The Islanders name comes from the school's location on Ward Island in Corpus Christi Bay, surrounded by water on three sides.
357.iconicMoment: Winning the 2022 Southland Conference Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance since joining Division I.
357.funFact: Texas A&M-Corpus Christi's campus is located on its own island in Corpus Christi Bay, making it the only Division I program in the continental U.S. situated on an island.

399.blurb: UAlbany's basketball program dates to 1929, and the school made the transition from Division III to Division I in 1999. Competing in the America East Conference, the Great Danes quickly became competitive at the D-I level.
399.mascotOrigin: The Great Danes name was adopted in 1965, replacing "Pedagogues." The Great Dane breed was chosen for its size, strength, and regal bearing.
399.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2006 and 2007 NCAA Tournaments in consecutive years, marking the program's emergence as a legitimate Division I contender.
399.funFact: UAlbany made a remarkably quick transition from Division III to Division I relevance, earning NCAA Tournament bids within just a few years of becoming eligible for postseason play.

526.blurb: Florida Gulf Coast's basketball program began in 2002 at the young university in Fort Myers, Florida. FGCU became a national sensation in 2013 with 'Dunk City,' one of the most electrifying NCAA Tournament debuts in history. The Eagles compete in the ASUN Conference.
526.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was chosen to represent the bald eagles and other birds of prey common in Southwest Florida's coastal ecosystem.
526.iconicMoment: The 2013 'Dunk City' NCAA Tournament run as a 15-seed, defeating Georgetown and San Diego State with a high-flying, alley-oop-heavy style that captivated the nation.
526.funFact: FGCU's 2013 NCAA Tournament debut was so electrifying that 'Dunk City' became a nationally trademarked phrase, and the Eagles became the first 15-seed to reach the Sweet Sixteen.

2000.blurb: Abilene Christian's basketball program began in 1919 at the small Church of Christ-affiliated university in West Texas. The Wildcats spent decades as a Division II power, winning the NAIA national championship in 1969, before transitioning to Division I in 2013. ACU joined the WAC and later the Western Athletic Conference before moving to future conference realignments.
2000.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats nickname was adopted in the 1920s, a common choice for schools in the region reflecting the fierce, competitive spirit the university wanted to project.
2000.iconicMoment: Defeating third-seeded Texas 53-52 in the first round of the 2021 NCAA Tournament, the program's first-ever Division I tournament win.
2000.funFact: ACU's Moody Coliseum was renovated in 2019 and features a video board shaped like a wildcat paw print.

2005.blurb: Air Force's basketball program began in 1956, just one year after the United States Air Force Academy was established in Colorado Springs. The Falcons compete in the Mountain West Conference and have made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has produced NBA players like Bob Beckel and Reggie Jones.
2005.mascotOrigin: The Falcons name was chosen by the first class of cadets in 1955 through a vote, selecting the falcon as a symbol of speed, courage, and keen vision — traits prized by fighter pilots.
2005.iconicMoment: Reaching the Sweet Sixteen of the 2006 NIT after upsetting several higher-seeded teams, one of the program's best postseason runs.
2005.funFact: Air Force Academy cadets are required to attend home games as part of their military training, ensuring Clune Arena is always packed with a disciplined, loud crowd.

2006.blurb: Akron's basketball program dates to 1901 and has been a Mid-American Conference fixture since 1992. The Zips have won multiple MAC championships and made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program enjoyed strong success under Keith Dambrot in the 2010s.
2006.mascotOrigin: The 'Zips' nickname derives from 'Zippers,' a name chosen in 1926 after a popular rubber overshoe produced in Akron, the 'Rubber Capital of the World.'
2006.iconicMoment: Winning the 2009 MAC Tournament championship and earning an NCAA Tournament bid, then nearly upsetting Gonzaga in the first round.
2006.funFact: Akron's James A. Rhodes Arena is known as the 'JAR' and sits adjacent to the campus's polymer science buildings, fitting for a school in the rubber industry capital.

2010.blurb: Alabama A&M's basketball program began in 1928 at the historically Black university in Huntsville, Alabama. The Bulldogs compete in the SWAC and have won multiple conference championships. The program has been a consistent contender in the SWAC throughout its history.
2010.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted to represent toughness and tenacity, a common mascot among historically Black colleges and universities.
2010.iconicMoment: Winning the 2004 SWAC Tournament championship to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance in the modern era.
2010.funFact: Alabama A&M's campus in Huntsville sits near NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center, making it one of the few basketball programs located in a major aerospace hub.

2011.blurb: Alabama State's basketball program started around 1930 at the HBCU in Montgomery, Alabama. The Hornets compete in the SWAC and have won multiple conference titles. The program has produced several professional players and remains a proud member of the HBCU basketball tradition.
2011.mascotOrigin: The Hornets nickname reflects the aggressive, stinging defense the teams were known for, adopted in the early decades of the university's athletic program.
2011.iconicMoment: Winning the 2009 SWAC Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, the program's most prominent national stage appearance.
2011.funFact: Alabama State's Dunn-Oliver Acadome is one of the largest on-campus arenas in the SWAC, seating over 7,000 fans.

2016.blurb: Alcorn State's basketball program dates to around 1930 at the oldest public HBCU in Mississippi. The Braves compete in the SWAC and have had periods of strong conference play. Davey Whitney coached the program for over three decades and built it into a SWAC contender.
2016.mascotOrigin: The Braves nickname was chosen to represent courage and warrior spirit, reflecting the university's fighting determination as a historically Black institution.
2016.iconicMoment: Legendary coach Davey Whitney leading the Braves to the 1980 NCAA Tournament, where they nearly upset South Alabama in the first round.
2016.funFact: Alcorn State's campus in Lorman, Mississippi is one of the most rural Division I settings in America, located in a town with a population under 100.

2026.blurb: Appalachian State's basketball program began in 1919 in Boone, North Carolina, nestled in the Blue Ridge Mountains. The Mountaineers compete in the Sun Belt Conference and have won multiple conference titles. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances throughout its history.
2026.mascotOrigin: The Mountaineers nickname is a natural fit for a university located at over 3,300 feet elevation in the Appalachian Mountains of western North Carolina.
2026.iconicMoment: Winning the 2000 Southern Conference Tournament championship to earn an NCAA Tournament bid after a dominant conference season.
2026.funFact: Boone, North Carolina sits at 3,333 feet elevation, making App State's Holmes Convocation Center one of the highest-altitude Division I basketball venues east of the Mississippi.

2029.blurb: Arkansas-Pine Bluff's basketball program dates to around 1930 at the HBCU in Pine Bluff, Arkansas. The Golden Lions compete in the SWAC and have produced several professional players. The program has made occasional postseason appearances.
2029.mascotOrigin: The Golden Lions nickname was chosen to represent strength and pride, with the golden color reflecting the university's school colors.
2029.iconicMoment: Earning a bid to the 2009 NCAA Tournament as the SWAC's automatic qualifier, marking one of the program's biggest national moments.
2029.funFact: UAPB's campus sits along the Arkansas River, and the university is known as one of the top HBCU marching band schools in the country, with the band often stealing the show at basketball games.

2031.blurb: The University of Arkansas at Little Rock (now UA Little Rock) competes in the Ohio Valley Conference and has a history of mid-major success. The Trojans are best remembered for their 1986 NCAA Tournament upset and have produced competitive teams in multiple conferences.
2031.mascotOrigin: The Trojans nickname was adopted to evoke the warriors of ancient Troy, representing fighting spirit and determination for the Little Rock-based university.
2031.iconicMoment: The 2016 NCAA Tournament first-round double-overtime upset of #5 seed Purdue, 85-83, one of the most thrilling games of that tournament.
2031.funFact: Little Rock's Jack Stephens Center is named after Jackson T. Stephens, a billionaire financier and one of Arkansas's most prominent businessmen.

2032.blurb: Arkansas State's basketball program started in 1914 in Jonesboro, Arkansas. The Red Wolves compete in the Sun Belt Conference and have won multiple conference titles. The program has made occasional NCAA Tournament appearances and produced a few professional players.
2032.mascotOrigin: The school changed its nickname from 'Indians' to 'Red Wolves' in 2008, choosing the red wolf because it is a native endangered species of the Arkansas region.
2032.iconicMoment: Winning the 1999 Sun Belt Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's most exciting postseason runs.
2032.funFact: Arkansas State's First National Bank Arena is one of the largest on-campus arenas in the Sun Belt, and the university's change to 'Red Wolves' made it the first D-I school with that mascot.

2046.blurb: Austin Peay's basketball program started around 1930 in Clarksville, Tennessee. The Governors compete in the ASUN Conference (formerly Ohio Valley) and are known for passionate fan support. The program gained national attention for its spirited NCAA Tournament appearances.
2046.mascotOrigin: The Governors nickname honors Austin Peay, the 40th Governor of Tennessee, for whom the university was named when it was established in 1927.
2046.iconicMoment: The 2016 NCAA Tournament first-round game against Kansas, where the 'Let's Go Peay' chants went viral and the Governors became a beloved underdog on the national stage.
2046.funFact: Austin Peay's chant 'Let's Go Peay' became a viral sensation during March Madness, with the double meaning making it one of the most famous cheers in college basketball.

2050.blurb: Ball State's basketball program began in 1920 in Muncie, Indiana, the heartland of Hoosier basketball. The Cardinals compete in the Mid-American Conference and have made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program produced NBA player Bonzi Wells.
2050.mascotOrigin: The Cardinals nickname was adopted in 1927, chosen because the cardinal is Indiana's state bird, fitting for a university deeply rooted in Indiana culture.
2050.iconicMoment: Reaching the Sweet Sixteen of the 1990 NCAA Tournament under coach Dick Hunsaker, the deepest tournament run in program history.
2050.funFact: Ball State is named after the Ball family, who made their fortune manufacturing Ball mason jars in Muncie, and the university was originally called Indiana State Normal School Eastern Division.

2057.blurb: Belmont's basketball program started in 1951 at the Nashville university and became a mid-major powerhouse under Rick Byrd, who coached from 1986 to 2019. The Bruins have made numerous NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for their uptempo, high-scoring style of play. Belmont joined the Missouri Valley Conference in 2022.
2057.mascotOrigin: The Bruins nickname was adopted to project strength and ferocity, a nod to the grizzly bear's powerful reputation.
2057.iconicMoment: Nearly upsetting Duke in the 2008 NCAA Tournament first round, losing 71-70 in a game that announced Belmont as a legitimate mid-major threat.
