2057.funFact: Belmont's Curb Event Center sits on what was once the Belmont Mansion estate, a historic antebellum property in the heart of Nashville's Music Row district.

2065.blurb: Bethune-Cookman's basketball program dates to around 1930 at the HBCU in Daytona Beach, Florida, founded by the legendary educator Mary McLeod Bethune. The Wildcats compete in the SWAC and have won multiple conference championships. The program carries the proud legacy of one of America's most important historically Black institutions.
2065.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats nickname was chosen to represent the fierce, competitive spirit of the university's athletic teams.
2065.iconicMoment: Winning the 2007 MEAC Tournament championship to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, one of the program's signature achievements.
2065.funFact: Bethune-Cookman was founded in 1904 by Mary McLeod Bethune with just $1.50 and five students, making its rise to Division I athletics one of the most inspiring stories in college sports.

2066.blurb: Binghamton's basketball program began in 1946 at the State University of New York campus in upstate New York. The Bearcats compete in the America East Conference and made a dramatic rise to prominence in the late 2000s under coach Kevin Broadus. The program earned its first NCAA Tournament bid in 2009.
2066.mascotOrigin: The Bearcats nickname was chosen in the 1940s, combining the ferocity of a bear with the agility of a cat to create a unique mascot identity.
2066.iconicMoment: Winning the 2009 America East Tournament to earn the program's first and only NCAA Tournament appearance, a landmark moment for the university.
2066.funFact: Binghamton's campus is located in Vestal, New York — not actually in the city of Binghamton — and the Events Center is known for its intimate, loud atmosphere despite modest seating capacity.

2083.blurb: Bucknell's basketball program dates to 1896 at the private university in Lewisburg, Pennsylvania. The Bison compete in the Patriot League and have produced some of the conference's most memorable NCAA Tournament moments. The program has won multiple Patriot League titles.
2083.mascotOrigin: The Bison nickname was adopted in 1923, replacing 'Orange and Blue,' chosen because the American bison represents strength and the rugged spirit of central Pennsylvania.
2083.iconicMoment: Upsetting third-seeded Kansas 64-63 in the first round of the 2005 NCAA Tournament, one of the biggest upsets in March Madness history.
2083.funFact: Bucknell's Sojka Pavilion was named after former president Gary Sojka, and the 2005 Kansas upset was the first of back-to-back first-round NCAA wins — they also beat Arkansas in 2006.

2084.blurb: Buffalo's basketball program started in 1915 and the Bulls compete in the Mid-American Conference. The program rose to national prominence under Bobby Hurley and then Nate Oats in the 2010s, making three NCAA Tournament appearances in four years. Buffalo's uptempo style made them a dangerous tournament team.
2084.mascotOrigin: The Bulls nickname was adopted in 1931, a nod to the city of Buffalo's name and the powerful, charging nature of the animal.
2084.iconicMoment: Demolishing fourth-seeded Arizona 89-68 in the first round of the 2018 NCAA Tournament, one of the most lopsided upsets in tournament history.
2084.funFact: Buffalo's Alumni Arena seats over 6,100 and was renovated for basketball in 2012; the team's 2018 and 2019 tournament appearances were the first time a MAC team went dancing in consecutive years since the early 2000s.

2086.blurb: Butler University, founded in 1855 in Indianapolis, has played basketball since 1893. The 'Bulldogs' name was adopted in the early 1900s, fitting the school's tenacious spirit. Butler became college basketball's ultimate underdog story with back-to-back NCAA Championship game appearances in 2010 and 2011 under Brad Stevens, who later became head coach of the Boston Celtics. Gordon Hayward's half-court heave that nearly won the 2010 title against Duke remains one of the most dramatic shots in tournament history. Historic Hinkle Fieldhouse, a National Historic Landmark, served as the inspiration for the movie 'Hoosiers.'
2086.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in the early 1900s; Butler's live bulldog mascot Blue has been a beloved game-day presence
2086.iconicMoment: Back-to-back NCAA Championship game appearances in 2010 and 2011, including Gordon Hayward's near-miss half-court shot against Duke in the 2010 final
2086.funFact: Hinkle Fieldhouse, Butler's home court since 1928, is a National Historic Landmark and served as the inspiration for the gymnasium in the movie 'Hoosiers'

2097.blurb: Campbell's basketball program started in 1926 at the Baptist university in Buies Creek, North Carolina. The Fighting Camels compete in the CAA (formerly Big South) and have produced occasional conference success. The program is known for its unique mascot.
2097.mascotOrigin: The Fighting Camels nickname dates to the 1930s when students chose it partly as a humorous nod to the nearby town of Cameron and partly because camels represent endurance and resilience.
2097.iconicMoment: Winning the 1992 Big South Conference Tournament to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament appearance.
2097.funFact: Campbell has one of the most distinctive mascot names in college sports — the Fighting Camels — and the tiny town of Buies Creek has a population of about 3,000, making it one of the smallest D-I college towns.

2099.blurb: Canisius' basketball program dates to 1905 at the Jesuit college in Buffalo, New York. The Golden Griffins compete in the MAAC and have a long basketball tradition, including several NIT and NCAA Tournament appearances in the mid-20th century. The program was dominant in the 1950s.
2099.mascotOrigin: The Golden Griffins nickname (changed from 'Golden Griffs' officially) references the griffin, a mythical creature symbolizing courage and vigilance, fitting the Jesuit educational tradition.
2099.iconicMoment: The dominant 1955-57 era when Canisius made three consecutive NIT appearances and established itself as a northeastern basketball power.
2099.funFact: Canisius' Koessler Athletic Center is located on Main Street in Buffalo, and the school was named after Saint Peter Canisius, a Jesuit theologian who was one of the first Jesuits.

2110.blurb: Central Arkansas' basketball program began around 1920 in Conway, Arkansas. The Bears transitioned from Division II to Division I in 2006 and joined the ASUN Conference. UCA was a Division II power before making the jump, including an NAIA championship appearance.
2110.mascotOrigin: The Bears nickname was adopted in the early days of the university, representing the strength and determination associated with the black bears native to Arkansas.
2110.iconicMoment: Scottie Pippen's collegiate career at Central Arkansas in the mid-1980s, where the future NBA legend developed from a walk-on manager into an All-Conference player.
2110.funFact: NBA legend Scottie Pippen played at Central Arkansas, walking on as a freshman equipment manager before growing six inches and becoming a starter — one of the greatest development stories in basketball history.

2115.blurb: Central Connecticut's basketball program began around 1930 in New Britain, Connecticut. The Blue Devils compete in the NEC and have won multiple conference championships. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances as the NEC's automatic qualifier.
2115.mascotOrigin: The Blue Devils nickname was adopted in the early days of the athletics program, with the blue reflecting the school's colors and the 'Devils' adding a fierce competitive edge.
2115.iconicMoment: Winning the 2007 NEC Tournament championship to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's proudest moments on the national stage.
2115.funFact: Central Connecticut's Detrick Gymnasium is one of the most intimate venues in Division I basketball, and New Britain is known as the 'Hardware City' for its manufacturing history.

2116.blurb: UCF's basketball program began in the late 1960s before the school even had a nickname — the original proposed mascot was the 'Citronaut,' a bizarre orange-astronaut hybrid honoring Central Florida's citrus and space industries. Students eventually chose 'Knights' in 1970, allied with the university seal's Pegasus symbol. The program gained national attention when 7-foot-6 Tacko Fall, a Senegalese player who maintained a 4.0 high school GPA and scored in the 95th percentile on the SAT, became one of college basketball's most beloved figures from 2016-2019.
2116.mascotOrigin: The original mascot was the 'Citronaut' — an orange with an astronaut helmet; students voted for 'Knights' in 1970, connected to the Pegasus on the university seal
2116.iconicMoment: Tacko Fall's 2019 NCAA Tournament appearance, bringing national attention to UCF basketball as the 7-foot-6 center nearly led an upset of Duke
2116.funFact: UCF's original mascot proposal was the 'Citronaut' — a half-orange, half-astronaut creature honoring the area's citrus and space industries — which was rejected by students but has become a beloved retro symbol

2117.blurb: Central Michigan's basketball program dates to 1904 in Mount Pleasant, Michigan. The Chippewas compete in the Mid-American Conference and have won multiple MAC championships. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances and produced NBA player Dan Majerle.
2117.mascotOrigin: The Chippewas name honors the Saginaw Chippewa Indian Tribe, whose reservation is adjacent to the campus — the tribe has formally endorsed the use of the name.
2117.iconicMoment: Dan Majerle's dominant career in the mid-1980s, when 'Thunder Dan' became a MAC legend before his celebrated NBA career with the Phoenix Suns.
2117.funFact: Central Michigan is one of the few schools with a Native American nickname that has the explicit written endorsement of the local tribe, the Saginaw Chippewa Indian Tribe of Michigan.

2127.blurb: Charleston Southern's basketball program began in 1965 at the Baptist university in North Charleston, South Carolina. The Buccaneers compete in the Big South Conference and have won multiple conference titles. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances as the Big South's automatic qualifier.
2127.mascotOrigin: The Buccaneers nickname references Charleston's rich pirate history — the city was a famous haven for pirates like Blackbeard and Stede Bonnet in the early 18th century.
2127.iconicMoment: Winning the 2016 Big South Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, one of the program's biggest moments on the national stage.
2127.funFact: Charleston Southern's campus is located near the site where Blackbeard the pirate blockaded Charleston Harbor in 1718, making the Buccaneers nickname historically apt.

2130.blurb: Chicago State's basketball program started in 1967 on the far south side of Chicago. The Cougars have competed in several conferences and have faced significant challenges with funding and enrollment. Despite struggles, the program has maintained Division I status and joined the NEC.
2130.mascotOrigin: The Cougars nickname was chosen to represent speed and ferocity, giving the urban commuter campus a strong athletic identity.
2130.iconicMoment: Competing in the NCAA's First Four in Dayton as the NEC's automatic qualifier, giving the program a national television moment despite being heavy underdogs.
2130.funFact: Chicago State's campus has one of the most unique Division I settings — located on the city's south side near the historic Pullman neighborhood, a designated National Monument.

2132.blurb: Cincinnati's basketball program dates to 1901 and produced one of the sport's greatest dynasties in the early 1960s. The 'Bearcats' name was adopted in 1914 when a cheerleader combined 'bear' and 'cats' after a cartoon of a ferocious bear-cat hybrid. Under Ed Jucker, Cincinnati won back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1961 and 1962, becoming the last team to win consecutive titles until Duke in 1991-92. The program also featured Oscar Robertson, widely considered one of the greatest players in basketball history, who averaged 33.8 points per game during his college career.
2132.mascotOrigin: Adopted in 1914 when cheerleader Norman 'Pat' Lyon combined 'bear' and 'cats' after a cartoon of a ferocious bear-cat hybrid appeared in the school newspaper
2132.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1961 and 1962 under coach Ed Jucker, the last team to repeat until Duke thirty years later
2132.funFact: Oscar Robertson, 'The Big O,' averaged 33.8 points per game during his three-year varsity career at Cincinnati (1957-1960) and is widely considered one of the five greatest basketball players ever

2142.blurb: Colgate's basketball program dates to 1890 at the private university in Hamilton, New York. The Raiders compete in the Patriot League and have won multiple conference championships. The program has enjoyed a resurgence in recent years with several NCAA Tournament appearances.
2142.mascotOrigin: The Raiders nickname was adopted in the 1930s, replacing 'Maroon,' and represents the aggressive, attacking spirit of the university's athletic teams.
2142.iconicMoment: Earning back-to-back NCAA Tournament bids in 2019 and later years under coach Matt Langel, establishing Colgate as a Patriot League powerhouse.
2142.funFact: Colgate's Cotterell Court at Reid Athletic Center seats only 1,500 fans in tiny Hamilton, New York (population 4,000), making it one of the most remote and intimate D-I venues in the country.

2154.blurb: Coppin State's basketball program started around 1938 at the HBCU in Baltimore, Maryland. The Eagles compete in the MEAC and are best remembered for their stunning 1997 NCAA Tournament upset. Coach Fang Mitchell built the program into a MEAC power in the 1990s.
2154.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was chosen to represent the soaring ambition and vision of the university, named after Fanny Jackson Coppin, a pioneering African American educator.
2154.iconicMoment: The stunning 78-65 upset of second-seeded South Carolina in the first round of the 1997 NCAA Tournament as a No. 15 seed, one of the most shocking upsets in March Madness history.
2154.funFact: Coppin State's 1997 upset of South Carolina was just the fourth time a No. 15 seed had beaten a No. 2 seed in NCAA Tournament history, and coach Fang Mitchell became a folk hero in Baltimore.

2166.blurb: Davidson's basketball program began in 1909 at the small liberal arts college in Davidson, North Carolina. The Wildcats are best known for Stephen Curry's transcendent run in the 2008 NCAA Tournament and Lefty Driesell's coaching tenure in the 1960s. Davidson competes in the Atlantic 10 and consistently punches above its weight.
2166.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, representing the fierce, scrappy nature of the small school's athletic teams.
2166.iconicMoment: Stephen Curry's 2008 NCAA Tournament run, scoring 40 points against Gonzaga and leading the 10th-seeded Wildcats to the Elite Eight, captivating the nation.
2166.funFact: Davidson's enrollment is only about 2,000 students, making it the smallest school to reach the Elite Eight in the modern NCAA Tournament era — and Curry's No. 30 jersey is retired there.

2168.blurb: Dayton's basketball program dates to 1903 at the Catholic university founded by the Society of Mary in 1850. The 'Flyers' nickname honors the Wright Brothers, who conducted their aviation experiments in Dayton, Ohio. The program won the 1962 NIT Championship and reached the NCAA Championship game in 1967. Dayton is also known as the permanent host of the NCAA Tournament's First Four games at UD Arena, and the passionate Flyer faithful have helped create one of the most electric atmospheres in college basketball.
2168.mascotOrigin: The 'Flyers' nickname honors Wilbur and Orville Wright, the aviation pioneers who conducted their flying experiments in Dayton, Ohio
2168.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1967 NCAA Championship game, the highest point in Flyers basketball history
2168.funFact: Dayton has been the permanent host of the NCAA Tournament's First Four games since 2001, making UD Arena one of the most important venues in March Madness history

2169.blurb: Delaware State's basketball program began in 1947 at the HBCU in Dover, Delaware. The Hornets compete in the MEAC and have won multiple conference championships. The program has represented the MEAC in the NCAA Tournament on several occasions.
2169.mascotOrigin: The Hornets nickname was chosen to represent the aggressive, stinging competitiveness the university wanted its athletic teams to embody.
2169.iconicMoment: Winning the 2005 MEAC Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's signature moments.
2169.funFact: Delaware State's Memorial Hall is one of the smaller Division I arenas in the country, creating a uniquely intimate atmosphere for MEAC basketball.

2172.blurb: Denver's basketball program dates to 1904 at the private university in Colorado. The Pioneers compete in the Summit League and have had success in multiple conferences over the decades. The program enjoyed strong play in the 1950s-60s and has rebuilt in the modern era.
2172.mascotOrigin: The Pioneers nickname honors the settlers and explorers who traveled westward to Colorado during the Pike's Peak Gold Rush and the expansion of the American frontier.
2172.iconicMoment: The program's run to the 1960 NIT semifinals, which stood as one of the highlights of Denver basketball for decades.
2172.funFact: Denver's Magness Arena is named after cable television pioneer Bob Magness and sits at over 5,000 feet elevation, making visiting teams deal with the altitude disadvantage.

2174.blurb: Detroit Mercy's basketball program dates to 1897 at the Jesuit university in Detroit. The Titans compete in the Horizon League and have a rich basketball tradition, including producing multiple NBA players. The program was especially strong in the 1960s-70s under coaches Bob Calihan and Dick Vitale.
2174.mascotOrigin: The Titans nickname was adopted in 1964, reflecting the powerful industrial might of Detroit — the Motor City — and the larger-than-life spirit of the city.
2174.iconicMoment: Dick Vitale coaching the Titans to a 21-win season in 1977 before being hired by the Detroit Pistons, launching his path to becoming America's most famous basketball broadcaster.
2174.funFact: Dick Vitale, the legendary ESPN broadcaster known for his 'Awesome, baby!' catchphrase, was the head coach at Detroit from 1973 to 1977 before his brief NBA coaching stint.

2182.blurb: Drexel's basketball program dates to 1894 at the university in Philadelphia. The Dragons compete in the CAA and have made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has been a competitive force in Philadelphia's rich basketball landscape.
2182.mascotOrigin: The Dragons nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, with the mythical dragon representing power and the fiery spirit of the university's competitive teams.
2182.iconicMoment: Winning the 2021 CAA Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance in 25 years, a breakthrough moment for the Philadelphia school.
2182.funFact: Drexel's Daskalakis Athletic Center is located in the University City neighborhood of Philadelphia, just blocks from Penn's Palestra — one of basketball's most historic venues.

2184.blurb: Duquesne's basketball program dates to 1914 at the Catholic university in Pittsburgh. The Dukes were a national power in the 1930s-50s, winning the 1955 NIT Championship and producing NBA players like Sihugo Green and Dick Ricketts. The program competes in the Atlantic 10.
2184.mascotOrigin: The Dukes nickname honors the French Marquis de Duquesne, the colonial governor for whom the original Fort Duquesne (later Pittsburgh) was named.
2184.iconicMoment: Winning the 1955 NIT Championship, when the NIT was still considered one of the premier postseason tournaments in college basketball.
2184.funFact: Duquesne's campus sits on a bluff overlooking downtown Pittsburgh, and the university's A.J. Palumbo Center has one of the most dramatic urban backdrops of any D-I arena.

2193.blurb: East Tennessee State's basketball program began in 1919 in Johnson City, Tennessee. The Buccaneers compete in the Southern Conference and have won multiple conference championships. The program enjoyed great success in the late 1980s and early 1990s under coach Les Robinson and Alan LeForce.
2193.mascotOrigin: The Buccaneers nickname was adopted in the 1930s, referencing the pirates and adventurers of Tennessee's frontier history and the Appalachian region's independent spirit.
2193.iconicMoment: The 1992 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of Arizona under coach Alan LeForce, one of the program's most thrilling victories on the national stage.
2193.funFact: ETSU's Freedom Hall is located in the Appalachian Highlands and Johnson City's elevation of over 1,600 feet gives visiting teams a subtle altitude adjustment challenge.

2197.blurb: Eastern Illinois' basketball program began in 1909 in Charleston, Illinois. The Panthers compete in the OVC and have produced one of the greatest NBA players of all time — Kevin Duckworth. The program has been a competitive mid-major throughout its history.
2197.mascotOrigin: The Panthers nickname was adopted in the 1930s, representing the fierce and agile nature of the panther, chosen to give the university a strong athletic identity.
2197.iconicMoment: Kevin Duckworth's dominant college career in the mid-1980s before becoming a two-time NBA All-Star with the Portland Trail Blazers.
2197.funFact: Eastern Illinois' Lantz Arena is located in Charleston, Illinois — not to be confused with Charleston, South Carolina — and the town is also home to Lincoln Log Cabin State Historic Site.

2198.blurb: Eastern Kentucky's basketball program dates to 1909 in Richmond, Kentucky. The Colonels compete in the ASUN Conference (formerly OVC) and have won multiple conference championships. The program has a rich basketball tradition befitting the bluegrass state.
2198.mascotOrigin: The Colonels nickname honors the Kentucky Colonel, an honorary title bestowed by the Commonwealth of Kentucky, representing Southern gentility and pride.
2198.iconicMoment: Making the 2005 NCAA Tournament and competing against Kentucky in the first round, a David-vs-Goliath matchup within the same state.
2198.funFact: Eastern Kentucky's campus in Richmond is just 26 miles south of the University of Kentucky in Lexington, making it one of the closest Division I programs to a traditional blueblood.

2199.blurb: Eastern Michigan's basketball program dates to 1899 in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The Eagles compete in the Mid-American Conference and have made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program produced NBA players like George Gervin and Kennedy McIntosh.
2199.mascotOrigin: The Eagles nickname was adopted in 1991, replacing the previous 'Hurons' name. The bald eagle was chosen as a symbol of American strength and freedom.
2199.iconicMoment: George Gervin's brief but brilliant tenure at Eastern Michigan in the early 1970s before becoming 'The Iceman' and one of the greatest scorers in NBA history.
2199.funFact: George Gervin, the NBA legend known as 'The Iceman,' played at Eastern Michigan for two seasons before jumping to the ABA, where his scoring artistry made him one of basketball's all-time greats.

2210.blurb: Elon's basketball program began in 1928 in Elon, North Carolina. The Phoenix compete in the CAA and have steadily risen in competitiveness since moving to Division I in 1999. The program has become a regular contender in the CAA.
2210.mascotOrigin: The Phoenix nickname was adopted in 1999 when Elon moved to Division I, symbolizing the mythological bird reborn from ashes — fitting because the campus was rebuilt after a devastating 1923 fire.
2210.iconicMoment: The 2022 CAA season where Elon emerged as a serious conference contender, signaling the program's arrival as a competitive D-I force.
2210.funFact: Elon University's campus was rebuilt after a catastrophic 1923 fire destroyed much of the original buildings, making the Phoenix mascot a deeply meaningful symbol of the school's resilience.

2217.blurb: Fairfield's basketball program began in 1948 at the Jesuit university in Fairfield, Connecticut. The Stags compete in the MAAC and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has been a consistent MAAC contender over the decades.
2217.mascotOrigin: The Stags nickname represents the male deer, chosen to symbolize grace, speed, and strength — fitting the school's competitive aspirations.
2217.iconicMoment: Winning the 2022 MAAC Tournament championship to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, ending a long tournament drought for the program.
2217.funFact: Fairfield's campus is located in one of the wealthiest counties in America — Fairfield County, Connecticut — and Alumni Hall provides an intimate setting for MAAC basketball.

2226.blurb: Florida Atlantic's basketball program started in 1989 and the Owls compete in the AAC (formerly Conference USA). The program gained national fame with its 2023 Final Four run, one of the most improbable Cinderella stories in NCAA Tournament history. FAU has rapidly risen in the college basketball landscape.
2226.mascotOrigin: The Owls nickname was chosen because the burrowing owl is native to the FAU campus in Boca Raton, Florida, and has been a protected species on the university grounds.
2226.iconicMoment: The 2023 Final Four run as a No. 9 seed under coach Dusty May, defeating Memphis, FDU, Tennessee, and Kansas State before falling to San Diego State in the semifinal.
2226.funFact: FAU's campus in Boca Raton is home to a protected colony of burrowing owls that nest in the ground throughout campus — students are careful not to disturb the real-life mascots.

2229.blurb: Florida International's basketball program began in 1972 at the public university in Miami. The Panthers compete in Conference USA and have made occasional NCAA and NIT appearances. The program has steadily grown alongside one of the largest universities in Florida.
2229.mascotOrigin: The Panthers nickname references the Florida panther, an endangered big cat native to South Florida and the official state animal of Florida.
2229.iconicMoment: Isiah Thomas (not the NBA legend) leading FIU to a competitive Sun Belt Conference era, building the program's credibility.
2229.funFact: FIU's campus in University Park, Miami, is one of the largest in the country, and the university's enrollment exceeds 55,000 students, making it one of the biggest schools in Division I.

2230.blurb: Fordham's basketball program dates to 1903 at the Jesuit university in the Bronx, New York. The Rams compete in the Atlantic 10 and enjoyed their greatest success in the 1950s-70s, including NIT appearances and producing NBA talent. The program carries the tradition of New York City basketball.
2230.mascotOrigin: The Rams nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, chosen to represent the powerful, charging nature of the male sheep and the university's fighting spirit.
2230.iconicMoment: The 1971 season when Fordham reached the NCAA Tournament and gave a strong account of itself, led by standout players from the New York City basketball scene.
2230.funFact: Fordham's Rose Hill campus in the Bronx has been featured in numerous films and TV shows, and the school's original Rose Hill Gym is one of the most historic basketball venues in New York City.

2239.blurb: Cal State Fullerton's basketball program began in 1957 and the Titans compete in the Big West Conference. The program has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and is known for producing competitive teams despite limited resources. The Titans have been a consistent Big West contender.
2239.mascotOrigin: The Titans nickname was adopted in 1959, representing size and strength — a bold choice for a young university looking to establish a powerful athletic identity.
2239.iconicMoment: Upsetting Purdue in the first round of the 2018 NCAA Tournament as a No. 13 seed, one of the most exciting upsets of that year's March Madness.
2239.funFact: Cal State Fullerton's Titan Gym is one of the oldest basketball venues in the Big West, and the campus is located just minutes from Disneyland in Orange County.

2241.blurb: Gardner-Webb's basketball program began in 1942 at the Baptist university in Boiling Springs, North Carolina. The Runnin' Bulldogs compete in the Big South Conference and have won multiple conference titles. The program transitioned to Division I in 2000.
2241.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted to represent toughness, with the 'Runnin' prefix added to reflect the team's fast-paced, high-energy playing style.
2241.iconicMoment: Winning the 2019 Big South Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, where they drew Virginia in the first round.
2241.funFact: Gardner-Webb's campus in Boiling Springs, North Carolina gets its name from natural springs in the area that appear to boil due to the sand and minerals bubbling up from underground.

2244.blurb: George Mason's basketball program began in 1966 in Fairfax, Virginia. The Patriots compete in the Atlantic 10 and are best known for one of the greatest Cinderella runs in NCAA Tournament history. The program has been a competitive mid-major force.
2244.mascotOrigin: The Patriots nickname honors George Mason, one of America's Founding Fathers and the 'Father of the Bill of Rights,' for whom the university is named.
2244.iconicMoment: The 2006 Final Four run as an 11-seed under coach Jim Larranaga, defeating Michigan State, North Carolina, and Wichita State — one of the greatest Cinderella stories ever.
2244.funFact: George Mason's 2006 Final Four run was so improbable that the team was featured on the cover of Sports Illustrated, and coach Jim Larranaga was later hired by Miami (FL) on the strength of that run.

2247.blurb: Georgia State's basketball program began in 1963 in downtown Atlanta. The Panthers compete in the Sun Belt Conference and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. The program gained national attention with several March Madness upsets.
2247.mascotOrigin: The Panthers nickname was adopted to represent the fierce, agile big cat, giving the urban university in downtown Atlanta a strong athletic identity.
2247.iconicMoment: R.J. Hunter's game-winning three-pointer to upset third-seeded Baylor 57-56 in the 2015 NCAA Tournament first round, and his father Ron Hunter falling off his stool in celebration.
2247.funFact: Georgia State's home arena was formerly Philips Arena (now State Farm Arena), the same venue used by the Atlanta Hawks — one of the few mid-major programs to share a venue with an NBA team.

2250.blurb: Gonzaga University, a small Jesuit school in Spokane, Washington founded in 1887, began playing basketball in 1907. Once known as the 'Fighting Irish' in the 1910s, the school switched to 'Bulldogs' in 1921. Gonzaga's magical 1999 NCAA Tournament run as a No. 10 seed — highlighted by CBS announcer Gus Johnson's famous 'The slipper still fits!' call — transformed the program from unknown to household name. Under Mark Few, who took over in 1999, Gonzaga has become one of the most consistent programs in the nation, including a 31-0 regular season in 2021.
2250.mascotOrigin: The school switched from 'Fighting Irish' to 'Bulldogs' in 1921; a live bulldog mascot named Salty once bit a referee during a 1967 game, making Sports Illustrated
2250.iconicMoment: The 1999 Cinderella run to the Elite Eight as a No. 10 seed, cementing Gonzaga as a national brand with CBS's Gus Johnson calling 'The slipper still fits!'
2250.funFact: When Gonzaga opened McCarthey Athletic Center in 2004, the Bulldogs won 100 consecutive home games there, the longest such streak in the NCAA at the time

2253.blurb: Grand Canyon's basketball program began in 1949 at the Christian university in Phoenix, Arizona. The Lopes transitioned from Division II to Division I in 2013 and have quickly become competitive, making the NCAA Tournament. GCU's arena atmosphere has become one of the most celebrated in college basketball.
2253.mascotOrigin: The Lopes (short for Antelopes) nickname references the pronghorn antelope native to the Arizona desert, chosen for the animal's speed and grace.
2253.iconicMoment: Earning a 2021 NCAA Tournament bid in just the program's first year of tournament eligibility at Division I, proving the rapid investment in the program paid off.
2253.funFact: GCU's Havocs student section is famous for their coordinated, perfectly choreographed routines that go viral on social media, making GCU Arena one of the most entertaining environments in college basketball.

2261.blurb: Hampton's basketball program began in 1921 at the HBCU in Hampton, Virginia. The Pirates compete in the CAA (formerly Big South and MEAC) and have made several NCAA Tournament appearances. The program has been a consistent contender in its conference.
2261.mascotOrigin: The Pirates nickname references the maritime history of Hampton Roads, Virginia, one of the most important naval and shipping regions in American history.
2261.iconicMoment: Upsetting second-seeded Iowa State in the first round of the 2001 NCAA Tournament, one of the most memorable HBCU upsets in tournament history.
2261.funFact: Hampton's campus sits on the Chesapeake Bay waterfront, and the university was founded in 1868 by Black and White leaders to educate formerly enslaved people — making its basketball success part of a larger story of perseverance.

2272.blurb: High Point's basketball program began in 1924 at the private university in High Point, North Carolina. The Panthers compete in the Big South Conference and have been building competitiveness under significant investment from the university. The program transitioned to Division I in 1999.
2272.mascotOrigin: The Panthers nickname was chosen to represent the fierce, powerful big cat, giving the university a strong and distinctive athletic identity.
2272.iconicMoment: Tubby Smith's hiring as head coach in 2018, bringing a national championship-winning coach to the mid-major program and signaling High Point's ambitions.
2272.funFact: High Point's Qubein Arena and Conference Center opened in 2019 and is one of the most impressive facilities in all of mid-major basketball, made possible by university president Nido Qubein's ambitious vision.

2275.blurb: Hofstra's basketball program began in 1935 at the university in Hempstead, on Long Island, New York. The Pride compete in the CAA and have won multiple conference championships. The program has made several NCAA Tournament appearances and produced NBA players.
2275.mascotOrigin: The 'Pride' nickname was adopted in 2004, replacing 'Flying Dutchmen,' with the pride of lions imagery reflecting strength and the collective spirit of the university community.
2275.iconicMoment: Charles Jenkins leading Hofstra to the 2001 America East Tournament title and NCAA Tournament, one of the program's most exciting postseason runs.
2275.funFact: Hofstra's campus in Hempstead is located just minutes from New York City, giving the program access to one of the richest basketball recruiting grounds in the country.

2277.blurb: Houston Christian's basketball program (formerly Houston Baptist) began in 1963 at the Christian university in Houston, Texas. The Huskies compete in the Southland Conference and have been building their program steadily. The university rebranded from Houston Baptist to Houston Christian in 2022.
2277.mascotOrigin: The Huskies nickname was chosen to represent the strength, endurance, and loyalty associated with the Siberian Husky breed.
2277.iconicMoment: The university's rebranding to Houston Christian in 2022, which brought increased visibility and a fresh identity to the athletic program.
2277.funFact: Houston Christian's campus is located in the Sharpstown area of Houston, within one of the most basketball-rich metropolitan areas in the country, competing for attention with the University of Houston and Rice.

2287.blurb: Illinois State's basketball program dates to 1899 in Normal, Illinois. The Redbirds compete in the Missouri Valley Conference and have been a consistent MVC contender. Doug Collins and NBA referee Gene Steratore are among the program's notable alumni.
2287.mascotOrigin: The Redbirds nickname references the cardinal, also known as the redbird, which is the state bird of Illinois and a common sight on the campus in central Illinois.
2287.iconicMoment: Doug Collins' legendary career in the early 1970s, where he became an All-American before being selected first overall in the 1973 NBA Draft.
2287.funFact: Doug Collins, the No. 1 overall pick in the 1973 NBA Draft who famously hit clutch free throws in the controversial 1972 Olympic gold medal game against the Soviet Union, played his college ball at Illinois State.

2294.blurb: Iowa's basketball program became varsity in 1901 and joined the Big Ten in 1908. The 'Hawkeye' name comes from James Fenimore Cooper's novel 'The Last of the Mohicans' and was adopted as Iowa's state nickname in 1838. The Hawkeyes reached the NCAA Championship game in 1956, falling to Bill Russell's undefeated San Francisco team. Carver-Hawkeye Arena, known as 'The House That Lute Built' after coach Lute Olson championed its construction, opened in 1983 with innovative geothermal heating and most seating below ground level.
2294.mascotOrigin: The 'Hawkeye' name comes from the hero in James Fenimore Cooper's 'The Last of the Mohicans' and was adopted as Iowa's state nickname in 1838
2294.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1956 NCAA Championship game against Bill Russell's unbeaten San Francisco Dons — Iowa's only title game appearance
2294.funFact: Carver-Hawkeye Arena is called 'The House That Lute Built' after coach Lute Olson championed its construction; the mascot Herky the Hawk even served as military insignia for the 124th Fighting Squadron during the Korean War

2296.blurb: Jackson State is a historically Black university in Mississippi that competes in the Southwestern Athletic Conference. The Tigers have a proud basketball tradition and have produced several professional players, serving as a pipeline for talent in the state of Mississippi.
2296.mascotOrigin: The Tigers nickname reflects strength and ferocity, a common and powerful symbol adopted by many HBCUs in the early 20th century.
2296.iconicMoment: Lindsey Hunter's dominant career at Jackson State in the early 1990s, where he became the school's all-time leading scorer before being drafted 10th overall by the Detroit Pistons in 1993.
2296.funFact: Jackson State's Lindsey Hunter played 17 seasons in the NBA and won an NBA championship as a player-coach with the Los Angeles Lakers in 2010.

2305.blurb: Kansas is the only college basketball program founded by the inventor of the game — Dr. James Naismith started the program in 1898, though he remains the school's only coach with a losing record. His protege Phog Allen, the 'Father of Basketball Coaching,' won 746 games and mentored future legends Adolph Rupp and Dean Smith. The Jayhawks have won four NCAA titles and are among the winningest programs in history. Allen Fieldhouse, named for Phog Allen with its court named for Naismith, has been called the loudest arena in college basketball.
2305.mascotOrigin: Named for 'Jayhawkers,' pro-Union guerrilla fighters from Kansas Territory during the Civil War era, with the bird mascot based on a combination of a blue jay and a hawk
2305.iconicMoment: Mario Chalmers' miraculous three-pointer to force overtime in the 2008 NCAA Championship game, which Kansas won over Memphis
2305.funFact: Kansas's coaching lineage is unmatched: Naismith coached Allen, who coached Dean Smith and Adolph Rupp — between them, they won over 3,000 games and multiple national titles

2306.blurb: Kansas State has a proud basketball history, reaching the Final Four four times and finishing as national runner-up twice (1951, 1958). The Wildcats compete in the Big 12 and have produced stars like Mitch Richmond and Michael Beasley, while coach Jack Gardner and later Tex Winter brought innovative offenses to Manhattan, Kansas.
2306.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats name was adopted in 1915 after football coach John Bender said his team "fought like wildcats" during a tough game, and the name carried over to all sports.
2306.iconicMoment: The 2023 Elite Eight run under Jerome Tang, where the Wildcats reached the brink of the Final Four in Tang's first season, energizing the program.
2306.funFact: Tex Winter developed his famous Triangle Offense while coaching at Kansas State in the 1950s-60s, a system later perfected by Phil Jackson to win 11 NBA championships with the Bulls and Lakers.

2309.blurb: Kent State competes in the Mid-American Conference and had its greatest basketball moment in 2002 when the Golden Flashes reached the Elite Eight. The program has produced NBA players and consistently competes for MAC titles from its campus in northeast Ohio.
2309.mascotOrigin: The Golden Flashes name was adopted in 1926, inspired by a sportswriter's description of the team's fast, flashy style of play combined with the school's blue and gold colors.
2309.iconicMoment: The 2002 NCAA Tournament run to the Elite Eight, where Kent State upset Indiana and knocked off higher seeds before falling to the eventual national champion.
2309.funFact: Antonio Gates, who became one of the greatest tight ends in NFL history, played basketball at Kent State and never played college football.

2320.blurb: Lamar University in Beaumont, Texas, competes in the Southland Conference and enjoyed its basketball peak in the early 1980s under coach Pat Foster. The Cardinals made three consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances from 1979-1981, establishing Lamar as a mid-major power in that era.
2320.mascotOrigin: The Cardinals name was adopted for the bird's red plumage, matching the school's primary color of red, which has been associated with Lamar since its founding.
2320.iconicMoment: The 1980 NCAA Tournament run where Lamar advanced to the Sweet Sixteen, upsetting Oregon State and Weber State before falling to eventual champion Louisville.
2320.funFact: Lamar's Montagne Center was one of the loudest mid-major arenas in the Southland Conference, with passionate fans creating a difficult road environment.

2325.blurb: La Salle University in Philadelphia has a rich basketball tradition, winning the 1954 NCAA Championship behind consensus All-American Tom Gola. The Explorers were a dominant force in the 1950s and remain one of the historic programs in Big Five Philadelphia basketball, competing in the Atlantic 10 Conference.
2325.mascotOrigin: The Explorers name honors French explorer René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, the 17th-century adventurer for whom the university is named.
2325.iconicMoment: Winning the 1954 NCAA Championship behind Tom Gola, who had 19 points and 19 rebounds in the title game against Bradley, completing one of the great individual tournament performances.
2325.funFact: Tom Gola holds the NCAA career rebounding record with 2,201 rebounds, a mark that will almost certainly never be broken in the modern era.

2329.blurb: Lehigh University in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, competes in the Patriot League and is best known for one of the greatest upsets in NCAA Tournament history. The Mountain Hawks have produced NBA player C.J. McCollum and maintain an intense rivalry with nearby Lafayette.
2329.mascotOrigin: The Mountain Hawks name was adopted in 1996, replacing the previous "Engineers" nickname to better reflect the rugged South Mountain terrain surrounding the Bethlehem campus.
2329.iconicMoment: The 2012 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #2 seed Duke, 75-70, behind C.J. McCollum's 30 points — one of the biggest upsets in tournament history.
2329.funFact: C.J. McCollum scored 30 points in Lehigh's upset of Duke and went on to become a three-time All-Star-caliber player in the NBA, the most successful pro from a Patriot League school.

2330.blurb: Le Moyne College in Syracuse, New York, transitioned to Division I in 2023-24 after a successful Division II history. The Dolphins compete in the Northeast Conference and are one of the newest additions to the Division I basketball landscape.
2330.mascotOrigin: The Dolphins mascot was chosen to represent intelligence and agility. Le Moyne College is named after Simon Le Moyne, a French Jesuit missionary who worked in the region in the 1600s.
2330.iconicMoment: The transition to Division I in 2023, marking a major milestone for the small Jesuit college as it entered the highest level of college basketball competition.
2330.funFact: Le Moyne is one of the 27 Jesuit colleges and universities in the United States, and its move to Division I was part of a wave of smaller schools seeking greater athletic visibility.

2335.blurb: Liberty University in Lynchburg, Virginia, has grown from a small Baptist college into a major athletic program competing in Conference USA. The Flames transitioned to Division I in 1988 and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances, including a memorable 2019 first-round upset.
2335.mascotOrigin: The Flames name represents the "fire" of faith and liberty, reflecting the evangelical Christian mission of the university founded by Jerry Falwell Sr. in 1971.
2335.iconicMoment: The 2019 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #5 seed Mississippi State, 80-76, the program's first NCAA Tournament win in school history.
2335.funFact: Liberty's Vines Center was one of the largest on-campus arenas in college basketball when it opened in 1990, with a capacity exceeding 9,000.

2344.blurb: Longwood University in Farmville, Virginia, transitioned to Division I in 2004 and competes in the Big South Conference. The Lancers made their first NCAA Tournament appearance in 2022, a milestone for the growing program.
2344.mascotOrigin: The Lancers name evokes medieval knights and chivalry, fitting the university's historic Virginia roots and the stately Longwood Estate for which the school is named.
2344.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament bid in 2022 by winning the Big South Conference Tournament, a landmark achievement for the Farmville school.
2344.funFact: Longwood University is the third-oldest public university in Virginia, founded in 1839, but its basketball program has only been Division I since 2004.

2348.blurb: Louisiana Tech in Ruston, Louisiana, competes in Conference USA and has a basketball history that includes an appearance in the 1985 AP Top 25 and several NCAA Tournament bids. The Bulldogs have produced NBA talent including Karl Malone, one of the greatest power forwards ever.
2348.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs mascot was adopted early in the school's history to represent tenacity and determination, a common but fitting choice for the Ruston-based institution.
2348.iconicMoment: Karl Malone's dominant tenure from 1982-1985, during which "The Mailman" became one of the most feared players in college basketball before being drafted 13th overall by the Utah Jazz.
2348.funFact: Karl Malone is the most famous athlete from Louisiana Tech and went on to score 36,928 career NBA points, the second-most in league history at the time of his retirement.

2349.blurb: UMass Lowell's basketball program traces its roots to 1958, and the school completed its transition to Division I in 2013. The River Hawks compete in the America East Conference and have been building their D-I identity.
2349.mascotOrigin: The River Hawks name was adopted after the 1991 merger of Lowell University and the University of Lowell, referencing the hawks that fly over the Merrimack River running through campus.
2349.iconicMoment: Becoming eligible for the America East Conference Tournament and postseason play for the first time in 2017, completing their D-I transition.
2349.funFact: UMass Lowell's Tsongas Center, the River Hawks' home arena, is also a professional hockey venue and concert hall, giving the basketball program a larger arena than many mid-major schools.

2350.blurb: Loyola Chicago, a Jesuit university founded in 1870, has played basketball since 1913. The 'Ramblers' nickname dates to the early days when the team had to travel across the city for games. Loyola won the 1963 NCAA Championship with a historic team that started four Black players — a first for a championship team during the Civil Rights era. The program captured hearts again in 2018 when 98-year-old team chaplain Sister Jean Dolores Schmidt became a national sensation during their Cinderella Final Four run.
2350.mascotOrigin: The 'Ramblers' name originated because the team had to 'ramble' across Chicago to find places to play in the program's early years
2350.iconicMoment: The 2018 Final Four run as an 11-seed, with 98-year-old team chaplain Sister Jean becoming a national phenomenon
2350.funFact: Loyola's 1963 championship team broke racial barriers by starting four Black players; the team's semifinal game against Mississippi State only happened because MSU players defied a state injunction prohibiting them from playing against integrated teams

2351.blurb: Loyola Marymount in Los Angeles is best remembered for its revolutionary run-and-gun offense under coach Paul Westhead in the late 1980s, led by Hank Gathers and Bo Kimble. The Lions compete in the West Coast Conference and had one of the most tragic and inspiring stories in college basketball history.
2351.mascotOrigin: The Lions mascot represents courage and nobility, a fitting symbol for the Jesuit and Marymount traditions merged when the two institutions combined in 1973.
2351.iconicMoment: The 1990 NCAA Tournament run to the Elite Eight, dedicated to Hank Gathers who collapsed and died on the court during the WCC Tournament — Bo Kimble shot his first free throw left-handed in each game in tribute.
2351.funFact: The 1989-90 Loyola Marymount team averaged 122.4 points per game, still the highest-scoring team in Division I history, running Paul Westhead's revolutionary system.

2352.blurb: Loyola University Maryland in Baltimore competes in the Patriot League and has been a consistent contender in the conference. The Greyhounds have made several NCAA Tournament appearances and maintain a strong basketball tradition among Patriot League schools.
2352.mascotOrigin: The Greyhounds mascot was adopted in the 1920s, chosen for the breed's speed and elegance, giving the Jesuit university a distinctive athletic identity.
2352.iconicMoment: The 2012 Patriot League Tournament championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, where the Greyhounds represented the conference on the national stage.
2352.funFact: Loyola Maryland is one of the few schools in the country with the Greyhound as its mascot, making it instantly recognizable among college athletics programs.

2363.blurb: Manhattan College in the Bronx, New York, competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference and has a long basketball tradition. The Jaspers have made several NCAA Tournament appearances and are a fixture of New York City college basketball.
2363.mascotOrigin: The Jaspers nickname honors Brother Jasper of Mary, a legendary 19th-century athletic director at Manhattan College who is also credited with inventing the seventh-inning stretch in baseball.
2363.iconicMoment: The 2004 NCAA Tournament appearance, when Manhattan earned a MAAC Tournament title and faced Wake Forest in the first round of the Big Dance.
2363.funFact: Brother Jasper of Mary, Manhattan's namesake, is widely credited with inventing the seventh-inning stretch during an 1882 baseball game, one of the quirkiest origin stories in college athletics.

2368.blurb: Marist College in Poughkeepsie, New York, competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference and has been a consistent presence in MAAC basketball. The Red Foxes have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and produce competitive teams from their Hudson Valley campus.
2368.mascotOrigin: The Red Foxes mascot was chosen to reflect the cunning and agility of the fox, combined with the school's red primary color, giving Marist a distinctive identity.
2368.iconicMoment: The 2007 MAAC Tournament Championship and subsequent NCAA Tournament appearance, a program highlight for the Poughkeepsie school.
2368.funFact: Marist's McCann Arena sits along the scenic Hudson River, making it one of the most picturesque settings for a college basketball game in the Northeast.

2377.blurb: McNeese State University in Lake Charles, Louisiana, competes in the Southland Conference and has been a consistent competitor in the league. The Cowboys have produced several professional basketball players and maintain a loyal following in southwest Louisiana.
2377.mascotOrigin: The Cowboys nickname honors the ranching and cattle heritage of southwest Louisiana and the legacy of John McNeese, the educator for whom the university is named.
2377.iconicMoment: The multiple Southland Conference regular season and tournament championships in the 2000s and 2010s, establishing McNeese as a perennial conference contender.
2377.funFact: McNeese State's campus in Lake Charles has been rebuilt multiple times due to hurricane damage, demonstrating the resilience of the university and its athletic programs.

2378.blurb: UMBC (University of Maryland, Baltimore County) has fielded a basketball program since 1967 and competes in the America East Conference. The 'Retrievers' mascot is a Chesapeake Bay Retriever, the state dog of Maryland. On March 16, 2018, UMBC made history by becoming the first No. 16 seed to defeat a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, stunning Virginia 74-54 in a result that shook college basketball to its core. The victory, led by Jairus Lyles' 28 points, remains the most significant upset in tournament history.
2378.mascotOrigin: Named the 'Retrievers' after the Chesapeake Bay Retriever, the official state dog of Maryland and a symbol of the Chesapeake Bay region
2378.iconicMoment: The historic 74-54 upset of No. 1 overall seed Virginia on March 16, 2018 — the first time a 16-seed ever defeated a 1-seed in the NCAA Tournament
2378.funFact: UMBC's stunning 20-point victory over Virginia was so unexpected that an estimated 97.5% of ESPN Tournament Challenge brackets had Virginia advancing — nearly all 18 million brackets were busted

2379.blurb: The University of Maryland Eastern Shore competes in the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference and is a historically Black university located in Princess Anne, Maryland. The Hawks have a proud basketball tradition in the MEAC with several conference championships.
2379.mascotOrigin: The Hawks mascot represents sharp vision and aerial dominance, chosen to give the Eastern Shore campus a fierce and competitive athletic identity.
2379.iconicMoment: The 1974 season under coach Kirkland Hall, when UMES earned an NCAA Tournament bid and competed on the national stage as a proud HBCU program.
2379.funFact: Maryland Eastern Shore's campus is located on the Delmarva Peninsula, making road trips to conference opponents some of the longest in the MEAC.

2382.blurb: Mercer University in Macon, Georgia, competes in the Southern Conference and is best remembered for one of the greatest NCAA Tournament upsets of the 2010s. The Bears have built a competitive program and are a fixture of Southern Conference basketball.
2382.mascotOrigin: The Bears mascot was adopted early in the school's athletic history, representing the strength and power of the animal found throughout Georgia's forests.
2382.iconicMoment: The 2014 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #3 seed Duke, 78-71, one of the most celebrated mid-major victories in recent tournament history.
2382.funFact: Mercer's 2014 upset of Duke was led by five seniors who had committed to building the program together, making the victory a culmination of a four-year journey.

2385.blurb: Mercyhurst University in Erie, Pennsylvania, began competing in Division I in 2023-24 as a member of the Northeast Conference. The Lakers are one of the newest Division I programs and are transitioning from a successful Division II history.
2385.mascotOrigin: The Lakers mascot reflects Mercyhurst's location on the shores of Lake Erie, one of the five Great Lakes, making it a natural and fitting choice.
2385.iconicMoment: The 2023 announcement of the transition to Division I, signaling a new era for Mercyhurst athletics and its basketball program.
2385.funFact: Mercyhurst was founded by the Sisters of Mercy in 1926 and is one of the smallest universities to recently transition to Division I athletics.

2390.blurb: Miami's basketball program launched in 1926, just a year after the university's founding. The 'Hurricanes' name was chosen after a devastating 1926 hurricane postponed the opening football game — players suggested the name hoping they would 'sweep away opponents' like the storm. The program was actually shut down from 1971-1985 due to poor facilities and attendance, but was revived and has since become a competitive ACC member. Sebastian the Ibis serves as mascot — chosen because the ibis is the last bird to flee an approaching hurricane and the first to reappear.
2390.mascotOrigin: Players chose 'Hurricanes' in 1926 after a real hurricane postponed their opening game; the ibis mascot was chosen because it is the last bird to flee and first to return after a hurricane
2390.iconicMoment: Winning the 2013 ACC regular season and tournament championships under Jim Larranaga, marking Miami's emergence as a legitimate ACC basketball power
2390.funFact: In 1989, mascot Sebastian the Ibis was tackled and handcuffed by police after trying to extinguish Florida State's flaming spear with a fire extinguisher while wearing a fireman's helmet

2393.blurb: Middle Tennessee State University in Murfreesboro competes in Conference USA and has a history of producing NCAA Tournament upsets. The Blue Raiders have won multiple conference championships and are known for their competitive basketball program.
2393.mascotOrigin: The Blue Raiders name was coined in 1934 by a local sportswriter who described the team "riding" to victory, combined with the school's blue primary color.
2393.iconicMoment: The 2016 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #2 seed Michigan State, 90-81, one of the biggest upsets of that year's tournament.
2393.funFact: Middle Tennessee has pulled off multiple first-round NCAA Tournament upsets, earning a reputation as one of the most dangerous mid-major programs come March.

2400.blurb: Mississippi Valley State is a historically Black university in Itta Bena, Mississippi, competing in the Southwestern Athletic Conference. The Delta Devils are best known for producing one of the greatest wide receivers in NFL history, but their basketball program has also won SWAC championships.
2400.mascotOrigin: The Delta Devils name references the Mississippi Delta region where the university is located, combined with a fierce competitive spirit.
2400.iconicMoment: Winning the SWAC Tournament to earn NCAA Tournament bids, representing the conference on the national stage as a proud HBCU program.
2400.funFact: Mississippi Valley State is located in the heart of the Mississippi Delta, one of the most culturally rich regions in the American South, known for its blues music heritage.

2405.blurb: Monmouth University in West Long Branch, New Jersey, competes in the Coastal Athletic Association (formerly Colonial Athletic Association) and gained national attention for its entertaining bench celebrations. The Hawks have been competitive in their conference and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
2405.mascotOrigin: The Hawks mascot represents keen vision and aerial dominance, chosen to give the New Jersey shore school a competitive and aggressive athletic identity.
2405.iconicMoment: The 2015-16 season when Monmouth's bench mob went viral, with players performing elaborate choreographed celebrations that gained national media attention and endeared the team to fans.
2405.funFact: Monmouth's bench celebrations during the 2015-16 season became so famous that they were featured on SportsCenter's Top 10, turning the Hawks into a national sensation.

2413.blurb: Morehead State University in eastern Kentucky competes in the Ohio Valley Conference and has produced competitive basketball teams for decades. The Eagles have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and produced notable NBA players.
2413.mascotOrigin: The Eagles mascot represents the bald eagles that soar over the Daniel Boone National Forest near Morehead, symbolizing freedom and strength.
2413.iconicMoment: The 2011 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #4 seed Louisville, led by Kenneth Faried's dominant 25-point, 10-rebound performance.
2413.funFact: Kenneth Faried became the NCAA's all-time rebounding leader during his career at Morehead State, surpassing Tim Duncan's record with 1,673 career rebounds.

2415.blurb: Morgan State University in Baltimore is a historically Black university competing in the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference. The Bears have won multiple MEAC championships and have a proud basketball tradition among HBCUs.
2415.mascotOrigin: The Bears mascot was adopted to represent strength and tenacity, giving the Baltimore-based HBCU a powerful athletic identity.
2415.iconicMoment: The 2010 NCAA Tournament appearance, earned by winning the MEAC Tournament Championship and representing the conference on the national stage.
2415.funFact: Morgan State has one of the most active and spirited pep bands in the MEAC, creating an electric atmosphere at home basketball games.

2426.blurb: The United States Naval Academy competes in the Patriot League and has a proud basketball tradition, including a 1986 Elite Eight appearance led by David Robinson. Navy's players must fulfill a five-year military service commitment after graduation, making their athletic achievements even more remarkable.
2426.mascotOrigin: The Midshipmen name comes from the rank of students at the Naval Academy, who are called midshipmen as they train to become officers in the U.S. Navy and Marine Corps.
2426.iconicMoment: David Robinson leading Navy to the 1986 Elite Eight, averaging 28.2 points and 11.8 rebounds that season, before being drafted #1 overall by the San Antonio Spurs.
2426.funFact: David Robinson had to wait two years after being drafted to play in the NBA due to his military service obligation, yet he still became one of the greatest centers in basketball history.

2427.blurb: UNC Asheville's basketball program dates to 1961, and the mountain school competes in the Big South Conference. The Bulldogs have been a competitive presence in their conference, earning several NCAA Tournament bids.
2427.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was chosen as a traditional symbol of tenacity and determination, fitting for a small school competing against larger programs.
2427.iconicMoment: Winning the 2012 Big South Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, where the Bulldogs faced Syracuse as a 16-seed and played competitively.
2427.funFact: UNC Asheville is located in the Blue Ridge Mountains and is consistently ranked among the top public liberal arts universities in the South, making it one of the most academically focused D-I schools.

2428.blurb: North Carolina Central University in Durham is a historically Black university competing in the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference. The Eagles have won multiple MEAC championships and share a city with Duke University, creating a unique local basketball dynamic.
2428.mascotOrigin: The Eagles mascot represents soaring ambition and excellence, chosen to embody the aspirations of the Durham-based HBCU.
2428.iconicMoment: Earning back-to-back NCAA Tournament bids in 2014 and 2019, establishing NCCU as a consistent MEAC power and a force in HBCU basketball.
2428.funFact: NCCU coach LeVelle Moton became one of the most recognizable HBCU coaches in the country, known for his passionate advocacy for HBCU athletics and his winning record in the MEAC.

2429.blurb: Charlotte's basketball program began in 1965 and quickly rose to prominence under coach Lee Rose, reaching the Final Four in 1977. The 49ers compete in AAC (formerly Conference USA) and have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. The program produced NBA star Cedric Maxwell.
2429.mascotOrigin: The 49ers nickname was chosen in 1961 to honor the gold prospectors who came to Charlotte, North Carolina during America's first gold rush in the late 1700s and early 1800s.
2429.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1977 Final Four under coach Lee Rose, led by Cedric 'Cornbread' Maxwell, just 12 years after the program was founded.
2429.funFact: Charlotte was the site of the first documented gold find in America in 1799, and the 49ers play in a city that was once the center of American gold mining, predating the California Gold Rush by 50 years.

2430.blurb: UNC Greensboro's basketball program dates to 1968, and the Spartans compete in the Southern Conference. UNCG has built a solid program that has earned multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and consistently competes for SoCon titles.
2430.mascotOrigin: The Spartans nickname was adopted when the school began its athletics program, chosen for the ancient Greek warriors' discipline and competitive spirit.
2430.iconicMoment: Winning the 2018 Southern Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, the program's first in 17 years, under coach Wes Miller.
2430.funFact: Wes Miller, who played on North Carolina's 2005 national championship team, built UNCG into a Southern Conference power before being hired back at UNC as head coach.

2433.blurb: UL Monroe's basketball program dates to 1951, and the northeast Louisiana school competes in the Sun Belt Conference. The Warhawks have had periods of success, including an NIT appearance and several conference tournament runs.
2433.mascotOrigin: The Warhawks name was adopted in 2006, replacing "Indians." The Curtiss P-40 Warhawk fighter plane, which trained at nearby Selman Field during WWII, inspired the choice.
2433.iconicMoment: The program's competitive Sun Belt seasons in the 2010s, establishing ULM as a legitimate conference contender after years of rebuilding.
2433.funFact: UL Monroe's previous name, Northeast Louisiana, produced NFL quarterback Tim Rattay and is located in Monroe, Louisiana — the birthplace of Delta Air Lines.

2437.blurb: The University of Nebraska Omaha competes in the Summit League and transitioned to Division I in 2012. The Mavericks are building their basketball program and have become increasingly competitive in the conference.
2437.mascotOrigin: The Mavericks mascot represents independence and a pioneering spirit, fitting for a university in Omaha that charted its own course to Division I athletics.
2437.iconicMoment: The steady improvement and competitive Summit League seasons in the late 2010s, establishing Omaha as a legitimate contender rather than a conference afterthought.
2437.funFact: Omaha dropped its football program in 2011 to fund the transition to Division I in other sports, one of the few schools to make such a strategic sacrifice.

2439.blurb: UNLV's basketball program was founded in 1958 and became a national power under Jerry Tarkanian, the legendary 'Tark the Shark.' The Runnin' Rebels won the 1990 NCAA Championship in dominant fashion, crushing Duke 103-73 — the largest margin of victory in title game history. Tarkanian was famous for chewing on a towel during games and for recruiting talented players others overlooked. The 1991 team went 34-0 before a stunning upset loss to Duke in the Final Four, denying what many considered one of the greatest teams ever assembled.
2439.mascotOrigin: The 'Rebels' nickname and Hey Reb mascot reflect the frontier spirit of Las Vegas and the American West
2439.iconicMoment: The 1990 NCAA Championship, a 103-73 demolition of Duke that set the record for largest margin of victory in a title game
2439.funFact: The 1991 UNLV team went 34-0 during the regular season and was heavily favored to repeat as champions, but was upset by Duke in the Final Four semifinal in one of the biggest upsets in tournament history

2440.blurb: The University of Nevada in Reno competes in the Mountain West Conference and has had several strong basketball eras. The Wolf Pack reached the Sweet Sixteen in 2004 and 2018, and the program has produced NBA players including Luke Babbitt and Javale McGee's cousin, though the school is most known for its 2018 comeback.
2440.mascotOrigin: The Wolf Pack name was adopted in 1921, inspired by the wolf packs that roam the Sierra Nevada mountains near the Reno campus.
2440.iconicMoment: The 2018 NCAA Tournament comeback against Cincinnati, erasing a 22-point deficit to win in overtime and advance to the Sweet Sixteen in one of the greatest comebacks in tournament history.
2440.funFact: Nevada's 22-point comeback against Cincinnati in the 2018 NCAA Tournament is tied for the largest comeback in tournament history, a game that had fans across the country in disbelief.

2441.blurb: The University of New Haven transitioned to Division I in 2023-24, joining the Northeast Conference after a successful Division II history. The Chargers are among the newest Division I programs and are building their identity at the highest level of college basketball.
2441.mascotOrigin: The Chargers mascot represents speed and power, chosen to give the West Haven, Connecticut-based university an aggressive and energetic athletic identity.
2441.iconicMoment: The announcement of the transition to Division I in 2023, opening a new chapter for New Haven athletics and its basketball program.
2441.funFact: The University of New Haven is known for its forensic science program, one of the best in the nation, giving the school a unique academic identity alongside its growing athletic profile.

2443.blurb: The University of New Orleans competes in the Southland Conference and represents the Crescent City in Division I basketball. The Privateers have produced competitive teams and benefit from the rich basketball culture of New Orleans.
2443.mascotOrigin: The Privateers name honors the privateers and pirates who sailed the Gulf of Mexico and frequented New Orleans in the 18th and 19th centuries, including the legendary Jean Lafitte.
2443.iconicMoment: The 1993 Sun Belt Conference Tournament championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, a high-water mark for the New Orleans program.
2443.funFact: The University of New Orleans's Lakefront Arena has hosted major events including NBA preseason games, making it a versatile venue beyond college basketball.

2447.blurb: Nicholls State University in Thibodaux, Louisiana, competes in the Southland Conference. The Colonels have produced competitive basketball teams and represent the Cajun bayou region of southern Louisiana in Division I athletics.
2447.mascotOrigin: The Colonels nickname honors Francis T. Nicholls, a Confederate colonel and former Louisiana governor for whom the university is named.
2447.iconicMoment: The 1998 Southland Conference Tournament championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, a highlight for the Thibodaux-based program.
2447.funFact: Nicholls State is located in the heart of Louisiana's bayou country, and the university's culinary program is one of the best in the South, reflecting the region's rich food culture.

2448.blurb: North Carolina A&T is a historically Black university in Greensboro competing in the Colonial Athletic Association. The Aggies have won multiple MEAC championships and made NCAA Tournament appearances, representing one of the largest HBCUs in the nation.
2448.mascotOrigin: The Aggies nickname reflects the university's agricultural and technical roots, as NC A&T was founded in 1891 as an agricultural and mechanical college.
2448.iconicMoment: The 2013 NCAA Tournament appearance, when the Aggies won the MEAC Tournament and faced Louisville in the first round, proudly representing HBCU basketball on the national stage.
2448.funFact: NC A&T is the largest HBCU in the nation by enrollment, and its move from the MEAC to the CAA in 2022 was a significant realignment moment for HBCU athletics.

2449.blurb: North Dakota State University in Fargo competes in the Summit League and has transitioned to Division I with notable success. While best known for its dominant football program, the Bison have also made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances in basketball.
2449.mascotOrigin: The Bison (often called Thundering Herd) mascot represents the American bison that once roamed the Great Plains by the millions, a fitting symbol for the Fargo school.
2449.iconicMoment: The 2009 NCAA Tournament first-round game against Kansas, the Bison's first appearance in the Big Dance after transitioning to Division I, marking a program milestone.
2449.funFact: NDSU's transition to Division I was fueled by the success of its football program, which has won multiple FCS national championships, bringing visibility that benefited all sports including basketball.

2450.blurb: Norfolk State University is a historically Black university in Norfolk, Virginia, competing in the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference. The Spartans have won multiple MEAC championships and made memorable NCAA Tournament appearances.
2450.mascotOrigin: The Spartans mascot was chosen to represent the warrior spirit and discipline of ancient Sparta, embodying toughness and resilience.
2450.iconicMoment: The 2012 NCAA Tournament first-round upset of #2 seed Missouri, 86-84, one of the biggest upsets in tournament history and a proud moment for HBCU basketball.
2450.funFact: Norfolk State's 2012 upset of Missouri was only the fifth time a #15 seed had beaten a #2 seed in NCAA Tournament history, making it one of the most celebrated HBCU basketball victories ever.

2453.blurb: The University of North Alabama in Florence transitioned to Division I in 2018-19 and competes in the ASUN Conference. The Lions had a storied Division II history, including three national championships, before making the jump to the highest level.
2453.mascotOrigin: The Lions mascot dates back decades and represents courage and royalty. UNA famously kept live lions on campus, named Leo, for many years as part of the university's tradition.
2453.iconicMoment: The transition to Division I in 2018, backed by the confidence of three Division II national championships that demonstrated the program's ability to compete at a high level.
2453.funFact: North Alabama kept live lion mascots on campus for decades, making it one of the few universities in the country to house its actual mascot animal.

2454.blurb: The University of North Florida in Jacksonville competes in the ASUN Conference and has built a competitive basketball program since transitioning to Division I in 2005. The Ospreys have won ASUN championships and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
2454.mascotOrigin: The Osprey was chosen as the mascot because the fish-eating bird of prey is commonly found along the waterways near the Jacksonville campus.
2454.iconicMoment: The 2015 NCAA Tournament appearance after winning the ASUN Tournament, the program's first trip to the Big Dance in its Division I history.
2454.funFact: North Florida is one of the youngest Division I programs in the state of Florida, having only been founded as a university in 1972.

2458.blurb: The University of Northern Colorado in Greeley competes in the Big Sky Conference and has built a competitive basketball program. The Bears have contended for Big Sky titles and produced professional basketball players.
2458.mascotOrigin: The Bears mascot represents the black bears found in the Rocky Mountain foothills near Greeley, giving the university a rugged Colorado identity.
2458.iconicMoment: The 2011 Big Sky Conference Tournament championship run, earning the Bears an NCAA Tournament appearance and their moment on the national stage.
2458.funFact: Northern Colorado's Bank of Colorado Arena provides one of the more intimate settings in the Big Sky Conference, with student sections creating a loud atmosphere for visiting teams.

2459.blurb: Northern Illinois University in DeKalb competes in the Mid-American Conference and has a basketball tradition spanning over a century. The Huskies have made occasional NCAA Tournament appearances and compete in one of the more competitive mid-major conferences.
2459.mascotOrigin: The Huskies mascot was adopted in 1940, chosen for the breed's endurance and tenacity in harsh Midwestern winters, fitting for a school in northern Illinois.
2459.iconicMoment: The 1996 season, when the Huskies earned a bye and then competed in the NIT, showcasing the program's ability to compete beyond conference play.
2459.funFact: NIU's Convocation Center has hosted major events beyond basketball, including concerts and professional wrestling, making it a multi-purpose entertainment hub for the DeKalb area.

2460.blurb: The University of Northern Iowa competes in the Missouri Valley Conference and has been one of the most successful mid-major programs in the country. The Panthers have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for pulling off major upsets.
2460.mascotOrigin: The Panthers mascot was adopted in 1931, replacing "Tutors" and then "Teachers" as the school transitioned from a teachers' college to a university.
2460.iconicMoment: Ali Farokhmanesh's dagger three-pointer to upset #1 seed Kansas in the 2010 NCAA Tournament second round, one of the most iconic shots in March Madness history.
2460.funFact: UNI's McLeod Center is one of the loudest venues in the Missouri Valley Conference, and the Panthers have historically been one of the toughest home teams in mid-major basketball.

2463.blurb: Cal State Northridge's basketball program began in 1958 and the Matadors compete in the Big West Conference. The program transitioned to Division I in 1990 and has produced NBA players like Stephan Thompson. CSUN has been competitive in the Big West with occasional tournament appearances.
2463.mascotOrigin: The Matadors nickname was chosen in 1958 to evoke the bravery and showmanship of bullfighters, reflecting Southern California's Spanish cultural heritage.
2463.iconicMoment: Winning the 2001 Big Sky Conference Tournament championship, earning the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance as a Division I school.
2463.funFact: CSUN's campus was devastated by the 1994 Northridge earthquake, which destroyed or damaged nearly every building on campus — the basketball program had to play home games off-site for years during reconstruction.

2464.blurb: Northern Arizona University in Flagstaff competes in the Big Sky Conference and plays its home games at the highest elevation of any Division I basketball program. The Lumberjacks have won Big Sky titles and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
2464.mascotOrigin: The Lumberjacks name honors the logging industry that was central to Flagstaff's founding and economic development in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
2464.iconicMoment: The 2000 Big Sky Conference Tournament championship and NCAA Tournament appearance, when the Lumberjacks represented the conference on the national stage.
2464.funFact: Northern Arizona plays at approximately 7,000 feet elevation in Flagstaff, making visiting teams deal with altitude that can affect conditioning and shooting in the thin mountain air.

2466.blurb: Northwestern State University in Natchitoches, Louisiana, competes in the Southland Conference and has a basketball tradition anchored by occasional upsets and conference titles. The Demons play in one of the oldest communities in Louisiana.
