2466.mascotOrigin: The Demons nickname was adopted in the early 20th century, chosen for its fearsome and intimidating connotation to inspire the athletic teams.
2466.iconicMoment: The 2006 NCAA Tournament appearance, when Northwestern State earned a Southland Conference title and faced Iowa in the first round of the Big Dance.
2466.funFact: Natchitoches, home of Northwestern State, is the oldest permanent settlement in the Louisiana Purchase territory, founded in 1714 by the French.

2473.blurb: Oakland University in Rochester, Michigan, competes in the Horizon League and has built a competitive mid-major program. The Golden Grizzlies have made NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for producing upset-minded teams.
2473.mascotOrigin: The Golden Grizzlies mascot combines the school's gold primary color with the grizzly bear, representing power and ferocity for the suburban Detroit university.
2473.iconicMoment: The 2005 and 2011 NCAA Tournament appearances, establishing Oakland as a legitimate contender in the Horizon League and a program capable of competing nationally.
2473.funFact: Oakland University was founded on land donated by Matilda Dodge Wilson, the widow of auto pioneer John Dodge, making the campus one of the most scenic in Michigan.

2483.blurb: Oregon's basketball program dates to 1903, and the Ducks won the first-ever NCAA Championship in 1939, defeating Ohio State 46-33 in Evanston, Illinois. The program's identity is deeply tied to Nike co-founder Phil Knight, an Oregon alumnus whose generous donations transformed the Ducks' facilities into among the finest in the nation. Matthew Knight Arena, opened in 2011, features state-of-the-art amenities. Oregon has returned to national prominence in recent years with multiple Sweet Sixteen and Final Four appearances.
2483.mascotOrigin: The 'Ducks' name is commonly associated with Oregon; the mascot is modeled after Disney's Donald Duck through a handshake agreement with Walt Disney himself in 1947
2483.iconicMoment: Winning the first-ever NCAA Championship in 1939, defeating Ohio State 46-33 to claim the inaugural title
2483.funFact: Oregon's mascot is based on Donald Duck thanks to a 1947 handshake agreement between then-athletic director Leo Harris and Walt Disney, making the Ducks the only school with a Disney-licensed mascot

2492.blurb: Pepperdine University in Malibu, California, competes in the West Coast Conference and plays in one of the most beautiful campus settings in college basketball. The Waves have made NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for their picturesque oceanside campus.
2492.mascotOrigin: The Waves mascot was adopted to reflect the university's stunning location overlooking the Pacific Ocean in Malibu, where waves crash against the nearby shore.
2492.iconicMoment: The 2000 NCAA Tournament upset of Indiana in the first round, when the Waves knocked off the storied Hoosiers and advanced to the second round.
2492.funFact: Pepperdine's Firestone Fieldhouse overlooks the Pacific Ocean, making it arguably the most scenic basketball venue in Division I athletics.

2501.blurb: The University of Portland competes in the West Coast Conference and has a basketball tradition that occasionally produces competitive teams. The Pilots play in the basketball-rich Pacific Northwest and have had periods of conference contention.
2501.mascotOrigin: The Pilots mascot was adopted in 1935, inspired by Portland's history as a major shipping and river port, where boat pilots navigated the Willamette and Columbia rivers.
2501.iconicMoment: The 1996 West Coast Conference regular season title and subsequent NIT appearance, one of the program's strongest modern seasons.
2501.funFact: Portland's Chiles Center is a cozy on-campus arena that creates an intimate atmosphere for WCC games, giving the Pilots a modest but enthusiastic home-court advantage.

2502.blurb: Portland State University competes in the Big Sky Conference and represents the city of Portland in Division I basketball. The Vikings have been competitive in the Big Sky and have made occasional postseason appearances.
2502.mascotOrigin: The Vikings mascot was adopted to represent the Scandinavian heritage of many Pacific Northwest settlers, evoking strength and exploration.
2502.iconicMoment: The 2009 CIT Championship, when Portland State won the inaugural CollegeInsider.com Tournament, a postseason highlight for the program.
2502.funFact: Portland State is one of the few Division I basketball programs located in the heart of a major city's downtown, with its campus situated in Portland's urban core.

2504.blurb: Prairie View A&M University is a historically Black university in Texas competing in the Southwestern Athletic Conference. The Panthers have a long basketball tradition and are part of the Texas A&M University System, maintaining a proud HBCU identity.
2504.mascotOrigin: The Panthers mascot represents stealth, power, and aggressiveness, chosen to give the Prairie View campus a fierce and competitive athletic identity.
2504.iconicMoment: Breaking an 80-game losing streak in 2005 became a moment of celebration and relief, demonstrating the resilience and spirit of the Prairie View basketball community.
2504.funFact: Prairie View A&M once suffered an NCAA-record 80 consecutive losses from 1992-1998, making the eventual return to competitiveness a remarkable turnaround story.

2506.blurb: Presbyterian College in Clinton, South Carolina, competes in the Big South Conference and is one of the smallest schools in Division I basketball. The Blue Hose have been building their program since transitioning to Division I in 2007.
2506.mascotOrigin: The "Blue Hose" nickname comes from the blue stockings worn by Scottish Presbyterian worshippers, a nod to the college's Presbyterian heritage and Scottish roots.
2506.iconicMoment: The transition to Division I in 2007, marking Presbyterian's entry into the highest level of college basketball competition and a new era for the small South Carolina school.
2506.funFact: Presbyterian's "Blue Hose" is one of the most unique nicknames in college athletics, consistently appearing on lists of the strangest mascot names in Division I sports.

2507.blurb: Providence College, a Dominican Catholic institution founded in 1917, began its basketball program in 1921. The 'Friars' nickname naturally reflects the school's Dominican religious heritage. Providence has been a Big East Conference member since the league's founding in 1979 and reached the Final Four in 1973 and 1987. The program has produced notable NBA players including Jimmy Walker and Billy Donovan, who went on to become one of the most successful coaches in college basketball history at Florida.
2507.mascotOrigin: The 'Friars' name reflects Providence College's founding by Dominican friars, members of the Order of Preachers
2507.iconicMoment: The 1987 Final Four run under Rick Pitino, marking Providence's second and most recent trip to the national semifinals
2507.funFact: Former Providence player Billy Donovan went on to become the head coach at Florida, winning back-to-back national championships in 2006 and 2007

2509.blurb: Purdue played its first basketball game in 1896, a 34-19 win over the Lafayette YMCA, and has been a Big Ten powerhouse ever since. The 'Boilermakers' nickname originated in 1891 when a reporter described Purdue's 44-0 football rout of Wabash College. Purdue holds the most Big Ten regular season championships with 26 and was the first Big Ten program ranked No. 1 for three consecutive seasons. Despite producing legends like John Wooden and reaching multiple title games, the program is still chasing its first NCAA Championship.
2509.mascotOrigin: A Crawfordsville reporter dubbed the team 'Boiler Makers' after Purdue's 44-0 football trouncing of Wabash College on October 26, 1891
2509.iconicMoment: Zach Edey leading Purdue to the 2024 NCAA Championship game, the program's first Final Four in 44 years
2509.funFact: Purdue's official mascot is a full-size replica Victorian-era locomotive called the Boilermaker Special, claimed to be the world's largest, fastest, heaviest, and loudest collegiate mascot

2511.blurb: Queens University of Charlotte transitioned to Division I in 2023-24 and competes in the ASUN Conference. The Royals had a successful Division II basketball history before making the jump to the highest level of college competition.
2511.mascotOrigin: The Royals mascot reflects the regal name of the university, with "Queens" evoking royalty and nobility that the athletic teams embrace.
2511.iconicMoment: The 2023 transition to Division I and the ASUN Conference, marking a bold new era for the Charlotte-based university and its basketball program.
2511.funFact: Queens University of Charlotte was founded in 1857 as a women's college and only became coeducational in 1987, making its Division I athletics journey a relatively recent development.

2514.blurb: Quinnipiac University in Hamden, Connecticut, competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference and has grown from a small college into a competitive Division I program. The Bobcats have been building their basketball identity alongside the growth of the university.
2514.mascotOrigin: The Bobcats mascot was chosen to represent the agile and fierce wild cats found in the Connecticut woodlands, giving the Hamden campus a spirited athletic identity.
2514.iconicMoment: The 2018 MAAC Tournament run, when Quinnipiac made a deep conference tournament push and showed the program's growing competitiveness.
2514.funFact: Quinnipiac is named after the Quinnipiac River, which itself is named after the Quinnipiac people, a Native American tribe indigenous to the New Haven area of Connecticut.

2515.blurb: Radford's basketball program dates to 1920, and the Virginia school competes in the Big South Conference. The Highlanders have been a steady Big South competitor, occasionally earning NCAA Tournament bids.
2515.mascotOrigin: The Highlanders name reflects Radford's location in the New River Valley of the Virginia highlands, surrounded by the Appalachian Mountains.
2515.iconicMoment: Winning the 2018 Big South Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, the program's first trip to March Madness since 2009.
2515.funFact: Radford University was originally a women's college (Radford State Teachers College for Women) and didn't become coeducational until 1972 — men's basketball is a relatively modern addition.

2520.blurb: Rider's basketball program dates to 1929, and the Lawrenceville, New Jersey school competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference. The Broncs have been a competitive MAAC program with multiple conference tournament appearances.
2520.mascotOrigin: The Broncs name is a shortened form of broncos, representing the untamed horses of the American West and the school's original equestrian connection.
2520.iconicMoment: Winning the 1994 MAAC Tournament and earning an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's most successful seasons.
2520.funFact: Rider's campus is located in Lawrenceville, New Jersey, just minutes from Princeton University, creating one of the most unlikely Division I basketball rivalries in the Garden State.

2523.blurb: Robert Morris's basketball program began in 1979, and the Pittsburgh-area school competes in the Horizon League. The Colonials have won multiple conference titles and made several NCAA Tournament appearances.
2523.mascotOrigin: The Colonials name honors Robert Morris, a Founding Father and financier of the American Revolution, who signed the Declaration of Independence, the Articles of Confederation, and the Constitution.
2523.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 4 seed Kentucky in the 2013 NIT, one of the most memorable wins in program history.
2523.funFact: Robert Morris University is named after one of only two people to sign all three founding documents of the United States — the Declaration of Independence, Articles of Confederation, and Constitution.

2529.blurb: Sacred Heart's basketball program dates to 1963, and the Fairfield, Connecticut school made the jump to Division I in 1999. The Pioneers compete in the Northeast Conference and have grown into a competitive D-I program.
2529.mascotOrigin: The Pioneers name reflects the university's founding spirit as the first Catholic university in New England to be led by laypersons rather than clergy.
2529.iconicMoment: Winning the 2019 NEC Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance since moving to Division I.
2529.funFact: Sacred Heart is located in Fairfield, Connecticut, in affluent Fairfield County, giving it proximity to New York City recruiting and one of the wealthiest regions in the country.

2534.blurb: Sam Houston's basketball program dates to 1919, and the Huntsville, Texas school recently moved to Conference USA after years in the Southland Conference. The Bearkats have been competitive across multiple conferences.
2534.mascotOrigin: The Bearkats name (intentionally misspelled with a K) was adopted in the early 1900s, representing a fierce, hybrid creature — part bear, part wildcat.
2534.iconicMoment: Making the transition to the FBS level and Conference USA in 2023, elevating the entire athletics program including basketball.
2534.funFact: Sam Houston State is named after Sam Houston, president of the Republic of Texas and governor of two different U.S. states (Tennessee and Texas) — the only person to hold that distinction.

2535.blurb: Samford's basketball program dates to 1912, and the Birmingham, Alabama school competes in the Southern Conference. The Bulldogs have been a steady conference competitor with occasional postseason appearances.
2535.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname has been associated with Samford since the early 1900s, chosen as a traditional symbol of tenacity and grit.
2535.iconicMoment: Winning the 2024 Southern Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, the program's first trip to March Madness since 2000.
2535.funFact: Samford University was originally named Howard College and is the largest privately funded university in Alabama, located in the Homewood suburb of Birmingham.

2539.blurb: San Francisco's basketball program dates to 1927, and the Dons are one of the most historically significant programs in college basketball. USF won back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1955 and 1956 behind Bill Russell, who went on to become the greatest winner in NBA history.
2539.mascotOrigin: The Dons name is Spanish for "lords" or "gentlemen," reflecting San Francisco's Spanish colonial heritage and the Jesuit university's connection to the city's history.
2539.iconicMoment: Bill Russell leading USF to back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1955 and 1956, including a 55-game winning streak that remains one of the longest in college basketball history.
2539.funFact: USF's 1955-56 teams went on a 55-game winning streak, and Bill Russell revolutionized basketball with his shot-blocking and defensive dominance — he went on to win 11 NBA championships with the Celtics.

2540.blurb: UC Santa Barbara's basketball program dates to 1934, and the Gauchos have been a Big West Conference power. UCSB has made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and produced several NBA players.
2540.mascotOrigin: The Gauchos name was adopted in 1934, inspired by the South American cowboys known for their horsemanship — fitting for the school's coastal California ranching region.
2540.iconicMoment: Brian Shaw's buzzer-beating layup to upset UNLV in the 1990 NCAA Tournament, one of the most iconic upsets of that era.
2540.funFact: UCSB's Thunderdome (now UCSB Events Center) earned its nickname from the raucous student section, and the campus sits directly on the Pacific Ocean in Isla Vista, one of the most beautiful college settings in America.

2541.blurb: Santa Clara University, a Jesuit institution founded in 1851, has played basketball since the early 1900s. The 'Broncos' nickname reflects the school's California ranching heritage. Santa Clara's greatest moment came in 1952 when the Broncos upset heavily favored UCLA to advance to their one and only Final Four appearance. The program has produced NBA standout Steve Nash, a two-time NBA MVP who played for the Broncos from 1992-1996, putting the small West Coast Conference school on the national map.
2541.mascotOrigin: The 'Broncos' nickname reflects Santa Clara's California ranching heritage and the untamed spirit of the wild horse
2541.iconicMoment: Steve Nash's college career from 1992-1996, turning a small Jesuit school into a nationally recognized basketball program
2541.funFact: Two-time NBA MVP Steve Nash chose Santa Clara over other schools and led the Broncos to three consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances in the mid-1990s

2545.blurb: Southeastern Louisiana's basketball program dates to 1946, and the Hammond school competes in the Southland Conference. The Lions have been a consistent Southland competitor and play in the heart of Louisiana.
2545.mascotOrigin: The Lions name was adopted as a symbol of strength and courage, a traditional choice for the school's athletics program.
2545.iconicMoment: Winning the 2005 Southland Conference Tournament to earn the program's first NCAA Tournament appearance in decades.
2545.funFact: Southeastern Louisiana's most famous basketball alum is Bo Outlaw, who went undrafted but played 14 seasons in the NBA, proving that Lions basketball can produce professional talent.

2546.blurb: Southeast Missouri State's basketball program dates to 1906, and the Cape Girardeau school competes in the Ohio Valley Conference. The Redhawks have been a consistent OVC program with occasional conference titles.
2546.mascotOrigin: The Redhawks name was adopted in 2003, replacing "Indians." The red-tailed hawk, native to the Missouri Bootheel region, inspired the new identity.
2546.iconicMoment: Winning the 2000 OVC Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament berth, one of the program's signature achievements.
2546.funFact: Cape Girardeau, home of Southeast Missouri State, was the hometown of Rush Limbaugh and sits on the Mississippi River, giving the school a unique riverfront location.

2547.blurb: Seattle University's basketball program dates to 1901, and the Jesuit school returned to Division I in 2009 after competing at the NAIA and D-II levels. The Redhawks were a national power in the 1950s and 1960s, led by the great Elgin Baylor.
2547.mascotOrigin: The Redhawks name was adopted in 2000, replacing "Chieftains." The red-tailed hawk, commonly seen in the Pacific Northwest, inspired the new mascot.
2547.iconicMoment: Elgin Baylor leading Seattle University to the 1958 NCAA Championship game, where they fell to Kentucky — Baylor's 25 points showcased one of basketball's greatest talents.
2547.funFact: Elgin Baylor, one of the most revolutionary players in basketball history, played at Seattle University and took the small Jesuit school to the 1958 NCAA title game before becoming an NBA legend with the Lakers.

2550.blurb: Seton Hall's basketball program dates to 1903, making it one of the oldest Catholic university basketball programs in the nation. The 'Pirates' nickname was chosen in the 1930s to reflect the school's New Jersey coastal heritage. Seton Hall reached the 1989 NCAA Championship game under P.J. Carlesimo, falling to Michigan in overtime on Rumeal Robinson's free throws — one of the most dramatic title games ever played. As a founding member of the Big East, the Pirates have been a consistent presence in one of basketball's premier conferences.
2550.mascotOrigin: The 'Pirates' nickname was adopted in the 1930s, reflecting New Jersey's coastal heritage and seafaring history
2550.iconicMoment: The 1989 NCAA Championship game against Michigan, a classic that went to overtime with the title decided on free throws in the final seconds
2550.funFact: Seton Hall was the first Big East team from New Jersey to reach the NCAA Championship game, and the 1989 title game remains one of the most dramatic finals in tournament history

2561.blurb: Siena's basketball program dates to 1941, and the Loudonville, New York school competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference. The Saints have been one of the MAAC's most successful programs, winning multiple conference titles.
2561.mascotOrigin: The Saints name reflects Siena College's Catholic Franciscan identity — the school is named after the Italian city of Siena, home to St. Catherine and St. Bernardine.
2561.iconicMoment: Winning three consecutive MAAC Tournament titles from 2008-2010 and reaching the NCAA Tournament each year, establishing Siena as a MAAC dynasty.
2561.funFact: Siena won three straight MAAC Tournament championships from 2008-2010, a rare three-peat that made them the dominant force in the conference during that era.

2565.blurb: SIU Edwardsville's basketball program dates to 1967, and the school made the transition from Division II to Division I in 2008. The Cougars compete in the Ohio Valley Conference and have been building their D-I program steadily.
2565.mascotOrigin: The Cougars name was adopted in the 1960s, chosen for the cougar's speed, agility, and predatory instincts as a symbol for the athletics program.
2565.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to Division I and becoming eligible for OVC postseason play, marking a new era for the Edwardsville campus.
2565.funFact: SIUE's campus is located across the Mississippi River from St. Louis, giving it access to a major metropolitan area's recruiting base while maintaining a smaller-school identity.

2567.blurb: SMU's basketball program began in 1917 at the Dallas-based university founded in 1911. The 'Mustangs' name was adopted in 1917, inspired by the wild horses of the American Southwest. SMU was a founding member of the Southwest Conference and won multiple SWC titles before the conference dissolved. The program has experienced a resurgence in recent years, joining the ACC in 2024 after successful runs in the American Athletic Conference. Moody Coliseum has been the Mustangs' home court since 1956.
2567.mascotOrigin: The 'Mustangs' name was adopted in 1917, inspired by the wild horses native to the American Southwest and Texas plains
2567.iconicMoment: The 2015 season when SMU went 27-7 and earned a No. 6 seed in the NCAA Tournament under Larry Brown, marking the program's return to national relevance
2567.funFact: SMU's most famous basketball alum is Jim Krebs, who played on the Lakers alongside Jerry West and Elgin Baylor in the early 1960s

2569.blurb: South Carolina State's basketball program dates to 1926, and the Orangeburg school is a proud HBCU competing in the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference. The Bulldogs have won multiple MEAC titles and made several NCAA Tournament appearances.
2569.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted as a traditional symbol of toughness and tenacity for the historically Black university.
2569.iconicMoment: Winning the 2018 MEAC Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, continuing the proud tradition of HBCU basketball in March Madness.
2569.funFact: South Carolina State has produced several professional athletes and is one of the most prominent HBCUs in the Southeast, located in Orangeburg, South Carolina.

2571.blurb: South Dakota State's basketball program dates to 1907, and the Jackrabbits have become a Summit League powerhouse. SDSU made the transition from Division II to Division I in 2004 and quickly became one of the conference's elite programs.
2571.mascotOrigin: The Jackrabbits nickname was adopted in 1907 after a newspaper reporter wrote that the team was "quick as jackrabbits" — the white-tailed jackrabbit is native to the South Dakota prairie.
2571.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2023 NCAA Tournament and earning their first tournament victory, defeating in-state rival South Dakota in the Summit League final to get there.
2571.funFact: The Jackrabbits have dominated the Summit League since joining Division I, and their rivalry with North Dakota State is one of the fiercest in all of mid-major basketball.

2572.blurb: Southern Miss's basketball program dates to 1912, and the Hattiesburg school competes in the Sun Belt Conference. The Golden Eagles have had competitive stretches and produced NBA talent over the decades.
2572.mascotOrigin: The Golden Eagles name was adopted in 1972, replacing "Southerners." The golden eagle, a powerful bird of prey, represents strength and pride.
2572.iconicMoment: Making the 1990 NCAA Tournament and earning a first-round upset, one of the program's signature postseason moments.
2572.funFact: Southern Miss produced NBA player Clarence Weatherspoon, who played 13 seasons in the league and remains the school's most prominent basketball alumnus.

2579.blurb: South Carolina's basketball program dates to 1908, and the 'Gamecocks' nickname — one of the most unique in college sports — was adopted in honor of Revolutionary War hero Thomas Sumter, known as the 'Fighting Gamecock' for his aggressive tactics. The program reached its peak under Frank McGuire, who led the Gamecocks to their only Final Four appearance in 1970. South Carolina joined the SEC in 1991 and has been building its basketball tradition in one of football's most dominant conferences.
2579.mascotOrigin: Named in honor of Revolutionary War general Thomas Sumter, called the 'Fighting Gamecock' by the British for his fierce combat style
2579.iconicMoment: The 2017 NCAA Tournament run to the Final Four, the program's first since 1970 and only the second in school history
2579.funFact: South Carolina is one of only two SEC schools with a Gamecock mascot — the live rooster mascot Sir Big Spur attends football games wearing garnet accessories

2582.blurb: Southern University's basketball program dates to 1928, and the Baton Rouge HBCU is one of the most successful programs in the Southwestern Athletic Conference. The Jaguars have won numerous SWAC titles and made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
2582.mascotOrigin: The Jaguars name represents the speed, power, and ferocity of the jaguar, chosen to embody the competitive spirit of the university's athletics.
2582.iconicMoment: Multiple SWAC Tournament championships and NCAA Tournament appearances, establishing Southern as the premier basketball program in the SWAC alongside Texas Southern.
2582.funFact: Southern University's "Human Jukebox" marching band is one of the most famous in the country, and their presence at basketball games creates one of the most electric atmospheres in college hoops.

2598.blurb: Saint Francis's basketball program dates to 1948, and the Loretto, Pennsylvania school competes in the Northeast Conference. The Red Flash have been a steady NEC competitor with occasional conference titles.
2598.mascotOrigin: The Red Flash name originated in the 1920s when the school's red-clad athletes were said to move like a "red flash" across the field. The unique name has stuck ever since.
2598.iconicMoment: Winning the 2024 NEC Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's most successful recent seasons.
2598.funFact: Saint Francis University in Loretto, Pennsylvania was founded in 1847 and is the oldest Franciscan institution of higher learning in the Americas.

2599.blurb: St. John's basketball was founded in 1907 and the 1910-11 team went undefeated at 14-0, earning a retroactive national championship. Originally known as the 'Redmen' for their all-red uniforms, the school changed to 'Red Storm' in 1994. Under Lou Carnesecca, St. John's became a Big East powerhouse in the 1980s, reaching the Final Four in 1985 behind All-American Chris Mullin. The program has played at Madison Square Garden every year since 1931 — the longest continuous relationship between a team and a venue in college basketball.
2599.mascotOrigin: Originally called 'Redmen' after their all-red athletic uniforms in the 1920s; changed to 'Red Storm' in 1994 to move away from perceived Native American references
2599.iconicMoment: The 1985 Big East Championship 'Sweater Game' between Lou Carnesecca and Georgetown's John Thompson, featuring Chris Mullin vs. Patrick Ewing
2599.funFact: St. John's has played at least one game at Madison Square Garden every year since 1931, the longest continuous relationship between a team and venue in college basketball history

2603.blurb: Saint Joseph's basketball program dates to 1909, and the Philadelphia school is one of the city's "Big Five" programs. The Hawks reached legendary status with the 2003-04 team that started 27-0 and was ranked No. 1, led by Jameer Nelson.
2603.mascotOrigin: The Hawks nickname was adopted in 1929, chosen as a symbol of speed, sharp vision, and predatory skill — the hawk also reflects the soaring Jesuit ideals of the university.
2603.iconicMoment: The 2003-04 season, when Jameer Nelson led the Hawks to a 27-0 start and a No. 1 ranking, captivating the nation before a late-season loss to Xavier.
2603.funFact: The famous "Hawk" mascot at Saint Joseph's must flap its wings continuously throughout every basketball game without stopping — a tradition that has continued since 1956.

2608.blurb: Saint Mary's College, a small Catholic school founded in 1863 in Moraga, California, has been playing basketball since the early 1900s. The 'Gaels' name was bestowed in 1926 by a San Francisco sportswriter, referencing the Irish warrior tradition fitting for a school founded by the Irish Catholic Brothers. Under Randy Bennett, who inherited a 2-27 team in 2001, the Gaels have become one of America's premier mid-major programs and a perennial WCC contender. Saint Mary's has a tradition of recruiting Australian players, having at least one on the roster every season since Bennett's arrival.
2608.mascotOrigin: Sportswriter Pat Frayne christened the teams 'The Gaels' in 1926, referencing Irish warriors — fitting for a school founded by Irish Catholic Christian Brothers
2608.iconicMoment: The 2010 NCAA Tournament run, where Omar Samhan led Saint Mary's past No. 2 seed Villanova to reach the Sweet Sixteen for the first time
2608.funFact: Two-time Oscar-winning actor Mahershala Ali played basketball at Saint Mary's under his original name, Mahershalalhashbaz Gilmore

2609.blurb: CCNY's basketball program dates to 1906 at the City College of New York in Manhattan. The Beavers achieved one of the most remarkable feats in college basketball history in 1950, winning both the NCAA Tournament and the NIT in the same season — the only team to ever do so. The program later dropped to Division III.
2609.mascotOrigin: The Beavers nickname was chosen because the beaver represents industriousness and hard work, values central to the working-class students of the public City College.
2609.iconicMoment: Winning both the 1950 NCAA Tournament and NIT Championship in the same season, a feat that will never be repeated since teams are no longer allowed to enter both tournaments.
2609.funFact: The 1950 championship was followed by a devastating point-shaving scandal in 1951 that led to criminal convictions for several players and effectively ended CCNY's status as a major basketball power.

2612.blurb: Saint Peter's basketball program dates to 1930, and the Jersey City school competes in the Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference. The Peacocks captured America's heart with a stunning 2022 NCAA Tournament Cinderella run.
2612.mascotOrigin: The Peacocks name was adopted because peacocks roamed the campus grounds of the Jersey City school in its early years, and the colorful bird became the school's symbol.
2612.iconicMoment: The 2022 NCAA Tournament run as a 15-seed, upsetting Kentucky, Murray State, and Purdue to reach the Elite Eight — one of the greatest Cinderella stories in tournament history.
2612.funFact: Saint Peter's 2022 Elite Eight run made them the first 15-seed to ever reach the Elite Eight in NCAA Tournament history, and coach Shaheen Holloway became a national sensation overnight.

2617.blurb: Stephen F. Austin's basketball program dates to 1923, and the Nacogdoches, Texas school has been a Southland and WAC Conference power. The Lumberjacks have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and are known for pulling off major upsets.
2617.mascotOrigin: The Lumberjacks name reflects the deep East Texas piney woods region where the university is located — Nacogdoches is surrounded by dense forests that fueled a historic lumber industry.
2617.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 3 seed West Virginia in the 2016 NCAA Tournament as a 14-seed, Thomas Walkup's dominant performance leading one of the biggest upsets of that year's tournament.
2617.funFact: Stephen F. Austin upset No. 1 Duke at Cameron Indoor Stadium in November 2019, one of the most shocking regular-season results in recent college basketball history.

2619.blurb: Stony Brook's basketball program dates to 1960, and the Long Island school moved to Division I in 1999. Competing in the America East Conference and now the Colonial Athletic Association, the Seawolves have built a growing D-I program.
2619.mascotOrigin: The Seawolves name was adopted in 1994, replacing "Patriots," referencing the seafaring wolves of maritime legend and the school's Long Island coastal location.
2619.iconicMoment: Winning the 2016 America East Tournament to earn the program's first-ever NCAA Tournament appearance.
2619.funFact: Stony Brook is part of the SUNY system and is known as a top research university, and its basketball arena sits on one of the largest college campuses on Long Island.

2623.blurb: Missouri State in Springfield competes in the Missouri Valley Conference and has produced competitive basketball teams over the decades. The Bears have made NCAA Tournament appearances and benefit from playing in one of the nation's strongest mid-major conferences.
2623.mascotOrigin: The Bears mascot was adopted to represent the black bears historically found in the Ozark Mountains of southern Missouri, near the Springfield campus.
2623.iconicMoment: The 1999 Sweet Sixteen run as a #10 seed, when the Bears (then Southwest Missouri State) upset Wisconsin and Tennessee before falling to Duke.
2623.funFact: The university was known as Southwest Missouri State until 2005, when it officially became Missouri State University, better reflecting its role as a statewide institution.

2627.blurb: Tarleton State's basketball program dates to 1917, and the Stephenville, Texas school joined Division I in 2020. The Texans compete in the WAC and are one of the newest D-I programs in the country.
2627.mascotOrigin: The Texans name celebrates the proud Texan identity, representing the university's deep roots in the Lone Star State since its founding in 1899.
2627.iconicMoment: Making the transition to Division I in 2020 as a member of the WAC, opening a new era for the Stephenville-based university.
2627.funFact: Tarleton State is a member of the Texas A&M University System and is located in Stephenville, the "Cowboy Capital of the World," home to some of the biggest rodeos in the state.

2628.blurb: TCU has played basketball since 1909, and the 'Horned Frogs' nickname — which actually refers to the Texas horned lizard — predates the university's current name, having been adopted in 1897. The school won ten Southwest Conference titles in its history and has been a member of the Big 12 since 2012. In 2022, TCU won its first NCAA Tournament game since 1987, signaling a resurgence for the program. SuperFrog, the costumed mascot since 1979, was named one of Sports Illustrated's Top 25 College Mascots.
2628.mascotOrigin: Named for the Texas horned lizard (commonly called a 'horned frog') in 1897, a year before the school was even named TCU; legend says the campus was covered with the creatures
2628.iconicMoment: The 2022 NCAA Tournament victory over Seton Hall, the program's first tournament win in 35 years, signaling TCU basketball's return to relevance
2628.funFact: The Texas horned lizard can shoot blood up to five feet from its eyes as a defense mechanism — fitting for one of college sports' most unique mascots

2630.blurb: UT Martin's basketball program dates to 1928, and the school competes in the Ohio Valley Conference from the small town of Martin in rural west Tennessee. The Skyhawks have been a competitive OVC program with occasional tournament appearances.
2630.mascotOrigin: The Skyhawks name was adopted in 1995, replacing "Pacers," chosen to represent the freedom and power of a hawk soaring over the flat farmlands of west Tennessee.
2630.iconicMoment: Lester Hudson's quadruple-double on November 13, 2007 — 25 points, 12 rebounds, 10 assists, and 10 steals — only the fourth quadruple-double in NCAA Division I history.
2630.funFact: UT Martin's Lester Hudson recorded one of only a handful of quadruple-doubles in NCAA Division I history, an almost impossibly rare statistical achievement.

2633.blurb: Tennessee's basketball program began in 1909, and the 'Volunteers' nickname comes from Tennessee's designation as 'The Volunteer State,' earned when overwhelming numbers of soldiers volunteered for the War of 1812 and the Mexican-American War. The mascot Smokey, a Bluetick Coonhound named for the Great Smoky Mountains, was selected in 1953 when a dog named Blue Smokey barked and howled at a halftime selection contest — prompting roaring student approval. Tennessee has been a consistent SEC contender with multiple Sweet Sixteen appearances and a tradition of passionate orange-clad fans.
2633.mascotOrigin: The 'Volunteers' nickname comes from Tennessee's role as 'The Volunteer State'; mascot Smokey is a Bluetick Coonhound named for the Great Smoky Mountains
2633.iconicMoment: The 2008 Sweet Sixteen run under Bruce Pearl, which reinvigorated Tennessee basketball and made Thompson-Boling Arena one of the toughest venues in the SEC
2633.funFact: Smokey II was dognapped by Kentucky students in 1955, and Smokey VIII had to have emergency veterinary care before the 1998 football championship game after eating a hotel washcloth

2634.blurb: Tennessee State's basketball program dates to 1912, and the Nashville HBCU has a proud basketball tradition. The Tigers won three consecutive NAIA National Championships from 1957-1959 under the legendary coach Harold Hunter and later John McLendon.
2634.mascotOrigin: The Tigers name was adopted as a symbol of ferocity and pride for the historically Black university in Nashville.
2634.iconicMoment: The three consecutive NAIA National Championships from 1957-1959 under coaching great Harold Hunter, establishing Tennessee State as a basketball dynasty.
2634.funFact: Tennessee State's Dick Barnett went on to win two NBA championships with the New York Knicks, and the school's NAIA dynasty of the late 1950s is one of the great runs in college basketball history.

2635.blurb: Tennessee Tech's basketball program dates to 1921, and the Cookeville school competes in the Ohio Valley Conference. The Golden Eagles have been a consistent OVC competitor with several conference titles and NCAA Tournament appearances.
2635.mascotOrigin: The Golden Eagles name represents the golden eagle, a powerful bird of prey, symbolizing strength and excellence for the technological university.
2635.iconicMoment: Making the 2009 NCAA Tournament and competing as an OVC champion, one of the program's signature postseason achievements.
2635.funFact: Tennessee Tech's Hooper Eblen Center is named after the legendary coach who built the Golden Eagles into an OVC power, and Cookeville sits on the Cumberland Plateau between Nashville and Knoxville.

2636.blurb: UTSA's basketball program dates to 1981, and the San Antonio school competes in the American Athletic Conference. The Roadrunners have been building their program as one of the fastest-growing universities in Texas.
2636.mascotOrigin: The Roadrunners name was chosen for the greater roadrunner bird native to the South Texas brush country, known for its speed and cunning.
2636.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1999 NCAA Tournament as a Southland Conference champion, the program's most notable postseason appearance.
2636.funFact: UTSA is one of the fastest-growing universities in the country by enrollment and is the largest university in San Antonio, a city that lives and breathes basketball thanks to the Spurs.

2638.blurb: UTEP's basketball program dates to 1914, and the El Paso school holds one of the most important places in basketball history. Then known as Texas Western, the Miners won the 1966 NCAA Championship with an all-Black starting five, defeating Adolph Rupp's all-white Kentucky team in a game that changed college basketball forever.
2638.mascotOrigin: The Miners name reflects El Paso's mining heritage and the school's original name, Texas School of Mines and Metallurgy.
2638.iconicMoment: Winning the 1966 NCAA Championship with an all-Black starting five under coach Don Haskins, defeating Kentucky in what is considered the most socially significant game in basketball history.
2638.funFact: The 1966 championship game was later immortalized in the movie "Glory Road" — Don Haskins simply played his best players, but the game broke a major racial barrier in college basketball.

2640.blurb: Texas Southern's basketball program dates to 1947, and the Houston HBCU has been one of the most successful programs in the Southwestern Athletic Conference. The Tigers have won numerous SWAC titles and made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
2640.mascotOrigin: The Tigers nickname was adopted as a symbol of power and pride for the historically Black university in Houston's Third Ward.
2640.iconicMoment: Winning the 2022 First Four game against Texas A&M-Corpus Christi to advance to the NCAA Tournament proper, continuing the program's tradition of March appearances.
2640.funFact: Texas Southern has one of the most active NCAA Tournament résumés among HBCUs, earning bids regularly and representing the SWAC in March Madness year after year.

2641.blurb: Texas Tech's basketball program began in 1925, and the 'Red Raiders' name was coined by a sports editor in 1936, inspired by the team's all-red uniforms and demanding schedule. The school has two mascots: the Masked Rider, a horseback tradition since 1936, and Raider Red, a costumed cowboy created in 1971 when opposing schools banned live horses. Texas Tech reached the 2019 NCAA Championship game in a thrilling overtime loss to Virginia, marking the program's greatest run. The 'Guns Up' hand sign, introduced in the 1970s, has become one of college sports' most recognizable gestures.
2641.mascotOrigin: Sports editor Collier Parrish coined 'Red Raiders' in 1936 for the team's all-red uniforms; the Masked Rider tradition began that same year with 'ghost riders' circling the field
2641.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2019 NCAA Championship game under Chris Beard, the program's first-ever title game appearance, falling to Virginia in overtime
2641.funFact: The first Raider Red mascot head was built from chicken wire and papier-mache and was so lightweight it could have doubled as a pinata — a new head was built every season until the 1990s

2643.blurb: The Citadel's basketball program dates to 1901, and the military college in Charleston, South Carolina competes in the Southern Conference. The Bulldogs face unique challenges as a military school but maintain a competitive basketball program.
2643.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted in 1917, chosen for the bulldog's tenacity and fighting spirit — fitting for a military institution.
2643.iconicMoment: Winning the 1960 Southern Conference Tournament, one of the most successful moments in the program's long history.
2643.funFact: As a senior military college, every Citadel student is a member of the Corps of Cadets, making the basketball team one of the few in Division I where every player follows a military lifestyle.

2649.blurb: Toledo's basketball program dates to 1914, and the Rockets have been one of the most successful programs in the Mid-American Conference. Toledo has won multiple MAC titles and made several NCAA Tournament appearances.
2649.mascotOrigin: The Rockets name was adopted in 1923 after a sportswriter described the football team as "starting off like rockets." The space-age name has endured for over a century.
2649.iconicMoment: The 1979 season under coach Bob Nichols, when the Rockets won the MAC and reached the NCAA Tournament, part of a stretch of consistent MAC success.
2649.funFact: Toledo's Savage Arena is one of the MAC's most raucous venues, and the Rockets have produced NBA players including Steve Mix and Larry Jones.

2653.blurb: Troy's basketball program dates to 1930, and the southeastern Alabama school competes in the Sun Belt Conference. The Trojans have been a growing presence in the Sun Belt with increasing competitiveness.
2653.mascotOrigin: The Trojans name was adopted in the 1930s, referencing the warriors of ancient Troy — a natural choice given the city's name of Troy, Alabama.
2653.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2003 NCAA Tournament, the program's breakthrough moment in postseason play.
2653.funFact: Troy, Alabama was a key site in the Civil Rights Movement — Rosa Parks's famous bus boycott was influenced by events in the Troy-Montgomery region.

2655.blurb: Tulane's basketball program dates to 1907, and the New Orleans school competes in the American Athletic Conference. The Green Wave have had competitive stretches and benefit from the vibrant culture and recruiting appeal of New Orleans.
2655.mascotOrigin: The "Green Wave" name originated in 1920 when a song about the school's "rolling green wave" was written, replacing the "Olive and Blue" identity. The Riptide pelican mascot was added later.
2655.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1995 NIT Championship game, one of the program's deepest postseason runs in the modern era.
2655.funFact: Tulane's campus is located on the iconic St. Charles Avenue streetcar line in Uptown New Orleans, and the school's basketball games are infused with the city's unmatched energy and culture.

2670.blurb: VCU's basketball program was founded in 1968 after the merger of the Richmond Professional Institute and the Medical College of Virginia. The 'Rams' name was adopted in 1970, influenced by Richmond's colonial-era sheep farming history. VCU pulled off one of the most incredible tournament runs ever in 2011, becoming the first team to advance from the First Four to the Final Four under Shaka Smart's 'Havoc' defense. The Rams upset Georgetown, Purdue, Florida State, and top-seeded Kansas in consecutive games.
2670.mascotOrigin: Originally named the 'Panthers' in 1968, VCU changed to 'Rams' in 1970, influenced by Richmond's colonial-era sheep farming history
2670.iconicMoment: The 2011 First Four-to-Final Four run under Shaka Smart, the first team ever to accomplish this feat, defeating five opponents including top-seeded Kansas
2670.funFact: VCU's 2011 Final Four run was the first time a team advanced from the First Four play-in games all the way to the Final Four — coach Shaka Smart was named after the Zulu monarch Shaka kaSenzangakhona

2674.blurb: Valparaiso's basketball program dates to 1917, and the northwest Indiana school competes in the Missouri Valley Conference. The Beacons (formerly Crusaders) achieved national fame with a stunning 1998 NCAA Tournament run.
2674.mascotOrigin: The Beacons name was adopted in 2021, replacing "Crusaders," to represent a guiding light — fitting for the university's Lutheran heritage and mission.
2674.iconicMoment: Bryce Drew's iconic buzzer-beating three-pointer against Ole Miss in the 1998 NCAA Tournament, drawn up by his father and coach Homer Drew — one of the most famous shots in tournament history.
2674.funFact: The play that produced Bryce Drew's 1998 buzzer-beater was designed by his father, coach Homer Drew, and is simply known as "The Shot" — it remains one of March Madness's most replayed moments.

2678.blurb: VMI's basketball program dates to 1908, and the Lexington, Virginia military school competes in the Southern Conference. The Keydets face unique challenges as a military institute but have had occasional competitive seasons.
2678.mascotOrigin: The "Keydets" name is a phonetic spelling of "cadets," reflecting VMI's status as a senior military college where all students are cadets in the Corps of Cadets.
2678.iconicMoment: The 1976 season, when VMI's high-scoring offense averaged over 90 points per game and earned an NCAA Tournament bid behind a run-and-gun style.
2678.funFact: VMI's 1976 team averaged over 90 points per game, making them one of the highest-scoring teams in the country that year — a remarkable feat for a military school with rigorous cadet obligations.

2681.blurb: Wagner's basketball program dates to 1928, and the Staten Island, New York school competes in the Northeast Conference. The Seahawks have won multiple NEC titles and made NCAA Tournament appearances.
2681.mascotOrigin: The Seahawks name reflects Wagner's location on Grymes Hill in Staten Island, overlooking New York Harbor and the Atlantic Ocean.
2681.iconicMoment: Winning the 2003 NEC Tournament and earning an NCAA Tournament bid, one of the program's most successful seasons.
2681.funFact: Wagner College sits on Grymes Hill on Staten Island, offering stunning views of the Statue of Liberty and the Manhattan skyline — arguably the most scenic campus overlook of any NYC-area basketball program.

2692.blurb: Weber State's basketball program dates to 1962, and the Ogden, Utah school has been one of the Big Sky Conference's most successful programs. The Wildcats have made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances and produced NBA talent.
2692.mascotOrigin: The Wildcats name was adopted in the 1960s, chosen as a traditional symbol of athletic ferocity and competitiveness.
2692.iconicMoment: Damian Lillard's dominant seasons at Weber State (2008-12), where he became one of the most prolific scorers in college basketball before being drafted sixth overall by Portland.
2692.funFact: Damian Lillard, an NBA All-Star and one of the greatest players in Portland Trail Blazers history, played at Weber State and put the program on the national map.

2698.blurb: West Georgia's basketball program dates to 1958, and the Carrollton school is one of the newest Division I programs, having transitioned from Division II in 2023. The Wolves compete in the ASUN Conference.
2698.mascotOrigin: The Wolves nickname was adopted to represent the strength and pack mentality associated with wolves — fitting for a competitive university athletics program.
2698.iconicMoment: Making the transition to Division I in 2023 and joining the ASUN Conference, beginning a new chapter in the program's history.
2698.funFact: West Georgia is one of the most recent programs to make the jump to Division I, joining the ASUN Conference and bringing small-town Georgia basketball to the national stage.

2710.blurb: Western Illinois's basketball program dates to 1906, and the Macomb school competes in the Summit League. The Leathernecks have been a steady mid-major program with occasional conference success.
2710.mascotOrigin: The Leathernecks name honors the U.S. Marines, who are famously known as "Leathernecks" — the name was adopted to represent toughness and military discipline.
2710.iconicMoment: Making the 2004 Summit League tournament and competing for an NCAA Tournament berth, one of the program's most competitive recent eras.
2710.funFact: Western Illinois is one of the few college programs named after a branch of the military — the Leathernecks nickname is a direct tribute to the U.S. Marines.

2711.blurb: Western Michigan's basketball program dates to 1913, and the Kalamazoo school competes in the Mid-American Conference. The Broncos have been a steady MAC competitor with occasional conference titles and postseason appearances.
2711.mascotOrigin: The Broncos name was adopted in the 1930s, representing the wild, unbroken bronco horse — a symbol of untamed spirit and competitive energy.
2711.iconicMoment: Winning the 2014 MAC Tournament under coach Steve Hawkins, earning an NCAA Tournament berth and revitalizing fan interest in the program.
2711.funFact: Western Michigan's University Arena in Kalamazoo is affectionately known as "The House That Eldon Built," named after legendary coach Eldon Miller who elevated the program.

2717.blurb: Western Carolina's basketball program dates to 1930, and the Cullowhee, North Carolina school competes in the Southern Conference. The Catamounts play in the heart of the Great Smoky Mountains.
2717.mascotOrigin: The Catamounts name refers to the eastern cougar (catamount), which historically roamed the mountains of western North Carolina.
2717.iconicMoment: Winning the 1996 Southern Conference Tournament to earn an NCAA Tournament bid, the program's most celebrated postseason achievement.
2717.funFact: Western Carolina's campus in Cullowhee sits in a valley in the Great Smoky Mountains at over 2,000 feet elevation, making it one of the most secluded and scenic Division I settings in the country.

2724.blurb: Wichita State's basketball program dates to 1905, and the Shockers have been one of the most successful mid-major programs in college basketball history. Under coach Gregg Marshall, Wichita State went undefeated in the regular season in 2013-14 and reached the 2013 Final Four.
2724.mascotOrigin: The "Shockers" name dates to 1904 when student-athletes worked in wheat fields "shocking" (bundling) wheat to earn money — WuShock, the wheat-headed mascot, embodies this heritage.
2724.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2013 Final Four under Gregg Marshall, upsetting Gonzaga and Ohio State en route and establishing Wichita State as one of the elite programs outside the power conferences.
2724.funFact: Wichita State's 2013-14 team went 34-0 before losing in the NCAA Tournament, finishing with the best regular-season record in the country and proving mid-majors could go wire-to-wire undefeated.

2729.blurb: William & Mary's basketball program dates to 1905, and the Williamsburg, Virginia school is the second-oldest college in America (founded 1693). Competing in the Colonial Athletic Association, the Tribe have been a steady conference competitor.
2729.mascotOrigin: The Tribe nickname has been used since 1926. As one of the oldest colleges in America, the name reflects the school's colonial-era heritage in Williamsburg.
2729.iconicMoment: The 2014-15 season when the Tribe won 20 games and contended for a CAA title, one of the strongest seasons in modern program history.
2729.funFact: William & Mary was founded in 1693, making it the second-oldest institution of higher education in the United States — Thomas Jefferson, James Monroe, and John Tyler all attended.

2737.blurb: Winthrop's basketball program dates to 1977, and the Rock Hill, South Carolina school has been one of the Big South Conference's dominant programs. The Eagles have won numerous Big South titles and made multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
2737.mascotOrigin: The Eagles name was adopted to represent strength, vision, and the soaring aspirations of the university's athletics program.
2737.iconicMoment: The program's remarkable run of Big South titles, including nine regular-season championships in ten years from 1999-2008 under coaches Gregg Marshall and Randy Peele.
2737.funFact: Gregg Marshall began building his coaching reputation at Winthrop before moving to Wichita State — he went 194-84 in nine seasons with the Eagles and won seven conference titles.

2739.blurb: Green Bay's basketball program began in 1969 at the University of Wisconsin-Green Bay. The Phoenix compete in the Horizon League and have won multiple conference championships. The program has been a consistent Horizon League contender.
2739.mascotOrigin: The Phoenix nickname was adopted in 1993, replacing 'Pioneers,' symbolizing rebirth and rising — a metaphor for the growing university in northeastern Wisconsin.
2739.iconicMoment: Making the 1994 NCAA Tournament and nearly upsetting Jason Kidd's California Golden Bears in the first round, one of the most exciting games in program history.
2739.funFact: Green Bay's Kress Events Center is located in the football-obsessed city of Green Bay, Wisconsin, meaning the basketball team competes for attention with the NFL's Packers — one of the most unique competitive dynamics in college sports.

2747.blurb: Wofford's basketball program dates to the late 1800s, and the Spartanburg, South Carolina school competes in the Southern Conference. The Terriers have had impressive recent success, including NCAA Tournament wins.
2747.mascotOrigin: The Terriers name was adopted because the terrier dog breed is known for being feisty, tenacious, and punching above its weight — a perfect metaphor for the small liberal arts college.
2747.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 7 seed Seton Hall 84-68 in the 2019 NCAA Tournament as a 10-seed, behind Fletcher Magee's sharpshooting and a 30-4 record.
2747.funFact: Fletcher Magee became the NCAA's all-time leader in career three-pointers made while at Wofford, cementing the Terriers as one of the best shooting teams in college basketball during his era.

2750.blurb: Wright State's basketball program dates to 1970, and the Dayton, Ohio school competes in the Horizon League. Named after the Wright Brothers, the Raiders have been a competitive Horizon League program with multiple NCAA Tournament appearances.
2750.mascotOrigin: The Raiders name was chosen by students, representing a bold and aggressive competitor — fitting for a university named after aviation pioneers Orville and Wilbur Wright.
2750.iconicMoment: The 1993 NCAA Tournament appearance, the program's first, establishing Wright State as a legitimate Division I contender in the Horizon League.
2750.funFact: Wright State is named after the Wright Brothers, who were from Dayton, and the university sits near Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, one of the most important military aviation facilities in the world.

2751.blurb: Wyoming's basketball program dates to 1905, and the Laramie school competes in the Mountain West Conference. The Cowboys won the NCAA Championship in 1943 and have had competitive stretches throughout their history.
2751.mascotOrigin: The Cowboys name reflects Wyoming's deep ranching and rodeo culture — the cowboy is the state's most iconic figure, appearing on the Wyoming license plate and state symbols.
2751.iconicMoment: Winning the 1943 NCAA Championship under coach Ev Shelton, defeating Georgetown in the final — the program's only national title.
2751.funFact: Wyoming's Arena-Auditorium sits at 7,220 feet elevation in Laramie, making it one of the highest-altitude home courts in Division I basketball — visiting teams often struggle with the thin air.

2752.blurb: Xavier's basketball program dates to 1920, and the Cincinnati school has become one of the most successful mid-major programs in the country. The Musketeers have made 28 NCAA Tournament appearances and reached the Elite Eight multiple times.
2752.mascotOrigin: The Musketeers name was adopted in 1925, inspired by the Alexandre Dumas novel "The Three Musketeers" — fitting for Xavier, a Jesuit school named after St. Francis Xavier.
2752.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2017 Elite Eight under coach Chris Mack, capping a stretch of consistent NCAA Tournament success that included 10 appearances in 12 years.
2752.funFact: Xavier's rivalry with crosstown Cincinnati is known as the "Crosstown Shootout" and is one of the most heated rivalries in college basketball — the schools are just four miles apart.

2754.blurb: Youngstown State's basketball program dates to 1927, and the Ohio school competes in the Horizon League. The Penguins have been a steady conference competitor, representing the blue-collar city of Youngstown.
2754.mascotOrigin: The Penguins nickname was adopted in 1933 after a player was described as "waddling like a penguin" due to the cold weather gear he wore — the unusual name stuck and became the school's identity.
2754.iconicMoment: Competing in the Horizon League and making occasional postseason appearances, building the program's Division I identity in the steel city.
2754.funFact: Youngstown State may be the only Division I school with a penguin mascot — the name originated as a joke about cold Ohio winters but has become one of the most unique and beloved mascots in the sport.

2755.blurb: Grambling's basketball program began in 1928 at the HBCU in Grambling, Louisiana. The Tigers compete in the SWAC and carry the legendary athletic tradition built by football coach Eddie Robinson, which extends to all sports at the university. Grambling has won multiple SWAC basketball titles.
2755.mascotOrigin: The Tigers nickname represents power, ferocity, and pride — fitting for a university that has become one of the most recognizable HBCU athletic brands in America.
2755.iconicMoment: Willis Reed's dominant play at Grambling in the early 1960s before becoming an NBA legend and captain of the New York Knicks' championship teams.
2755.funFact: Willis Reed, the iconic New York Knick whose Game 7 entrance in the 1970 NBA Finals is one of sport's most legendary moments, played his college basketball at Grambling.

2771.blurb: Merrimack College in North Andover, Massachusetts, transitioned to Division I in 2019-20 and competes in the Northeast Conference. The Warriors are building their Division I identity after a successful Division II tenure that included a national championship.
2771.mascotOrigin: The Warriors mascot represents fighting spirit and determination, chosen to embody the competitive nature of the Augustinian Catholic institution.
2771.iconicMoment: Winning the 2023 NEC regular season title during their transition period, though the Warriors were ineligible for the NCAA Tournament due to the four-year transitional rules.
2771.funFact: Merrimack won the Division II national championship in 2005, and their success at that level helped fuel the decision to move up to Division I.

2803.blurb: Bryant's basketball program began in 1966 in Smithfield, Rhode Island. The Bulldogs transitioned from Division II to Division I in 2008 and joined the America East Conference. The program has steadily built competitiveness at the D-I level.
2803.mascotOrigin: The Bulldogs nickname was adopted to represent determination and tenacity, fitting for a university originally focused on business education.
2803.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to full Division I status and becoming eligible for the NCAA Tournament, a milestone that elevated the entire athletic program.
2803.funFact: Bryant University was originally founded in 1863 as a business school in Providence, making it one of the oldest business institutions in the country before expanding into a full university.

2815.blurb: Lindenwood University in St. Charles, Missouri, began its transition to Division I in 2022-23 and competes in the Ohio Valley Conference. The Lions are one of the newest Division I programs and are building their basketball identity at the highest level.
2815.mascotOrigin: The Lions mascot symbolizes strength and courage, adopted to give the university a powerful athletic identity as it grew its sports programs.
2815.iconicMoment: The official start of Division I competition in 2022-23, marking the beginning of a new era for Lindenwood athletics after decades as an NAIA and Division II program.
2815.funFact: Lindenwood was founded in 1827, making it one of the oldest universities west of the Mississippi River despite being one of the newest Division I athletic programs.

2837.blurb: East Texas A&M's basketball program (formerly Texas A&M-Commerce) dates to 1917 at the university in Commerce, Texas. The Lions were a Division II power for decades before transitioning to Division I. The program won multiple Lone Star Conference championships at the D-II level.
2837.mascotOrigin: The Lions nickname has been associated with the university for decades, representing the pride and strength of the East Texas institution.
2837.iconicMoment: The successful transition to Division I, marking a new chapter for a program with a long and proud history at lower divisions.
2837.funFact: The university's most famous alumnus may be Sam Rayburn, the longest-serving Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives, who attended the school when it was East Texas State.

2856.blurb: California Baptist's basketball program began in 1955 at the Christian university in Riverside, California. The Lancers transitioned from NAIA to Division II and then to Division I, joining the WAC in 2018. CBU has quickly become competitive at the D-I level.
2856.mascotOrigin: The Lancers nickname evokes the image of a mounted knight carrying a lance, symbolizing the Christian warrior spirit the university aims to embody.
2856.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to full Division I status and becoming eligible for the NCAA Tournament, marking a new era for the rapidly growing university.
2856.funFact: California Baptist is one of the fastest-growing universities in the country, with enrollment more than tripling since 2000, and the basketball program has benefited from significant investment in new facilities.

2870.blurb: Purdue University Fort Wayne (formerly IPFW) competes in the Horizon League and has been building its basketball program as a regional university in northeast Indiana. The Mastodons have been increasingly competitive in their conference.
2870.mascotOrigin: The Mastodons mascot was inspired by the discovery of mastodon fossils near Fort Wayne, Indiana, giving the university a unique and locally meaningful identity.
2870.iconicMoment: The competitive Horizon League seasons in the 2010s, as the program established itself as a legitimate conference contender after years of building.
2870.funFact: The mastodon fossils discovered near Fort Wayne are displayed at the Indiana State Museum, making Purdue Fort Wayne the only Division I school with a mascot based on a local paleontological discovery.

2885.blurb: The New Jersey Institute of Technology in Newark competes in the America East Conference and transitioned to Division I in 2006. The Highlanders spent years as an independent before joining the ASUN and then America East, building their program along the way.
2885.mascotOrigin: The Highlanders mascot references the New Jersey Highlands region, combined with a Scottish warrior theme that represents strength and determination.
2885.iconicMoment: The 2015 upset of Michigan at Crisler Center, a stunning road victory that put the then-independent NJIT program on the national map.
2885.funFact: NJIT spent seven years as a Division I independent without a conference, one of the longest such stretches in modern college basketball, before joining the ASUN in 2020.

2900.blurb: St. Thomas's basketball program dates to 1920, and the St. Paul school made a historic and controversial jump directly from Division III to Division I in 2021. The Tommies compete in the Summit League and are the only school ever to make this leap.
2900.mascotOrigin: The Tommies nickname is derived from the university's name, St. Thomas — a "Tommie" is a student or athlete of St. Thomas, a simple and enduring identifier.
2900.iconicMoment: Making the unprecedented jump from Division III directly to Division I in 2021, the only school in NCAA history to receive a waiver to skip Division II.
2900.funFact: St. Thomas is the only school in NCAA history to jump directly from Division III to Division I, bypassing Division II entirely — the move was approved after the MIAC forced them out for being too dominant.

2908.blurb: South Carolina Upstate's basketball program dates to 1967, and the Spartanburg school competes in the Big South Conference. The Spartans moved to Division I in 2003 and have been building their program since.
2908.mascotOrigin: The Spartans nickname was chosen to represent the ancient Greek warriors' discipline and competitive spirit, a common but powerful choice for the upstate South Carolina school.
2908.iconicMoment: Earning the program's first Big South Tournament bid and becoming competitive in their relatively new Division I era.
2908.funFact: USC Upstate is located in Spartanburg, which was named after the Spartan Regiment from the American Revolution — the city's name made "Spartans" an obvious choice for the mascot.

2916.blurb: Incarnate Word's basketball program began in 1997 at the Catholic university in San Antonio, Texas. The Cardinals transitioned to Division I in 2013, joining the Southland Conference. The program is still building its D-I identity in one of Texas's most basketball-rich cities.
2916.mascotOrigin: The Cardinals nickname references the cardinal bird, symbolizing vitality and spirit, and also connects to the Catholic tradition — cardinals being senior officials of the Church.
2916.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to full Division I status, marking a significant milestone for the small Catholic university in San Antonio.
2916.funFact: Incarnate Word's campus is located in the Alamo Heights area of San Antonio, just minutes from the historic Alamo and the famous San Antonio River Walk.

2934.blurb: Cal State Bakersfield's basketball program began in 1970 and the Roadrunners were a Division II power before transitioning to Division I in 2006. CSUB joined the WAC and later the Big West Conference. The program has built a loyal following in California's Central Valley.
2934.mascotOrigin: The Roadrunners nickname was chosen to represent the greater roadrunner bird native to the desert and semi-arid regions of California's Central Valley.
2934.iconicMoment: The 1993 and 1997 Division II Final Four appearances, which established CSUB as one of the premier small-school programs before the jump to D-I.
2934.funFact: Bakersfield's Icardo Center is named after a local family, and the city of Bakersfield is better known as the birthplace of the Bakersfield Sound in country music than as a college basketball town.

3084.blurb: Utah Valley's basketball program dates to 1941, and the Orem school moved to Division I in 2009. The Wolverines compete in the WAC and have been building their program in the scenic Utah Valley region.
3084.mascotOrigin: The Wolverines name was adopted in 2003, chosen for the wolverine's fierce and relentless nature — a predator that punches above its size, fitting for a growing D-I program.
3084.iconicMoment: Earning a CBI postseason appearance and becoming competitive in the WAC, establishing UVU as a legitimate Division I program.
3084.funFact: Utah Valley University is one of the largest public universities in Utah by enrollment and sits in the shadow of the Wasatch Mountains in Orem, with stunning views from campus.

3101.blurb: Utah Tech's basketball program dates to 1941, and the St. George school is one of the newest Division I programs, having transitioned in 2020. Formerly known as Dixie State, the Trailblazers compete in the WAC.
3101.mascotOrigin: The Trailblazers name was adopted in 2022 as part of the school's rebranding from Dixie State to Utah Tech, symbolizing pioneering innovation and the spirit of the American West.
3101.iconicMoment: Completing the transition to Division I and the rebranding to Utah Tech in 2022, beginning a new identity for the St. George university.
3101.funFact: Utah Tech is located in St. George, one of the fastest-growing cities in America, near Zion National Park — the school changed its name from Dixie State to Utah Tech in 2022 to better reflect its identity.

112358.blurb: Long Island University merged its Brooklyn and Post campuses into one unified Division I program competing in the Northeast Conference. The LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds had a storied early history, winning two NIT championships in 1939 and 1941 when the NIT was the premier postseason tournament.
112358.mascotOrigin: The Sharks mascot was adopted when LIU unified its two campuses in 2019, chosen to represent Long Island's connection to the Atlantic Ocean.
112358.iconicMoment: Winning the 1939 NIT Championship — at a time when the NIT was considered the most prestigious postseason tournament in college basketball.
112358.funFact: LIU Brooklyn's 1930s and 1940s teams were among the best in the nation, but the program was implicated in the 1951 point-shaving scandal that rocked college basketball.
